title: Multilayer Q-Matrix-Embedded Neural Network for Cognitive Diagnosis (M-QCDNet): Structure-Aware Deep Learning Architecture for Psychometric Interpretability
summary: The research proposes a multilayer Q-matrix-embedded neural network for cognitive diagnosis (M-QCDNet), which integrates the structural interpretability of cognitive diagnostic models (CDMs) with the deep learning neural network (NN). M-QCDNet structures the item-skill relationship using the Q-matrix as a structural prior, ensuring latent mastery profiles remain interpretable and consistent with cognitive theory, followed by the proposed loss function with an L2 penalty to penalize skills not aligned with the Q-matrix and to balance predictive performance and structural alignment. Corresponding evaluation matrices, the interpretable alignment-based metrics that quantify the degree to which predicted skill activations correspond to item-level skills, were further developed. M-QCDNet offers practical benefits for classroom practice, enabling early detection of learning difficulties and supporting mastery-based interventions. By embedding diagnostic validity into model design, M-QCDNet bridges psychometric transparency and neural flexibility, advancing interpretable, fair, and actionable AI for cognitive diagnostics.

AI.tldr: M-QCDNet is a neural network that embeds Q-matrix structure for cognitive diagnosis, combining interpretable psychometric models with deep learning to maintain diagnostic validity while leveraging neural network flexibility.
AI.motivation: The research aims to bridge the gap between traditional cognitive diagnostic models (CDMs) that have structural interpretability and deep learning neural networks that offer powerful predictive capabilities. Current approaches either sacrifice interpretability for performance or lack the flexibility of neural networks.
AI.method: The proposed M-QCDNet uses a multilayer Q-matrix-embedded neural network that structures item-skill relationships using the Q-matrix as a structural prior. It ensures latent mastery profiles remain interpretable and consistent with cognitive theory through a proposed loss function with an L2 penalty to penalize skills not aligned with the Q-matrix, balancing predictive performance and structural alignment.
AI.result: The method maintains interpretable skill mastery profiles while leveraging neural network capabilities. It also introduces interpretable alignment-based evaluation metrics to quantify how well predicted skill activations correspond to item-level skills, providing practical benefits for early detection of learning difficulties and supporting mastery-based interventions.
AI.conclusion: M-QCDNet successfully bridges psychometric transparency with neural flexibility, advancing interpretable, fair, and actionable AI for cognitive diagnostics by embedding diagnostic validity directly into the model design.

title: I\textsuperscript{2}RiMA: Spectral Riemannian Representation with Temporal Attention for Mental Stress Detection based on EEG Signals
summary: Cross-subject EEG stress detection remains challenging because discriminative stress-related patterns are both subject-dependent and frequency-specific. Conventional Riemannian methods model spatial covariance mainly in the time domain, overlooking neural oscillations that are critical for high-level cognitive state decoding, while standard temporal tokenization often fragments inter-slice temporal coherence. To address these limitations, we propose \method{}, an Intra-Inter Riemannian Manifold Attention Network for EEG-based stress detection. \method{} constructs spatial covariance matrices independently at each frequency point and maps them to the SPD tangent space, preserving channel-wise geometry together with frequency-specific discriminative cues. It further introduces frequency cluster aggregation to select informative spectral components and reduce redundancy by forming compact, data-driven frequency clusters aligned with EEG rhythms. Finally, an intra-inter slice attention module adaptively integrates local slice-level spectral dynamics and global temporal context across EEG sequences. Experiments on three datasets show that \method{} consistently outperforms five state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 82.78\% balanced accuracy while remaining efficient with only 1.60M parameters and 31.95M FLOPs.

AI.tldr: A novel Intra-Inter Riemannian Manifold Attention Network (method) improves EEG stress detection by modeling spatial covariance at each frequency point, using frequency cluster aggregation, and intra-inter slice attention for better cross-subject performance.
AI.motivation: Cross-subject EEG stress detection is challenging because stress-related patterns are both subject-dependent and frequency-specific. Conventional Riemannian methods model spatial covariance mainly in the time domain and overlook neural oscillations crucial for cognitive state decoding, while standard temporal tokenization fragments inter-slice temporal coherence.
AI.method: 1) Constructs spatial covariance matrices independently at each frequency point and maps them to the SPD (Symmetric Positive Definite) tangent space to preserve channel-wise geometry with frequency-specific discriminative cues. 2) Introduces frequency cluster aggregation to select informative spectral components and reduce redundancy by forming compact, data-driven frequency clusters aligned with EEG rhythms. 3) Uses an intra-inter slice attention module to adaptively integrate local slice-level spectral dynamics and global temporal context across EEG sequences.
AI.result: Experiments on three datasets show consistent outperformance over five state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 82.78% balanced accuracy while remaining efficient with only 1.60M parameters and 31.95M FLOPs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses limitations of conventional approaches by jointly modeling spatial, spectral, and temporal information for improved cross-subject EEG stress detection, demonstrating superior performance with computational efficiency.

title: Fixed-Set Robustness in Programming by Example: Example Corruption and Semantic Partition Recovery
summary: Programming-by-example systems infer programs from a small set of input-output examples. Robust PBE work usually models wrong examples as samples from a stochastic noise process and then minimizes an expected or empirical loss. This paper studies a different failure mode: an adversary who sees the synthesizer and chooses the examples whose corruption most damages the returned program. We formalize fixed-set worst-case corruption for finite PBE version spaces, implement exact-within-bounded-pool and heuristic corruption searches for a string-transformation DSL, and introduce version-space partition aggregation (VPA), a defense that synthesizes on disjoint example groups and votes by semantic signatures. The central claim is deliberately bounded and partly negative: low-margin PBE tasks have an adversarial robustness dimension that random-typo and noisy-PBE evaluations miss, while semantic partition aggregation helps only when the clean semantics keep a partition vote margin, which often fails on realistic tasks. Evidence from curated/generated DSL tasks, accepted public SyGuS PBE_SLIA slices, SYNTRA Playgol v2, and noisy-PBE objective baselines supports that boundary. One curated edit flips all 8 spike tasks while 200-trial typo, DSL-pool, and distance-matched random controls succeed on 10.3%, 11.0%, and 16.7%; generated margin-1 rows flip under budget 1 yet VPA recovers them; on public SyGuS the vote margin is near one, so an adaptive attacker drives VPA accuracy to zero; accepted public SyGuS slices move across exact-within-pool budget boundaries; and Playgol shows positive paired-bootstrap gaps against typo and same-pool random controls on the 141 accepted rows. A small exact-output prompt harness over 20 controlled margin-1 tasks shows the same qualitative clean-to-attacked pattern across local and API models, while it is treated as a scope check, not a broad LLM benchmark.

AI.tldr: This paper studies adversarial robustness in programming-by-example systems, showing that worst-case corruption can be much more damaging than random noise, and introduces a defense mechanism that works only under specific conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing PBE systems treat wrong examples as random noise, but this paper examines a more dangerous scenario: an adversary who strategically corrupts examples to maximally damage the synthesized program, revealing a robustness dimension that standard evaluations miss.
AI.method: The authors formalize fixed-set worst-case corruption for finite PBE version spaces, implement exact and heuristic corruption searches for string-transformation DSLs, and introduce version-space partition aggregation (VPA) - a defense that synthesizes on disjoint example groups and votes by semantic signatures.
AI.result: Experimental results show: 1) single curated edit flips all 8 spike tasks while random controls succeed much less, 2) VPA recovers margin-1 tasks under budget 1 corruption, 3) on public SyGuS datasets, VPA accuracy drops to zero under adaptive attack, 4) Playgol shows positive gaps against random controls, and 5) similar patterns observed in LLM-based synthesis.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that PBE systems have a significant adversarial robustness dimension that conventional noisy-PBE evaluations overlook. VPA defense only helps when clean semantics maintain partition vote margin, which often fails in realistic tasks. This establishes a bounded but important negative result about PBE robustness.

title: Cache Merging as a Convergent Replicated State for Multi-Agent Latent Reasoning
summary: Multi-agent latent reasoning composes agents' KV-caches into one context for a final agent. Prior work (Agent Primitives) does this by concatenating caches along the sequence axis with RoPE re-encoding, which we call BagMerge. BagMerge is non-commutative, and the best input ordering is unpredictable, shifting with the regime, the latent-step budget, and the model scale. We make this exchange a convergent replicated state. First, CanonicalMerge fixes the layout by content: ordering caches by mean K-norm at a middle layer renders the merged cache byte-identical under any input permutation, verified algorithmically (arity N<=5) and bit-for-bit on real Qwen3-1.7B and 4B state. Second, we separate the replicated state from decode-time layout: the state is a set of content-addressed latent fragments whose merge is set union, a state-based CvRDT (commutative, associative, idempotent, absorbing), and CanonicalMerge is its deterministic render. Because the render is byte-equivalent, every N=2 accuracy number carries over unchanged and re-delivered duplicates are absorbed rather than re-concatenated. On a partitioned-reasoning benchmark, CanonicalMerge matches the best BagMerge ordering in every regime-by-budget-by-ordering cell without knowing which order is best, trading a small, statistically insignificant accuracy margin for an unconditional structural guarantee. The behaviour transfers to real multi-document QA (HotpotQA), while the closest training-free output-fusion baseline (PackLLM) loses by 45 points at matched budget, placing cache-level merging in a regime distinct from output-level fusion. Finally, at k>2 the approach transports and colocates latent traces but does not by itself compose them, which we characterize to motivate future work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CanonicalMerge, a commutative and deterministic method for merging multi-agent KV-caches by ordering them by mean K-norm at a middle layer, eliminating unpredictable performance variations from input ordering.
AI.motivation: Prior work (BagMerge) suffers from being non-commutative, meaning the performance depends on unpredictable input ordering that varies with model scale, regime, and budget, creating reliability issues.
AI.method: CanonicalMerge fixes cache layout by content: ordering KV-caches by mean K-norm at a middle layer, making the merged cache byte-identical under any input permutation. This creates a deterministic render of a replicated state where fragments are content-addressed and their merge is set union (CvRDT).
AI.result: CanonicalMerge matches the best BagMerge ordering in every regime-budget scenario without knowing which order is best, trading minimal accuracy loss for structural guarantees. It outperforms output-fusion baselines (PackLLM) by 45 points on HotpotQA and demonstrates that cache-level merging is distinct from output-level fusion.
AI.conclusion: CanonicalMerge provides reliable, commutative multi-agent cache merging with structural guarantees, solving the ordering unpredictability problem while maintaining competitive performance, though it doesn't solve latent trace composition for k>2, motivating future work.

title: Domain Knowledge Based Temporal-Spatial Graph Convolution Network for ECG Recognition
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Presented at ICONIP 2024, Auckland, New Zealand. Published in LNCS 15290, Springer, 2025
summary: In light of strides in Arti cial Intelligence (AI) and its wide spread application, challenges persist in the interpretability of AI models, particularly within specialized domains like healthcare, such as electro cardiograph (ECG) recognition. Rather than relying solely on end-to-end convolutional neural networks, this paper introduces a novel approach using a domain knowledge-based graph convolution network for ECG recognition. Key landmarks points of PRQST, vital to ECG interpreta tion, are incorporated as domain knowledge. The double-stream directed graph is employed to model both intra and inter ECG cycles. Speci cally, spatial directed graphs capture the positional relationships among key points, while temporal directed graphs delineate temporal dependencies between adjacent cycles in extended ECG sequences. Experimental re sults on the First Chinese ECG Intelligent Competition dataset, which speci cally classify ECG into nine categories, prove the e cacy of the proposed model. The overall average F1 score is 88.1%, the average F1 score of rare categories is 76.3%, both outperform the state-of-the-art models. The introduction of domain knowledge did enhance the detec tion performance, especially for rare categories.

AI.tldr: A novel domain knowledge-based graph convolution network for ECG recognition that incorporates key ECG landmarks (PRQST points) using spatial and temporal directed graphs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on nine ECG categories.
AI.motivation: Despite advances in AI, interpretability remains challenging in specialized domains like healthcare, particularly for ECG recognition. Current end-to-end convolutional neural networks lack domain knowledge integration and interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes a domain knowledge-based graph convolution network that incorporates PRQST landmarks as domain knowledge. Uses double-stream directed graphs: spatial directed graphs for positional relationships among key points, and temporal directed graphs for dependencies between adjacent cycles in extended ECG sequences.
AI.result: Achieved 88.1% overall average F1 score and 76.3% average F1 score for rare categories on the First Chinese ECG Intelligent Competition dataset (9 ECG categories), outperforming state-of-the-art models. Domain knowledge integration significantly improved detection performance, especially for rare categories.
AI.conclusion: The proposed domain knowledge-based graph convolution network effectively addresses interpretability challenges in ECG recognition while improving performance. Incorporating domain knowledge enhances detection capabilities, particularly for rare ECG categories, demonstrating the value of combining domain expertise with deep learning approaches in specialized medical domains.

title: Simulation Based Reward Function Validation for Multi-Agent On Orbit Inspection
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures. This submission integrates a published correction made to the original manuscript. The DOIs for both the original manuscript as well as the correction are provided
summary: A proposed method for the control of groups of inspection spacecraft is Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). While MARL has already been employed for this purpose in previous work, the reward functions used focus on reaching a finite set of predetermined inspection points around the target. In this work, we study and develop a generalized reward function for the MARL inspection task informed by the analysis of 3D reconstructions of inspected objects in orbit. Because the reward function is generalized such that any number of images at arbitrary locations may evaluated, we also allow trained agents to have complete control over when images are collected. With this approach, we gather insights into best practices for not only the specific MARL inspection task, but also gain key takeaways informative to the broader inspection task outside of a MARL context.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a generalized reward function for multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) in spacecraft inspection, allowing agents to determine when and where to capture images based on 3D reconstruction analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing MARL approaches for spacecraft inspection use reward functions focused on reaching predetermined inspection points, which limits flexibility. There's a need for more generalized reward functions that can evaluate images from arbitrary locations and give agents control over timing.
AI.method: Developed a generalized reward function for MARL inspection tasks informed by analysis of 3D reconstructions of inspected objects in orbit. This approach allows any number of images at arbitrary locations to be evaluated and gives trained agents complete control over when images are collected.
AI.result: The generalized reward function enables more flexible spacecraft inspection where agents can decide optimal imaging locations and timing. Insights were gathered regarding best practices for both the specific MARL inspection task and broader inspection applications beyond MARL.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an advancement in MARL-based spacecraft inspection by moving beyond predetermined inspection points to a more generalized approach. This provides valuable insights applicable to both MARL-specific inspection tasks and broader inspection methodologies in space operations.

title: PACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Plausible and Actionable Counterfactual Explanations
summary: Counterfactual explanations explain machine learning predictions by identifying minimal input changes that would alter a model's decision. Although many existing methods successfully generate prediction-changing alternatives, they often produce unrealistic or infeasible recommendations due to a lack of explicit mechanisms for incorporating domain knowledge and intervention constraints. Neuro-symbolic AI offers a promising direction by combining data-driven predictive models with symbolic reasoning capable of representing human-understandable rules and feasible actions. This paper presents PACE, a modular neuro-symbolic framework for generating feasibility-aware counterfactual explanations. The framework separates prediction and reasoning into two components: a neural predictive model for classification and a symbolic reasoning layer that enforces domain-specific constraints during counterfactual generation. By explicitly modeling feasible interventions, the framework produces explanations consistent with domain knowledge while remaining interpretable and actionable. The approach is model-agnostic and adaptable to domains requiring realistic decision support. A case study is conducted on the Adult Income dataset, combining a multilayer perceptron classifier with Answer Set Programming (ASP) rules encoding feasible modifications to education, occupation, and working hours while preserving immutable attributes. Results highlight the trade-off between counterfactual validity and plausibility and show that symbolic constraints yield explanations that better satisfy domain-specific feasibility requirements, illustrating the potential of neuro-symbolic methods for transparent, feasibility-aware counterfactual explanation in explainable AI.

AI.tldr: PACE is a neuro-symbolic framework that generates realistic counterfactual explanations by combining neural predictive models with symbolic reasoning to enforce domain-specific feasibility constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing counterfactual explanation methods often produce unrealistic or infeasible recommendations because they lack mechanisms to incorporate domain knowledge and intervention constraints, limiting their practical usefulness for decision support.
AI.method: PACE uses a modular neuro-symbolic approach with two components: 1) a neural predictive model (multilayer perceptron) for classification, and 2) a symbolic reasoning layer using Answer Set Programming (ASP) to enforce domain-specific constraints during counterfactual generation while preserving immutable attributes.
AI.result: The framework produces explanations that are consistent with domain knowledge while remaining interpretable and actionable. Results on the Adult Income dataset demonstrate the trade-off between counterfactual validity and plausibility, showing that symbolic constraints yield explanations better satisfying domain-specific feasibility requirements.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic methods like PACE have significant potential for transparent, feasibility-aware counterfactual explanations in explainable AI by combining data-driven predictions with symbolic reasoning to generate realistic, actionable decision support.

title: Scaling Laws for Grid-Based Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search in High Dimensions
summary: Grid-based approaches to approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search have been absent from modern scaling analyses. We present a systematic characterization of a multiprobe grid algorithm with respect to dataset size $N$ and dimensionality $d$. Our experiments reveal a previously unreported $d$-scaling crossover on the GloVe embedding family, in which multiprobe grid search maintains an approximately constant dimensional scaling exponent while other graph-, tree-, and partitioning-based methods exhibit degrading throughput. The advantage comes with near-linear query scaling in $N$, but also with lower indexing cost than competing ANN methods. Our results suggest that grid-based methods such as multiprobe grid may be competitive in rebuild-heavy or high-dimensional settings where indexing cost and dimensional robustness dictate performance. More broadly, recent work has formalized self-attention as an ANN operation. Thus, the $N$- and $d$-scaling properties of ANN algorithms may guide cost analysis of efficient transformer architectures. Code is available at: https://github.com/weiz345/MultiProbeANN.

AI.tldr: Multiprobe grid ANN search shows superior dimensional scaling on high-d GloVe embeddings compared to graph/tree methods, with near-linear query scaling and lower indexing cost.
AI.motivation: Grid-based ANN methods have been absent from modern scaling analyses, and their performance characteristics relative to dataset size and dimensionality need systematic characterization.
AI.method: Systematic experimental characterization of multiprobe grid algorithm across different dataset sizes (N) and dimensionalities (d) using GloVe embedding family.
AI.result: Multiprobe grid shows previously unreported d-scaling crossover on GloVe, maintaining constant dimensional scaling exponent while other methods degrade. It has near-linear query scaling in N with lower indexing cost than competing ANN methods.
AI.conclusion: Grid-based methods like multiprobe grid may be competitive in rebuild-heavy or high-dimensional settings where indexing cost and dimensional robustness are crucial. ANN scaling properties may inform cost analysis of efficient transformer architectures.

title: Congestion-Based Slot Pricing in a Railway Auction Game
summary:
We present a multi-agent system for studying the allocation of discrete, congested resources among heterogeneous strategic agents, motivated by the problem of railway slot allocation under deregulation. Multiple operator-agents, differing in size and capacity, interact through a shared auction mechanism over repeated rounds under time-constrained decision-making. The mechanism combines a congestion-based base price that increases with aggregate demand with an asymmetric corrective adjustment that penalises the agent requesting the most slots and rewards the agent requesting the fewest, and is designed to mitigate strategic dominance by large agents while preserving transparency and congestion sensitivity. We formulate the interaction as a repeated game with incomplete information and implement the system as a real-time, web-based multi-agent environment in which human participants control individual agents and observe live marginal-cost and competitor feedback.
  We report exploratory observations from two structured sessions with domain experts acting as operator-agents. The congestion mechanism responds to aggregate demand as designed and the corrective incentives are actively triggered, but agents representing large operators persist with high-request strategies despite the penalty, suggesting that corrective pricing is necessary but not sufficient to neutralise strategic dominance in this multi-agent setting. A post-session debrief indicates that participants' decisions were driven by the assumed agent role rather than personal disposition, and provides qualitative support for strategic motives, such as preserving market presence and raising rivals' costs, operating alongside short-term profit maximisation. We discuss implications for multi-agent mechanism design under asymmetric budgets and outline directions for analytical validation and larger-scale multi-agent experiments.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent system for railway slot allocation combines congestion pricing with asymmetric corrective adjustments to mitigate strategic dominance of large operators, tested with human domain experts in real-time simulations.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of allocating discrete, congested resources (railway slots) among heterogeneous strategic agents under deregulation, aiming to prevent large operators from dominating the market through strategic behavior.
AI.method: Developed a real-time, web-based multi-agent environment where human participants control operator agents in a repeated game with incomplete information. The auction mechanism includes a congestion-based base price that increases with aggregate demand plus an asymmetric corrective adjustment that penalizes agents requesting the most slots and rewards those requesting the fewest.
AI.result: The congestion mechanism responded to aggregate demand as designed, but large operators persisted with high-request strategies despite penalties. Participant decisions were driven by assumed agent roles rather than personal disposition, with strategic motives including market presence preservation and raising rivals' costs alongside profit maximization.
AI.conclusion: Corrective pricing is necessary but not sufficient to neutralize strategic dominance by large agents in multi-agent settings. The study highlights the need for further analytical validation and larger-scale experiments, especially considering asymmetric budgets in mechanism design.

title: Auto-FL-Research: Agentic Search for Federated Learning Algorithms
summary: Federated learning (FL) research often depends on many small but consequential algorithmic choices: optimizer variants, server aggregation rules, local training schedules, normalization, regularization, and model architecture. These choices are expensive to explore manually and difficult to compare fairly when candidate changes can also alter the FL training or evaluation path. In this work, we present Auto-FL-Research (AFR), a constrained coding-agent workflow for FL algorithmic recipe search. Agents may propose and implement candidate training algorithms, including server aggregation rules, client update schedules, local objectives, and registered model variants, while task profiles fix the mutation surface, compute budget, communication contract, and final model evaluation. Each campaign records candidate scores, runtime, edited files, artifacts, and failure status. We evaluate AFR on five healthcare cross-silo FLamby tasks and on grouped-client profiles for the five fixed LEAF datasets plus the LEAF synthetic task. Five-seed repeat evaluations support gains on four FLamby tasks and five of six LEAF profiles, while also exposing seed-sensitive and search-selected failure cases. Same-budget controls show that several gains correspond to FL-recipe changes, whereas other improvements are recovered by fixed-surface scalar controls or fail under repeat or held-out evaluation. These mixed outcomes are part of the contribution: they show how agent-generated candidates can be separated into repeated FL mechanisms, fixed-surface tuning effects, and selected single-run artifacts.

AI.tldr: Auto-FL-Research (AFR) is an automated workflow using coding agents to search for optimal federated learning algorithmic recipes, showing mixed but promising results on healthcare and standard FL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Federated learning research requires exploring many small but important algorithmic choices (optimizers, aggregation rules, training schedules, etc.) that are expensive to explore manually and difficult to compare fairly due to interaction effects between components.
AI.method: AFR uses constrained coding-agent workflow where agents propose and implement candidate training algorithms within fixed constraints: mutation surface, compute budget, communication contract, and evaluation protocol. The system records scores, runtime, edited files, artifacts, and failure status.
AI.result: Evaluation on five healthcare FLamby tasks and six LEAF datasets shows gains on four FLamby tasks and five of six LEAF profiles. However, some improvements correspond to genuine FL-recipe changes while others are due to fixed-surface tuning effects or fail under repeated/hold-out evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The mixed outcomes demonstrate the value of separating agent-generated candidates into three categories: repeated FL mechanisms, fixed-surface tuning effects, and selected single-run artifacts. This helps identify genuine algorithmic improvements versus other effects in FL recipe search.

title: IonSense-QKG: A Quantum-Readiness Metadata Framework for Lithium-Ion Battery Dataset Discovery
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables. Code and metadata artifact available at GitHub
summary:
Public lithium-ion battery datasets are increasingly used for state-of-health estimation, remaining-useful-life prediction, anomaly detection, electrochemical diagnostics, second-life analytics, and battery safety research. However, these datasets vary substantially in chemistry, modality, scale, label quality, sequence structure, access status, and preprocessing complexity. These differences directly affect whether a dataset is feasible for near-term hybrid quantum-classical machine-learning workflows.
  This paper presents IonSense-QKG, a quantum-readiness metadata framework for lithium-ion battery dataset discovery. Starting from the EV-Battery-IonSense index, the proposed framework enriches public battery dataset records with quantum-relevant metadata, including task type, sensing modality, chemistry, label availability, sequence type, preprocessing requirements, candidate quantum encodings, estimated qubit range, and NISQ feasibility. A transparent Quantum Readiness Score is introduced to rank datasets as candidate resources for future hybrid quantum-classical battery benchmarks. The score is intended as a dataset-selection heuristic, not as evidence of quantum advantage.
  The framework demonstrates query-based discovery over enriched metadata to identify datasets suitable for compact quantum feature maps, quantum time-series workflows, limited-label anomaly detection, and future battery-health benchmarking. The released artifact includes metadata tables, scoring scripts, robustness checks, link-checking utilities, and SQL-style query examples. IonSense-QKG positions dataset selection as a data-management problem and provides a reproducible foundation for data-centric quantum battery analytics.

AI.tldr: IonSense-QKG is a quantum-readiness metadata framework that enriches public lithium-ion battery datasets with quantum-relevant attributes and provides a Quantum Readiness Score to help identify datasets suitable for hybrid quantum-classical machine learning workflows.
AI.motivation: Public lithium-ion battery datasets vary significantly in characteristics like chemistry, modality, scale, and preprocessing requirements, making it difficult to determine which datasets are suitable for near-term hybrid quantum-classical machine learning applications. There's a need for systematic metadata enrichment to facilitate dataset selection for quantum battery analytics.
AI.method: The paper proposes IonSense-QKG, a framework that extends the EV-Battery-IonSense index by enriching battery dataset records with quantum-relevant metadata including task type, sensing modality, chemistry, label availability, sequence type, preprocessing requirements, candidate quantum encodings, estimated qubit range, and NISQ feasibility. A transparent Quantum Readiness Score is introduced to rank datasets.
AI.result: The framework enables query-based discovery to identify datasets suitable for various quantum applications including compact quantum feature maps, quantum time-series workflows, limited-label anomaly detection, and future battery-health benchmarking. The released artifact includes metadata tables, scoring scripts, robustness checks, and query examples.
AI.conclusion: IonSense-QKG positions dataset selection as a data-management problem and provides a reproducible foundation for data-centric quantum battery analytics, offering a systematic approach to identify battery datasets that are quantum-ready for hybrid quantum-classical machine learning workflows.

title: CausalSteward: An Agentic Divide-Conquer-Combine Copilot for Causal Discovery
summary: Learning causal models from high-dimensional data is a significant challenge, particularly in real-world settings where violations of core assumptions lead to causal identifiability issues. Although massive amounts of prior knowledge are available, and contain valuable causal information, effectively integrating this knowledge into the causal discovery process remains an open problem. We introduce CausalSTeward (CAST), a novel human-in-the-loop framework for interactively assembling large causal models. CausalSteward is a multi-agent collaborative system that tackles high-dimensional causality through a divide-and-conquer approach where large clusters of variables are iteratively partitioned and then separately analyzed. Our framework fuses prior knowledge with a data-driven approach by using tailored tools such as retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests. Finally, we use this work to examine the capabilities and limitations of causal reasoning in multi-agent frameworks, and how the human-in-the-loop can contribute to accurate and trustworthy results.

AI.tldr: CAST is a human-in-the-loop multi-agent framework for building large causal models by combining prior knowledge with data through iterative partitioning and analysis of variable clusters.
AI.motivation: Causal discovery from high-dimensional data faces identifiability challenges when assumptions are violated, and existing methods struggle to effectively integrate valuable prior causal knowledge into the modeling process.
AI.method: CAST employs a multi-agent collaborative system with a divide-and-conquer approach: iteratively partitions large variable clusters, analyzes them separately using retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests, and integrates human feedback throughout the process.
AI.result: The paper presents the CAST framework that enables assembly of large causal models through interactive human-machine collaboration, addressing high-dimensional causality challenges more effectively than purely data-driven approaches.
AI.conclusion: CAST demonstrates how human-in-the-loop multi-agent frameworks can improve causal reasoning capabilities by effectively integrating prior knowledge with data-driven methods, though limitations in causal reasoning within such frameworks warrant further investigation.

title: The Wiola Architecture for Efficient Small Language Models
summary: We present Wiola, a fully original Small Language Model (SLM) architecture built from first principles, sharing no structural lineage with any existing model family including GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, or Falcon. Wiola introduces five independently novel components: (i) Spiral Rotary Positional Encoding (SRPE), which embeds token positions on a three-dimensional helical manifold combining absolute, relative, and hierarchical positional signals; (ii) Gated Cross-Layer Attention (GCLA), providing each decoder layer with soft cross-attention access to compressed summaries of two preceding layers for inter-layer coherence; (iii) Adaptive Token Merging (ATM), which dynamically merges se mantically redundant adjacent tokens in middle network layers to reduce attention complexity without information loss; (iv) Dual Stream Feed-Forward (DSFF), replacing the conventional MLP with two parallel streams fused by a learned per-dimension gate; and (v) WiolaRMSNorm, a modified normalisation introducing a per-dimension learned offset vector that prevents representation collapse. We provide complete mathematical derivations, architectural block diagrams, complexity analyses, and systematic comparisons against GPT-2, LLaMA-2, and Mistral. Wiola is released in four sizes (120M, 360M, 700M, and 1.5B parameters) and is fully compatible with the HuggingFace Transformers ecosystem, with all 22 architectural unit tests passing.

AI.tldr: Wiola is a completely new Small Language Model architecture with five novel components including 3D positional encoding, cross-layer attention, adaptive token merging, dual-stream feed-forward networks, and modified normalization, released in four sizes with full HuggingFace compatibility.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a fully original SLM architecture that doesn't rely on existing model families (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon), moving beyond incremental improvements to explore fundamentally new architectural components from first principles.
AI.method: Five novel components: 1) Spiral Rotary Positional Encoding (SRPE) using 3D helical manifolds, 2) Gated Cross-Layer Attention for inter-layer coherence, 3) Adaptive Token Merging to reduce complexity, 4) Dual Stream Feed-Forward network with learned gates, and 5) WiolaRMSNorm with per-dimension offsets. Full mathematical derivations and four model sizes (120M to 1.5B parameters).
AI.result: Complete implementation released in four parameter sizes (120M, 360M, 700M, 1.5B) with full HuggingFace Transformers compatibility, passing all 22 architectural unit tests. Systematic comparisons provided against GPT-2, LLaMA-2, and Mistral models.
AI.conclusion: Wiola demonstrates that entirely new language model architectures can be successfully developed from first principles without borrowing from existing families, offering a fresh research direction with novel components that could inspire future architectural innovations.

title: A Novel Machine Learning Approach for Central Nervous System Tumor Classification from DNA Methylation
summary: NA methylation profiling has become a powerful approach for central nervous system (CNS) tumor classification, yet important challenges remain regarding cross-cohort transferability, methodological correctness, and robust multiclass evaluation. In this work, we propose a novel and methodologically rigorous machine-learning approach for methylation-based CNS tumor classification that combines Sparse Random Projection for dimensionality reduction with multinomial logistic regression for classification. We evaluate the proposed approach in the same general experimental setting established by a widely used reference classifier. On the 2,801-sample reference cohort, our method achieves a mean accuracy of 96\% under stratified 3-fold cross-validation. On the independent 1,104-sample clinical evaluation cohort, it reaches 86\% accuracy at the 91-class level and 93\% when predictions are evaluated at the methylation class family level. These results improve upon the corresponding state-of-the-art reference figures of 82\% class-level concordance and 88\% family-level concordance, yielding absolute gains of approximately 4 and 5 percentage points, respectively. This improvement is clinically relevant: in a diagnostic setting, a 5-point increase in correct tumor classification can directly affect cancer subtype assignment and, in turn, influence treatment selection and downstream clinical decision-making. Our results show that the proposed model, grounded in stronger methodological practice in machine learning, consistently outperforms the previous state of the art across evaluation settings and can materially improve the reliability of CNS tumor classification.

AI.tldr: A novel machine learning approach combining Sparse Random Projection and multinomial logistic regression improves CNS tumor classification accuracy over state-of-the-art methods by 4-5 percentage points.
AI.motivation: NA methylation profiling has become powerful for CNS tumor classification but faces challenges with cross-cohort transferability, methodological correctness, and robust multiclass evaluation. Existing methods have room for improvement in accuracy and reliability.
AI.method: Proposes a methodologically rigorous machine learning approach that combines Sparse Random Projection for dimensionality reduction with multinomial logistic regression for classification, evaluated in the same experimental setting as a widely-used reference classifier.
AI.result: Achieves 96% mean accuracy on 2,801-sample reference cohort under stratified 3-fold cross-validation, and 86% accuracy on independent 1,104-sample clinical cohort at 91-class level (93% at family level). This improves upon state-of-the-art reference figures by 4-5 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model, grounded in stronger methodological practice in machine learning, consistently outperforms previous state of the art across evaluation settings and can materially improve the reliability of CNS tumor classification, with clinically relevant implications for treatment selection.

title: AgentsCAD: Automated Design for Manufacturing of FDM Parts via Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning and Geometric Feature Recognition
summary: Parts manufactured with Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) often require Design for Additive Manufacturing (DFAM) modifications to ensure printability, structural integrity, and reduced post-processing. Current slicers identify defects such as steep overhangs but are unable to modify the underlying geometry. This work presents AgentsCAD, a multi-agent system that bridges raw boundary-representation (B-Rep) geometry and Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning to automate targeted DFM. The workflow begins by parsing a STEP file. The agentic system detects overhangs above a 45°threshold, constructs a face-adjacency topology graph, and optionally injects semantic feature labels from a GraphSAGE model trained on MFCAD++ (59,665 parts), before dispatching a Claude Sonnet design-reasoning agent that recommends reorientations, fillets, chamfers, and similar modifications. A GPT-4o vision-language verifier inspects rendered views to confirm geometric integrity. Outputs include a modified STEP file and a human-readable report. A test case on a birdhouse model demonstrates that the system correctly diagnoses overhangs, selects appropriate defect mitigation strategies, and proposes physically valid corrections, partially solving the geometry-to-language translation problem central to LLM-driven CAD modification.

AI.tldr: AgentsCAD is a multi-agent system that uses LLM reasoning to automate Design for Additive Manufacturing (DFAM) modifications for FDM-printed parts by analyzing B-Rep geometry, detecting defects, and recommending corrective modifications.
AI.motivation: Current slicers can identify printing defects like steep overhangs but cannot modify the underlying geometry, creating a gap between defect detection and automated design correction for FDM manufacturing.
AI.method: A multi-agent system that parses STEP files, detects overhangs above 45°, constructs face-adjacency topology graphs, optionally injects semantic feature labels from a GraphSAGE model trained on MFCAD++ dataset, uses Claude Sonnet for design reasoning, and employs GPT-4o vision-language verification.
AI.result: The system successfully diagnoses overhangs, selects appropriate defect mitigation strategies, proposes physically valid corrections, and generates modified STEP files with human-readable reports, demonstrated on a birdhouse test case.
AI.conclusion: AgentsCAD partially solves the geometry-to-language translation problem for LLM-driven CAD modification, bridging raw B-Rep geometry with LLM reasoning to automate targeted DFAM for FDM parts.

title: Agent4cs: A Multi-agent System for Code Summarization in Large Hierarchical Codebases
comment: Accepted to the main track of the 23rd European Conference on Multi-Agent Systems (EUMAS 2026)
summary: Understanding large, complex codebases, especially those with obfuscated structures and incomplete documentation, remains a significant challenge. Existing code summarization solutions often rely on a single language model or coding assistant like Claude Code, and treat source code as flat text, underutilizing the rich interdependencies and hierarchical information within a repository. To address these shortcomings, we propose Agent4cs - a multi-agent framework that summarizes large codebases in a bottom-up fashion, where a summarization agent focuses on producing robust summaries; a keyword-extraction agent proactively identifies critical information from subfolders; and a quality-assurance agent iteratively refines the outputs for readability, coherence, and completeness. Evaluated on 7 frontier models, Agent4cs improves semantic consistency across all folder levels by average 8% compared to two structured prompting baselines with code segments. Furthermore, extensive evaluation on real-world datasets demonstrates up to 38% gains in normalized keyword coverage rate over the same baselines.

AI.tldr: Agent4cs is a multi-agent framework that uses three specialized agents (summarization, keyword-extraction, quality-assurance) to better summarize large, complex codebases through hierarchical bottom-up analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing code summarization approaches treat source code as flat text and underutilize repository structure and interdependencies. They often rely on single models, making them inadequate for complex, obfuscated codebases with incomplete documentation.
AI.method: Proposes Agent4cs - a multi-agent framework operating in bottom-up fashion: 1) summarization agent produces robust summaries, 2) keyword-extraction agent proactively identifies critical information from subfolders, 3) quality-assurance agent iteratively refines outputs for readability, coherence, and completeness.
AI.result: Evaluation on 7 frontier models shows Agent4cs improves semantic consistency across all folder levels by average 8% compared to structured prompting baselines. On real-world datasets, achieves up to 38% gains in normalized keyword coverage rate over the same baselines.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent framework with specialized agents and hierarchical bottom-up approach significantly improves code summarization for large, complex codebases compared to existing flat-text and single-model approaches.

title: From Approximation to Emergence: A Theory of Deep Learning
summary: Deep learning has outgrown any single mathematical explanation. From Approximation to Emergence develops a unified, proof-oriented account of modern deep learning theory, tracing a path from the classical foundations of approximation, optimization, and generalization to the contemporary mechanisms of overparameterization, robustness, generative modeling, transformers, in-context learning, scaling laws, interpretability, alignment, and emergence. Rather than presenting isolated results, the book organizes a broad literature into a coherent research narrative: each theory is examined through the object it controls, the assumptions that make it valid, and the phenomena it leaves unexplained. Written for researchers, graduate students, and mathematically trained practitioners, this monograph offers a rigorous map of deep learning theory as it stands today: powerful, incomplete, and increasingly centered on the question of how learned mechanisms arise from scale, data, architecture, and training.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive monograph that organizes deep learning theory into a unified framework, bridging classical foundations with modern emergent phenomena and highlighting the need to understand how learned mechanisms arise from scale, data, and architecture.
AI.motivation: Deep learning has grown beyond any single mathematical explanation, with current research spanning diverse areas including overparameterization, transformers, scaling laws, interpretability, and emergence. There is a need to unify this broad literature into a coherent theoretical framework rather than presenting isolated results.
AI.method: The book organizes existing deep learning theory into a coherent research narrative by examining each theory through three lenses: the object it controls, the assumptions that make it valid, and the phenomena it leaves unexplained. This structured approach helps build a unified, proof-oriented account of deep learning theory.
AI.result: The monograph offers a rigorous map of deep learning theory as it stands today, showing it to be powerful yet incomplete. It traces a developmental path from classical foundations (approximation, optimization, generalization) to contemporary mechanisms (overparameterization, robustness, generative modeling, transformers, in-context learning, scaling laws, interpretability, alignment, and emergence).
AI.conclusion: The monograph proposes that deep learning theory must evolve from studying isolated optimization and approximation properties to understanding how complex mechanisms emerge from the interplay of scale, data, architecture, and training. It argues for a unified framework that moves beyond classical mathematical explanations toward explaining emergent phenomena in modern deep learning systems.

title: Adoption and Ecosystem Health: A Longitudinal Analysis of Open-Source Multi-Agent Frameworks
summary: Since ChatGPT's launch in November 2022, open-source agentic frameworks have proliferated, making framework selection important for engineering teams while obscured by popularity signals such as GitHub stars. This paper analyzes 15 major open-source AI agent framework repositories from late 2022 to early 2026, using 808,042 stars, 73,997 pull requests, 86,241 commits, and 987,330 user profiles to assess ecosystem health across awareness, adoption, and retention. Three findings emerge. First, headline popularity is unreliable. Star counts reflect hype cycles and inorganic activity. AutoGPT gained 111,967 stars in one month but converted fewer than 9 contributors per 1,000 stars, defined as contributor density in this research, compared with LangChain's 41. Lower-profile frameworks such as Pydantic-AI show higher contributor density, indicating deeper adoption. Second, mapping awareness against adoption shows that visibility and engagement diverge. MetaGPT and LangFlow have contributor density ratios below 5 even with their high visibility. Openai-agents-python's limited contributor base suggests institutional backing alone does not ensure community depth. By analyzing cross-framework contribution, we discover that LangChain functions as a shared infrastructure, attracting 82.5% of cross-ecosystem contributors. Third, retention drops most steeply in the first 30 days of initial contribution and stabilizes near 90 days. Overall, ecosystem health is better measured by contributor density, cross-ecosystem engagement, and retention than by stars alone. These metrics offer teams a more robust basis for framework evaluation.

AI.tldr: Headline GitHub star counts are unreliable for evaluating AI agent frameworks. Better metrics include contributor density, cross-ecosystem engagement, and retention patterns.
AI.motivation: With the proliferation of open-source AI agent frameworks since ChatGPT's launch, engineering teams face challenges selecting frameworks due to misleading popularity signals like GitHub stars, which don't reflect actual ecosystem health.
AI.method: Analyzed 15 major open-source AI agent framework repositories from late 2022 to early 2026 using comprehensive data: 808,042 stars, 73,997 pull requests, 86,241 commits, and 987,330 user profiles to assess ecosystem health across awareness, adoption, and retention dimensions.
AI.result: 1) Star counts reflect hype cycles and inorganic activity (AutoGPT gained 111,967 stars in one month but had low contributor density). 2) High visibility doesn't guarantee engagement (MetaGPT/LangFlow have low contributor density). 3) LangChain serves as shared infrastructure attracting 82.5% cross-ecosystem contributors. 4) Contributor retention drops sharply in first 30 days then stabilizes at 90 days.
AI.conclusion: Ecosystem health is better measured by contributor density, cross-ecosystem engagement, and retention patterns than by stars alone. These metrics provide a more robust framework evaluation basis for engineering teams.

title: When Should Service Agents Reconsider? Difficulty-Routed Control in Customer-Service Operations
summary: Autonomous customer-service agents are shifting from conversational interfaces toward operational execution roles: they retrieve firm records, apply service policies, and execute backend writes such as refunds, cancellations, exchanges, order modifications, and reservation changes. This shift creates a service-control problem: firms must keep routine service fast and low-friction while preventing operational errors on requests where customer instructions, policy constraints, firm records, and backend writes interact. We propose a difficulty-routed service-control architecture that asks when service agents should reconsider before acting. A lightweight router keeps routine sessions on a low-cost baseline path and routes operationally coupled sessions to an escalated workflow. The escalated path uses conflict-aware communication and write-triggered reconsideration to concentrate deliberation and safeguards before consequential backend writes, rather than applying additional control uniformly across all service sessions. We evaluate the architecture on human-verified retail and airline tasks from $τ^{2}$-bench. In retail, the method improves reliability consistently on service requests with operational conflict. Routing evidence shows that stronger control is directed toward conflicted requests rather than broadly applied to routine ones. Dialogue and tool-use profiles suggest that gains do not come from indiscriminate interaction expansion or broader tool chains; instead, added turns and tool calls support evidence gathering, write separation, and pre-write reconsideration. Case-level evidence shows that the escalated workflow preserves fallback plans, binds retrieved records to the correct action, sequences writes, and decomposes multi-entity requests. Airline results extend the same service-control logic to reservation operations.

AI.tldr: A difficulty-routed architecture for autonomous customer-service agents that escalates operationally conflicted requests to a safer workflow with write-triggered reconsideration, improving reliability on complex tasks without slowing down routine service.
AI.motivation: As autonomous service agents evolve from conversational interfaces to operational execution roles (handling refunds, cancellations, exchanges, etc.), they create a service-control problem: maintaining fast routine service while preventing operational errors when customer instructions, policies, records, and backend writes interact.
AI.method: A lightweight router keeps routine sessions on a low-cost baseline path and routes operationally coupled sessions to an escalated workflow. The escalated path uses conflict-aware communication and write-triggered reconsideration to concentrate deliberation and safeguards before consequential backend writes.
AI.result: The method improves reliability consistently on service requests with operational conflict in retail tasks from τ²-bench. Stronger control is directed toward conflicted requests rather than broadly applied to routine ones. Gains come from evidence gathering, write separation, and pre-write reconsideration rather than indiscriminate interaction expansion.
AI.conclusion: The difficulty-routed service-control architecture effectively addresses the tension between fast routine service and safe operational execution, demonstrating that targeted escalation to deliberative workflows with pre-write safeguards can improve reliability without sacrificing efficiency for simple requests.

title: Black-Box Inference of LLM Architectural Properties with Restrictive API Access
summary: In practice, most commercial LLM providers do not publicly release details of underlying LLM architectures. However, prior work has shown that given limited API access to an LLM (namely, top-$k$ logits and/or a logit bias function), one can recover certain architectural details of an LLM, such as the hidden dimension of the feed-forward network. Perhaps in response to these results, most commercial LLM providers have restricted their APIs to expose only the single logit for each decoded token, and they no longer give users the ability to bias logits. We show that even under current restrictive APIs, several architectural parameters are still recoverable. We present NightVision, an attack that uses restrictive black-box API access to estimate the hidden dimension, depth, and parameter count of an LLM. Algorithmically, NightVision relies on a novel common set prompting technique in which multiple prompts expose log probabilities for the same set of output tokens; a spectral analysis of these results is used to infer hidden dimension. NightVision additionally uses end-to-end time to first token (TTFT) measurements and the estimated hidden dimension to estimate depth and parameter count. We empirically evaluate NightVision on 32 open-source LLMs, recovering hidden dimension to within 23% average relative error across all models (9% on MoE models), and depth and parameter count to within 53% for models exceeding three billion parameters. We run extensive ablations to demonstrate how these accuracies scale with token budget and model properties. Overall, our results suggest that current LLM APIs are not sufficiently restricted to fully obfuscate the architectural details of their underlying models.

AI.tldr: NightVision attack recovers LLM architectural parameters (hidden dimension, depth, parameter count) using only restrictive black-box API access with common set prompting and spectral analysis.
AI.motivation: Commercial LLM providers restrict APIs to hide architectural details for competitive advantage, but even current restrictive APIs still leak information about model architecture.
AI.method: Uses common set prompting technique where multiple prompts expose log probabilities for same output tokens, then applies spectral analysis to infer hidden dimension. Combines this with time-to-first-token (TTFT) measurements to estimate depth and parameter count.
AI.result: Evaluated on 32 open-source LLMs: hidden dimension recovered within 23% average relative error (9% on MoE models), depth and parameter count within 53% error for models >3B parameters.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM APIs are not sufficiently restricted to fully obfuscate architectural details; more restrictive measures may be needed to prevent model architecture extraction.

title: Hardware-Enforced Semantic Coordination for Safety-Critical Real-Time Autonomous Systems
summary:
Recent advances in agentic AI are producing increasingly complex autonomous systems that integrate large language models, world models, optimization engines, specialized neural architectures, autonomous platforms, and human operators. While much current research focuses on improving reasoning capabilities, safety-critical real-time deployment also requires bounded and verifiable coordination among heterogeneous components operating concurrently under uncertainty. Software-mediated coordination presents fundamental limitations in domains where bounded latency, deterministic coordination, and enforceable safety guarantees are essential.
  Hence, we propose a hardware-enforced semantic coordination architecture in which selected coordination semantics are implemented directly at the hardware level via field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs). The approach builds on the Topic-Based Communication Space Petri Net (TB-CSPN) framework, which separates semantic reasoning from interaction management.
  In this approach, selected TB-CSPN coordination mechanisms are mapped onto FPGA primitives, creating a hardware-native semantic coordination layer. Focus is not on acceleration, but on enforcing temporal synchronization, semantic gating, authorization constraints, and bounded coordination behavior directly in hardware. Semantic reasoning remains adaptive and software-driven, while embedded coordination semantics become deterministic.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hardware-enforced semantic coordination architecture using FPGAs to implement coordination semantics directly in hardware, separating semantic reasoning from deterministic interaction management.
AI.motivation: As agentic AI systems become more complex with heterogeneous components (LLMs, world models, optimization engines, neural architectures, autonomous platforms, human operators), software-mediated coordination presents limitations for safety-critical real-time deployment where bounded latency, deterministic coordination, and enforceable safety guarantees are essential.
AI.method: Proposes a hardware-enforced semantic coordination architecture where selected coordination semantics from the Topic-Based Communication Space Petri Net (TB-CSPN) framework are implemented directly at the hardware level via FPGAs, creating a hardware-native semantic coordination layer that enforces temporal synchronization, semantic gating, authorization constraints, and bounded coordination behavior.
AI.result: The approach enables deterministic embedded coordination semantics while keeping semantic reasoning adaptive and software-driven, providing bounded and verifiable coordination among heterogeneous AI components operating concurrently under uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Hardware-enforced semantic coordination via FPGAs addresses fundamental limitations of software-mediated coordination for safety-critical agentic AI systems, offering a viable approach for domains requiring bounded latency, deterministic coordination, and enforceable safety guarantees.

title: CreativityNeuro: Steering Language Model Weights to Improve Divergent Thinking and Reduce Mode Collapse
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Creativity & Generative AI
summary: Divergent thinking is a crucial aspect of creativity, yet large language models (LLMs) tend to consistently generate similar responses to open-ended questions, in what has been termed the artificial hivemind effect. Here, we introduce CreativityNeuro, a data-free method for enhancing divergent thinking in LLMs via contrastive weight steering. We evaluate our method across multiple creativity assessments and report several main findings. On the Divergent Association Task (DAT), a vocabulary-space creativity test, CreativityNeuro improves performance by up to 14 human percentile points. Next, in a large-scale human evaluation (N=720) on the Alternative Uses Test (AUT) and the Task Task, CreativityNeuro achieves significant improvements in originality, surprise, and creativity, transferring to longer-form and more open-ended tasks. Importantly, we find that across all three tasks, CreativityNeuro demonstrably reduces measures of mode collapse. Moreover, activation steering achieves comparable performance to CreativityNeuro on the DAT, but it does not transfer to the AUT and Task Task, demonstrating the effectiveness of weight-space steering in generalizing to unseen tasks. In conclusion, CreativityNeuro improves divergent thinking and reduces mode collapse without requiring behavioral data, re-training, or gradient-based fine-tuning, providing a straightforward way to enhance LLM performance in creative domains.

AI.tldr: CreativityNeuro is a data-free contrastive weight steering method that enhances divergent thinking in LLMs, reducing mode collapse and improving performance across multiple creativity assessments without requiring training data or fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit an "artificial hivemind effect" where they generate similar responses to open-ended questions, limiting their divergent thinking capabilities which are crucial for creativity.
AI.method: CreativityNeuro uses contrastive weight steering in weight space (rather than activation space) to enhance divergent thinking without requiring behavioral data, re-training, or gradient-based fine-tuning.
AI.result: On Divergent Association Task: improves performance by up to 14 human percentile points. In human evaluations (N=720) on Alternative Uses Test and Task Task: significant improvements in originality, surprise, and creativity. Reduces mode collapse across all three tasks. Weight-space steering outperforms activation steering in transferring to unseen tasks.
AI.conclusion: CreativityNeuro effectively improves divergent thinking and reduces mode collapse in LLMs through data-free weight-space steering, providing a straightforward way to enhance LLM performance in creative domains without requiring behavioral data or retraining.

title: Multi-modal Rail Crossing Safety Analysis
summary: Given one or more images of a railway crossing, can we leverage visual cues that allow us to robustly estimate how safe it is? Can we improve our ability to do so by introducing structured data (such as official accident reports) about the accident history of that crossing into our models? In this work, we explore how to best answer those questions towards building an AI system that can ingest multi-modal data for railway crossings and provide safety assessment and scores that align with expert opinion and with safety scoring used by the Federal Railroad Administration (FRA). To that end, we propose a proof-of-concept pipeline that delivers on that goal, while at the same time exploring and tackling a number of critical research challenges that pertain to different parts of the pipeline, from data preparation to different learning paradigms that can allow us to realize such a system. Indicatively, our proposed system identifies HIGH-RISK and LOW-RISK crossings with a macro F1 score of 0.757 and estimates FRA-based safety scores with an RMSE of 0.078 and correlation of 0.492 using a routed fine-tuned compact VLM pipeline, while producing qualitative results that align with domain-expert assessment.

AI.tldr: A multimodal AI system that combines visual data from railway crossings with structured accident reports to assess safety, achieving good performance in risk classification and FRA score estimation.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop an AI system that can assess railway crossing safety by leveraging both visual cues from images and structured data like accident reports, with the goal of producing safety assessments that align with expert opinions and official FRA safety scoring standards.
AI.method: The authors propose a proof-of-concept multimodal pipeline that processes visual data (railway crossing images) alongside structured accident data. They use a routed fine-tuned compact VLM (Visual Language Model) pipeline, addressing research challenges from data preparation to various learning paradigms for integrating different data modalities.
AI.result: The system achieves a macro F1 score of 0.757 for identifying HIGH-RISK and LOW-RISK crossings, and estimates FRA-based safety scores with an RMSE of 0.078 and correlation of 0.492. Qualitative results also align with domain-expert assessments.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates the feasibility of building effective multimodal AI systems for railway crossing safety assessment by combining visual and structured data, with the proposed pipeline showing promising results in both quantitative metrics and qualitative alignment with expert judgment.

title: What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates
summary: LLM agents will increasingly act in socially structured settings where role, audience, and relational context can shape what is advantageous or costly to say. We study whether such social structure, without any explicit objective in the prompt, changes what an agent expresses publicly relative to an off-the-record (OTR) channel elicited under the same condition. We introduce a dual-channel debate framework in which agents produce public utterances that enter the shared history alongside OTR responses that are recorded but never shown to the other participant. Across 10 models, 3 scenarios, and 5 variations within each scenario, alignment-inducing settings produce systematic public-OTR divergence in the targeted agent, with its decision divergence rising from a $\sim$3% baseline to roughly 40%. The effect is consistent across four aggregate analyses: stance, semantic similarity, natural language inference, and survey responses. In some cases, the OTR response explicitly attributes public accommodation to relational pressures, such as career risk or sponsorship obligation. The findings suggest that agent evaluation should extend beyond explicit goals and detect emergent objectives. We present a dual-channel evaluation framework and complementary behavioral measures that operationalize this assessment.

AI.tldr: LLM agents in social settings exhibit systematic differences between public statements and private (off-the-record) responses, with alignment pressures increasing divergence from 3% to 40% across various models and scenarios.
AI.motivation: LLM agents increasingly operate in socially structured settings where social factors (role, audience, relational context) influence expression, but current evaluation focuses only on explicit goals, missing emergent social pressures that shape agent behavior.
AI.method: Developed a dual-channel debate framework where agents produce both public utterances (shared history) and private off-the-record (OTR) responses. Tested across 10 models, 3 scenarios, and 5 variations each. Used four analytical methods: stance analysis, semantic similarity, natural language inference, and survey responses.
AI.result: Alignment-inducing settings produce systematic public-OTR divergence, increasing from ~3% baseline to ~40%. The effect is consistent across all analytical methods. OTR responses sometimes explicitly attribute public accommodations to relational pressures like career risk or sponsorship obligation.
AI.conclusion: Agent evaluation must extend beyond explicit goals to detect emergent social objectives. The authors present a dual-channel evaluation framework with complementary behavioral measures for operationalizing this assessment.

title: Discrete Diffusion Language Models for Interactive Radiology Report Drafting
summary: Diffusion language models, which generate text by denoising a token canvas bidirectionally instead of emitting tokens left to right, have become competitive with autoregressive (AR) generation. Medical foundation models, however, remain almost entirely autoregressive. We adapt a mixture-of-experts diffusion language model, DiffusionGemma-26B, and benchmark it against its same-size AR sibling Gemma-4-26B under an identical LoRA recipe on medical visual question answering datasets, scored by a verbosity-robust LLM judge. Diffusion matches or exceeds AR on all of them, and the finetuned model (3.8B active) is competitive with frontier vision-language models; its decoding is also 3.5-4.4x faster. Beyond this parity, the diffusion model offers a drafting capability AR lacks: any-order infill. Because the canvas is denoised bidirectionally, a radiologist can fix report fragments and have the model fill the text between them, an operation inherent to diffusion but not to autoregression, which is subpar at it. This suits real reports, which are often terse or inconsistent across clinicians and institutions.

AI.tldr: Diffusion language models can match or exceed autoregressive models on medical VQA tasks while being faster and offering unique infill capabilities that are valuable for medical reporting.
AI.motivation: Medical foundation models are almost entirely autoregressive despite diffusion language models becoming competitive with AR generation. There's a need to explore diffusion models for medical applications, particularly for capabilities like any-order infill that could address real-world challenges in medical reporting.
AI.method: Adapted a mixture-of-experts diffusion language model (DiffusionGemma-26B) and benchmarked it against its same-size autoregressive sibling (Gemma-4-26B) using identical LoRA fine-tuning recipes. Evaluation used medical visual question answering datasets with a verbosity-robust LLM judge.
AI.result: Diffusion matches or exceeds AR performance on all medical VQA datasets. The fine-tuned diffusion model (3.8B active parameters) is competitive with frontier vision-language models while offering 3.5-4.4x faster decoding. Most significantly, diffusion provides any-order infill capability that AR lacks, allowing radiologists to fix report fragments and have the model fill in between.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion language models are not only competitive with autoregressive models for medical applications but offer superior practical advantages including faster decoding and unique any-order infill capabilities that address real-world challenges in medical reporting where reports are often terse or inconsistent across clinicians and institutions.

title: How Should Transformers Encode Numeric Values in Electronic Health Records?
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, 3 tables, accepted to ICML 2026, to be published in Proceedings of Machine Learning Research
summary: How do we encode numeric values in transformer-based sequence processing, particularly in electronic health record (EHR) data? We systematically compare discrete, continuous, and hybrid value encoding strategies using synthetic arithmetic tasks embedded within real-world EHR data, as well as real-world clinical prediction tasks. Our study reveals trade-offs between numeric precision, optimisation stability, and architectural flexibility. We find that approaches that explicitly model value-concept interactions perform best on precision-sensitive arithmetic tasks when architectural constraints permit. Hybrid token-based approaches that retain numeric values but apply binning prior to projection provide a more robust and broadly applicable alternative, with the optimal number of bins following a simple empirically derived power-law in dataset size. Across tasks, models consistently exhibit reliable "good enough" numeric computation rather than exact arithmetic, while clinical gains from incorporating laboratory values are task-dependent. This suggests that robustness and deployability often outweigh maximal numeric precision in practice, motivating hybrid token-based approaches as a practical default.

AI.tldr: Comparing encoding methods for numeric values in transformer EHR models reveals hybrid token-based approaches with value binning offer robust trade-offs between precision and deployability.
AI.motivation: To understand how best to encode numeric values in transformer-based sequence processing, particularly for electronic health record (EHR) data, since numeric values are critical components of clinical data but their optimal representation in transformers remains unclear.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of discrete, continuous, and hybrid value encoding strategies using: (1) synthetic arithmetic tasks embedded within real-world EHR data, and (2) real-world clinical prediction tasks. Investigated value-concept interaction modeling and hybrid token-based approaches with binning prior to projection.
AI.result: 1. Value-concept interaction models perform best on precision-sensitive arithmetic tasks when architectural constraints permit. 2. Hybrid token-based approaches with binning before projection provide robust, broadly applicable alternatives with optimal bin count following a power-law relation to dataset size. 3. Models exhibit reliable "good enough" numeric computation rather than exact arithmetic. 4. Clinical gains from lab values are task-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Robustness and deployability often outweigh maximal numeric precision in practice, motivating hybrid token-based approaches as a practical default for encoding numeric values in transformer-based EHR processing.

title: Beyond Next-Token Prediction: An RLVR Proof of Concept for Tool-Use Agents on Atlassian Workflows
summary: Large language models are trained to predict the next token, not to act inside a specific API. In niche enterprise SaaS workflows -- where success means hitting the right endpoint with the right nested arguments in the right order -- this objective mismatch shows up as silent failures: dropped required fields, hallucinated tools, or early stops after a single read. We ask whether Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), applied directly in the target environment, closes the gap. As a proof of concept we build a suite of five synthetic environments emulating the Jira REST v3 and Confluence v2 APIs at schema fidelity; rewards are computed entirely from the tool-call trace, with no live API, no learned judge, and no human label in the loop. Scoring prompted Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3.5-4B on the same checkers that drive GRPO training, we find that on the four scenarios whose rewards are non-degenerate the RL-trained policy lifts average reward from a 4B-baseline range of 0.35--0.92 to 0.95--1.00, with the largest single gain on Confluence page creation ($0.35 \rightarrow 1.00$). We position this as a preliminary step toward outcome-optimised small models for niche enterprise APIs, and foreground two limitations a workshop reader should weigh: hand-crafting verifiable rewards does not scale beyond the handful of endpoints reported here, and one of our five scenarios (ticket-transition) has a saturating reward shape that the prompted 4B already maxes out.
