AI.tldr: RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards) significantly improves small LLMs' ability to correctly use niche enterprise APIs by training directly in synthetic environments, achieving near-perfect performance on most tasks.
AI.motivation: There's an objective mismatch between standard LLM training (next token prediction) and the requirements of niche enterprise SaaS workflows, where models must correctly hit specific API endpoints with proper nested arguments in sequence. This leads to silent failures like dropped required fields, hallucinated tools, or early stops after single reads.
AI.method: The authors use Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) applied directly in target environments. They create five synthetic environments emulating Jira REST v3 and Confluence v2 APIs at schema fidelity. Rewards are computed entirely from tool-call traces without live APIs, learned judges, or human labels. They test on Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3.5-4B models using GRPO training with the same checkers that drive training.
AI.result: On four scenarios with non-degenerate rewards, RL-trained policies lift average reward from baseline ranges of 0.35-0.92 to 0.95-1.00. The largest gain is on Confluence page creation (0.35 → 1.00). The ticket-transition scenario has saturating rewards that the prompted 4B already maximizes.
AI.conclusion: This represents a preliminary step toward outcome-optimized small models for niche enterprise APIs. Key limitations: hand-crafting verifiable rewards doesn't scale beyond a handful of endpoints, and some scenarios have reward shapes that baseline models already optimize effectively.

title: NeuroBridge: Bridging Multi-Task MRI Knowledge for Neurodegenerative Disease Diagnosis
summary: INTRODUCTION: Accurate MRI-based identification of Alzheimer's disease (AD), mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and related dementias remains challenging because disease-related structural changes are often subtle and heterogeneous. We developed NeuroBridge, a clinically guided multi-task MRI framework for neurodegenerative disease diagnosis. METHODS: NeuroBridge integrates large-scale self-supervised MRI pretraining with hippocampal segmentation, hippocampal atrophy classification, and reconstruction objectives, followed by gated fusion fine-tuning. Performance was evaluated across ADNI and OASIS cohorts, including cross-cohort transfer, probability-based analysis, and opportunistic screening. RESULTS: NeuroBridge achieved the highest performance across evaluated classification tasks, reaching 88.17% accuracy for AD versus cognitively normal controls in ADNI and 82.78% in OASIS. The largest gains occurred in MCI-related and mixed-diagnosis settings. The framework demonstrated strong cross-cohort generalization, systematic associations between predicted-class probability and accuracy, and the feasibility of probability-based opportunistic screening. DISCUSSION: Clinically guided multi-task representation learning improves neurodegenerative MRI diagnosis beyond conventional single-task approaches. NeuroBridge provides a robust and scalable framework for dementia assessment and MRI-based opportunistic screening.

AI.tldr: NeuroBridge is a clinically guided multi-task MRI framework that uses self-supervised pretraining with hippocampal segmentation, atrophy classification, and reconstruction tasks, followed by gated fusion fine-tuning, achieving state-of-the-art performance for Alzheimer's disease and dementia diagnosis across multiple cohorts.
AI.motivation: Accurate MRI-based identification of Alzheimer's disease, mild cognitive impairment, and related dementias is challenging because disease-related structural changes are often subtle and heterogeneous. Existing single-task approaches may not capture the complexity of neurodegenerative diseases effectively.
AI.method: NeuroBridge integrates large-scale self-supervised MRI pretraining with three key tasks: hippocampal segmentation, hippocampal atrophy classification, and reconstruction objectives. This is followed by gated fusion fine-tuning. The framework was evaluated across ADNI and OASIS cohorts with cross-cohort transfer, probability-based analysis, and opportunistic screening assessments.
AI.result: NeuroBridge achieved the highest performance across evaluated classification tasks: 88.17% accuracy for AD vs cognitively normal controls in ADNI and 82.78% in OASIS. The largest performance gains occurred in MCI-related and mixed-diagnosis settings. The framework demonstrated strong cross-cohort generalization, systematic associations between predicted-class probability and accuracy, and feasibility of probability-based opportunistic screening.
AI.conclusion: Clinically guided multi-task representation learning improves neurodegenerative MRI diagnosis beyond conventional single-task approaches. NeuroBridge provides a robust and scalable framework for dementia assessment and MRI-based opportunistic screening, offering better performance and generalization across different clinical populations.

title: World Feedback for Clinical Agents: Diagnosing RL in FHIR Environments
summary: Clinical protocol-execution tasks -- checking a lab value, applying a threshold, placing a correctly structured FHIR order -- are natural candidates for RL from world feedback: once clinical SMEs encode decision logic into a verifier, that verifier grades unlimited rollouts without per-episode annotation. But applying RL requires a sound feedback channel and sufficient base capability. We audit MedAgentBench v1/v2, find a 41.7\% silent-finish ceiling that makes inaction the RL dominant strategy, and construct \textbf{MedAgentBench-v3 (MAB-v3)} (508 tasks, 8.9\% ceiling). Training Qwen3-8B exposes two structural barriers: a \emph{capability ceiling} (10/20 task types have 0\% base performance, zero gradient) and a \emph{format-knowledge barrier} (3/20 types require exact clinical codes undiscoverable by exploration). Pure RL reaches 18.2\% pass@1 vs.\ 34.1\% for rule-based SFT; the 15.9~pp gap is attributable entirely to these barriers. A decision/format-knowledge/lookup taxonomy predicts RL learnability and prescribes the fix: SFT to inject codes, RL to learn conditionals.

AI.tldr: RL for clinical protocol tasks faces structural barriers: high "silent-finish" ceilings incentivize inaction, capability gaps prevent learning, and format knowledge barriers require external code injection.
AI.motivation: Clinical protocol-execution tasks (lab value checks, threshold applications, structured FHIR orders) should be ideal for RL from world feedback since clinical subject matter experts can encode decision logic into verifiers that grade unlimited rollouts without per-episode annotation.
AI.method: 1) Audited MedAgentBench v1/v2, found 41.7% silent-finish ceiling that makes inaction the RL dominant strategy. 2) Constructed MedAgentBench-v3 (MAB-v3) with 508 tasks and 8.9% ceiling. 3) Trained Qwen3-8B model, identified structural barriers: capability ceiling (10/20 task types had 0% base performance) and format-knowledge barrier (3/20 types require exact clinical codes). 4) Tested pure RL vs. rule-based supervised fine-tuning (SFT).
AI.result: Pure RL reached 18.2% pass@1 vs. 34.1% for rule-based SFT, showing a 15.9 percentage point gap entirely attributable to the structural barriers. A decision/format-knowledge/lookup taxonomy predicts RL learnability.
AI.conclusion: The solution requires hybrid approach: SFT to inject necessary clinical codes, RL to learn conditionals. The taxonomy provides a predictive framework for understanding what parts of clinical decision tasks can be learned through RL versus what requires external knowledge injection.

title: Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics Enable Complete and Scalable $\mathrm{E}(3)$-Equivariant Networks
summary: $\mathrm{E}(3)$-equivariant networks are promising for 3D atomistic system modeling, yet their scalability is limited by the $O(L^6)$ complexity of the Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Product (CGTP). The recently proposed Gaunt Tensor Product (GTP) reduces the complexity but is unable to capture the antisymmetric paths, resulting in incomplete expressivity. In this work, we present SpinGTP, an approach to overcome the GTP incompleteness by generalizing from scalar functions to Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics (SWSH). By relying on the algebraic properties of SWSH, SpinGTP recovers the missing antisymmetric interactions while maintaining the asymptotic efficiency of GTP. It also allows for a more expressive equivariant basis that naturally accounts for the parity-odd components of tensor products. We evaluate SpinGTP across diverse benchmarks, including Tetris, 3BPA, SPICE-MACE-OFF, and OC20. Our results show that SpinGTP achieves accuracies comparable to full CGTP. Notably, by explicitly capturing antisymmetric paths, SpinGTP exhibits superior performance in tasks involving chiral materials and non-centrosymmetric geometries. This work provides a complete, scalable, and mathematically rigorous path toward high-order equivariance in large-scale 3D atomistic system simulations.

AI.tldr: SpinGTP improves E(3)-equivariant neural networks by generalizing the Gaunt Tensor Product to use Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics, recovering missing antisymmetric interactions while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to full Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Product.
AI.motivation: E(3)-equivariant networks for 3D atomistic systems face scalability issues due to O(L^6) complexity of Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Product (CGTP). While Gaunt Tensor Product (GTP) reduces complexity, it lacks antisymmetric paths, resulting in incomplete expressivity and inability to handle certain physical interactions.
AI.method: SpinGTP generalizes from scalar functions to Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics (SWSH). By leveraging algebraic properties of SWSH, the approach recovers missing antisymmetric interactions while maintaining GTP's asymptotic efficiency. It enables more expressive equivariant basis that naturally incorporates parity-odd tensor product components.
AI.result: SpinGTP achieves accuracies comparable to full CGTP across diverse benchmarks (Tetris, 3BPA, SPICE-MACE-OFF, OC20). It shows superior performance for chiral materials and non-centrosymmetric geometries due to explicit antisymmetric path capture, while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SpinGTP provides a complete, scalable, and mathematically rigorous approach for high-order equivariance in large-scale 3D atomistic simulations, overcoming GTP's expressivity limitations without sacrificing computational efficiency.

title: Procedural Memory Distillation: Online Reflection for Self-Improving Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), along with recent selfdistillation variants such as SDPO, evaluates each rollout against a verifier and updates the policy from that episode-level signal. However, the richer procedural information in the rollout is rarely retained or reused. Across episodes and epochs, the model repeatedly encounters related problems under a changing policy, producing cross-episode signals that episode-local updates cannot capture: which strategies consistently pass verification, which failure modes persist, which patterns recur. We propose Procedural Memory Distillation (PMD), which converts these crossepisode signals into reusable procedural memory and distills it into the policy's weights during training. This memory functions as a training scaffold, absorbed into the policy itself, yielding a memory-free model at inference. PMD organizes the memory at three levels of abstraction: raw trajectories, self-reflected strategies and lessons, and higher-level behavioral patterns that recur across problems, all extracted online from the model's own trajectories. A memory-conditioned self-teacher draws on the accumulated experience to supervise the student on its own rollouts, enabling student to progressively internalize procedural knowledge within its parameters. The central design principle is co-evolution: the policy generates rollouts that update the memory, and memory shapes the supervision that updates the policy. Empirically, across Qwen3-8B and OLMo3-Instruct-7B, PMD improves over SDPO by 3.8-5.5% on SCIKNOWEVAL and 7.9-13.6% on LIVECODEBENCH. Co-evolution powers these gains: freezing either the memory or the policy trails PMD by more than 10% across SCIKNOWEVAL domains.

AI.tldr: PMD introduces procedural memory distillation that converts cross-episode signals from RLVR into reusable memory scaffold, improving policy training through co-evolution between memory and policy.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR methods discard rich procedural information from rollouts, failing to capture cross-episode patterns like which strategies consistently work, which failure modes persist, and which behavioral patterns recur across problems under changing policies.
AI.method: PMD builds procedural memory at three abstraction levels: raw trajectories, self-reflected strategies/lessons, and higher-level behavioral patterns extracted online from model's trajectories. A memory-conditioned self-teacher uses accumulated experience to supervise student on its rollouts, enabling progressive internalization through co-evolution of policy and memory.
AI.result: Across Qwen3-8B and OLMo3-Instruct-7B, PMD improves over SDPO by 3.8-5.5% on SCIKNOWEVAL and 7.9-13.6% on LIVECODEBENCH. Freezing either memory or policy trails PMD by more than 10% across SCIKNOWEVAL domains, demonstrating co-evolution's importance.
AI.conclusion: PMD successfully captures and distills cross-episode procedural knowledge into policy weights through co-evolution, significantly improving RLVR performance and showing that memory scaffolds can be absorbed into parameterized models for memory-free inference.

title: The Rollout Infrastructure Tax in Coding-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Preprint. 6 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to ACM SoCC 2026
summary: Coding-agent reinforcement learning treats execution infrastructure as a background implementation detail, despite relying on large numbers of interactive software rollouts. This is a missed opportunity: measuring infrastructure overhead can reveal practical efficiency gains for RL post-training, where small per-rollout savings compound at scale. We present a comparative study of four execution substrates: single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud virtual machines. We find up to $110\times$ variation in cold-start latency and a $1.8\times$ spread in projected worker-hours for one million 150-step trajectories. Our results suggest that future coding-agent RL systems should optimize execution substrates as part of the training system itself, not merely as deployment plumbing.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that execution infrastructure choices (containers, sandboxes, Kubernetes, VMs) significantly impact coding-agent RL efficiency, with up to 110× variation in cold-start latency and 1.8× spread in projected worker-hours, suggesting infrastructure optimization should be integrated into training systems.
AI.motivation: Current coding-agent reinforcement learning treats execution infrastructure as an implementation detail rather than optimizing it, despite reliance on large numbers of interactive software rollouts. Measuring infrastructure overhead could reveal practical efficiency gains in RL post-training where small per-rollout savings compound at scale.
AI.method: The paper presents a comparative study of four execution substrates: single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud virtual machines, measuring their performance characteristics for coding-agent RL workloads.
AI.result: The study found up to 110× variation in cold-start latency across different execution substrates and a 1.8× spread in projected worker-hours for executing one million 150-step trajectories, showing significant performance differences.
AI.conclusion: Future coding-agent RL systems should optimize execution substrates as part of the training system itself rather than treating them merely as deployment plumbing, as infrastructure choices have substantial impact on efficiency that compounds at scale.

title: The Agentic Garden of Forking Paths
summary: Empirical research rarely admits a unique analysis. Different analytical choices can lead to different conclusions from the same data, yet these hidden forking paths are difficult to observe. We show that AI agents capture much of the analytical variation among human researchers while making these paths explicit. Across four high-stakes domains, assigning different personas is sufficient for AI agents to report divergent, often opposing, conclusions from the same data and question, with findings systematically aligned with those beliefs. In a study in which 42 human research teams analyzed the same immigration dataset, AI agents reproduced 72% of the human ideological gap in reported effect estimates. Despite reaching opposing conclusions, it is difficult to identify clear issues in each analysis based on the final AI reports: 86% passed independent AI review and 78% passed majority human expert review. These findings suggest that the central challenge is often not flawed analyses, but selective exploration and reporting from a large space of methodologically defensible analyses. AI agents may amplify this longstanding problem by making such exploration inexpensive and scalable. To address this, we introduce the m-value (multiverse value), the probability that an analysis path would produce a claim at least as extreme as the reported one. We further introduce Agentic Bootstrap, which estimates the m-value by using AI agents to sample plausible analysis paths. Applied to the human immigration study, 13.5% of reported human analyses fell in the most extreme 5% of the analysis space (m<0.05). Scientific evidence should therefore be evaluated not only by a single reported analysis but also by its position within the distribution of analyses that could reasonably have been reported. Agentic Bootstrap makes this distribution observable and turns it into a criterion for scientific credibility.

AI.tldr: AI agents can reproduce human ideological biases in data analysis, showing that selective exploration from defensible analytical choices (rather than flawed methods) drives divergent conclusions - requiring new metrics like m-value to assess scientific credibility.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem that empirical research often involves multiple defensible analytical choices ("forking paths") that remain hidden, leading to different conclusions from the same data. This selective exploration and reporting creates ideological gaps in scientific findings that are difficult to detect.
AI.method: Researchers used AI agents with different personas to analyze the same datasets across four domains. They compared AI results with 42 human research teams analyzing an immigration dataset. They introduced the m-value (multiverse value) and Agentic Bootstrap method, which uses AI agents to sample plausible analysis paths to estimate how extreme a reported finding is within the space of defensible analyses.
AI.result: AI agents reproduced 72% of the human ideological gap in reported effect estimates. Despite opposing conclusions, 86% of AI analyses passed independent AI review and 78% passed human expert review. 13.5% of reported human analyses in the immigration study fell in the most extreme 5% of the analysis space (m<0.05), indicating selective reporting.
AI.conclusion: Scientific evidence should be evaluated not just by single analyses but by their position within the distribution of reasonable analyses. AI agents both reveal and potentially amplify this problem by making selective exploration scalable. The m-value and Agentic Bootstrap provide tools to assess credibility by making this analytical distribution observable.

title: Conditional Inference Trees and Forests for Feature Selection
summary: Conditional inference trees (CIT) and conditional inference forests (CIF) reduce split-selection bias by testing features before choosing split thresholds, but repeated permutation tests and threshold searches can make these methods computationally expensive. We study CIT and CIF as top-$k$ feature-ranking methods for downstream prediction using real-data benchmarks, runtime ablations, and synthetic feature-recovery experiments. At a fixed node, if the features and permutation budget do not depend on the node responses, Bonferroni-corrected $+1$ Monte Carlo permutation $p$-values control nodewise rejection under the complete permutation null. CIF ranks 4th among 17 classification methods on 22 datasets and 3rd among 18 regression methods on 8 datasets. With Bonferroni correction held fixed, the CIF runtime ablations indicate that adaptive stopping and the number of thresholds searched have the largest measured effect on runtime: turning off adaptive stopping and using exact threshold search increase fitting time by 4.0--8.4$\times$ and 1.9--10.8$\times$, respectively, while downstream score changes are at most 0.011. Sparse high-$p$ simulations indicate that forest feature sampling can leave informative features out of many split decisions. Overall, the results support CIF as a top-$k$ feature-ranking method in the evaluated downstream prediction benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Conditional inference forests (CIF) are effective for top-k feature ranking in prediction tasks, with runtime optimizations (adaptive stopping and limited threshold search) providing significant speedups without compromising downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Conditional inference trees and forests reduce split-selection bias but can be computationally expensive due to repeated permutation tests and threshold searches. The authors aim to evaluate them as feature-ranking methods for downstream prediction tasks.
AI.method: The study uses real-data benchmarks (22 classification datasets, 8 regression datasets), runtime ablations, and synthetic feature-recovery experiments. They analyze CIF's performance as a top-k feature-ranking method and examine computational aspects like Bonferroni-corrected permutation p-values, adaptive stopping, and threshold search strategies.
AI.result: CIF ranks 4th among 17 classification methods and 3rd among 18 regression methods. Runtime optimizations (turning off adaptive stopping and using exact threshold search) increase fitting time by 4.0-8.4× and 1.9-10.8× respectively, with minimal downstream performance impact (≤0.011). Forest feature sampling can exclude informative features in high-dimensional sparse settings.
AI.conclusion: The results support CIF as an effective top-k feature-ranking method for downstream prediction tasks, with identified runtime optimizations that provide substantial computational benefits while maintaining performance.

title: Janus: a Playground for User-Involved Agentic Permission Management
comment: Code and data released on GitHub: https://github.com/GraceBrigham/Janus
summary: AI agents that autonomously execute tool calls on a user's behalf raise pressing questions about permission management: what role could users play, and what role should they play? Despite many proposed approaches, the user's role in agentic permission management remains under explored. We introduce Janus, a playground system for implementing and evaluating user-involved agentic permission management designs. Janus consists of two components: Janus-Core, a modular agentic system supporting a diverse spectrum of permission management designs, and Janus-Harness, an automated evaluation framework. Grounded in a conceptual model that identifies key design axes for user involvement, we implement six permission assistants spanning the design space and evaluate them across three scenarios and three synthetic responders. We demonstrate that user input is critical and can significantly strengthen privacy and security, that AI augmentation of user decisions can help reduce cognitive load, and that realistic user behavior including permission fatigue must be accounted for in system design. No single design performs optimally across all contexts, motivating a more principled and context-sensitive approach to deploying permission assistants in agentic systems. Janus is publicly available to support future investigation into this dimension of agentic system design.

AI.tldr: Janus is a system for exploring user-involved permission management in AI agents, showing that user input is critical for security but must be balanced with cognitive load concerns, with no single design working best in all contexts.
AI.motivation: AI agents that autonomously execute tool calls raise pressing permission management questions about user roles. Current approaches under-explore user involvement in agentic permission management systems.
AI.method: Introduce Janus playground system with two components: Janus-Core (modular agentic system supporting diverse permission management designs) and Janus-Harness (automated evaluation framework). Grounded in conceptual model identifying key design axes, implement six permission assistants spanning design space and evaluate across three scenarios with three synthetic responders.
AI.result: User input is critical and can significantly strengthen privacy and security. AI augmentation of user decisions can help reduce cognitive load. Realistic user behavior including permission fatigue must be accounted for. No single design performs optimally across all contexts.
AI.conclusion: Motivates more principled, context-sensitive approach to deploying permission assistants in agentic systems. Janus is publicly available to support future investigation into user involvement dimension of agentic system design.

title: On the Utility and Factual Reliability of Pruned Mixture-of-Experts Models in the Biomedical Domain
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models offer inference speedups via selective activation but impose substantial memory requirements because the whole network must remain loaded. Structured expert pruning is a practical approach for reducing deployment costs in resource-constrained settings. However, prior studies primarily evaluate benchmark utility, leaving the effect of pruning on factual reliability underexplored, particularly in high-stakes domains such as biomedicine. In this paper, we investigate how domain-specific expert pruning affects both utility and reliability. We assess four MoE models, six pruning methods, and multiple pruning ratios across generation and classification tasks under in-domain (biomedical) and cross-domain settings. Results reveal that moderate pruning preserves in-domain utility without immediate reliability decline, although hallucination risks increase at extreme pruning ratios. When shifting to the general domain, both utility and reliability degrade rapidly. These findings indicate that safe compression depends heavily on the task and domain. Evaluating pruned MoE models solely on utility is inadequate for high-stakes deployment without reliability assessment.

AI.tldr: Pruning Mixture-of-Experts models in biomedical domains shows moderate pruning preserves utility without immediate reliability issues, but utility and reliability degrade rapidly in cross-domain settings, indicating utility-only evaluation is insufficient for high-stakes deployment.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts models offer inference speedups but have high memory requirements. While structured expert pruning reduces deployment costs, prior studies only evaluate benchmark utility, leaving the effect of pruning on factual reliability in high-stakes domains like biomedicine underexplored.
AI.method: Investigated domain-specific expert pruning effects on both utility and reliability. Assessed four MoE models, six pruning methods, and multiple pruning ratios across generation and classification tasks under both in-domain (biomedical) and cross-domain settings.
AI.result: Moderate pruning preserves in-domain utility without immediate reliability decline, though hallucination risks increase at extreme pruning ratios. When shifting to general domain, both utility and reliability degrade rapidly. Safe compression depends heavily on task and domain.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating pruned MoE models solely on utility is inadequate for high-stakes deployment without reliability assessment. The findings indicate that comprehensive evaluation must consider both utility and factual reliability, particularly when deploying compressed models in critical domains like biomedicine.

title: Revisiting Chain-of-Thought Reasoning under Limited Supervision: Semi-supervised Chain-of-Thought Learning
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has emerged as an effective approach for activating latent reasoning capabilities in large language models. However, most existing CoT methods use reasoning chains mainly as inference-time prompts, while the generated reasoning traces are rarely reused as semi-supervised learning signals. In this report, we define \textbf{Semi-supervised Chain-of-Thought Learning} and propose \textbf{Semi-CoT}, a simple framework that uses unlabeled questions to construct pseudo reasoning supervision. Semi-CoT samples multiple pseudo-CoTs for each unlabeled question, estimates answer-level semantic entropy, and selects low-entropy reasoning chains as reliable pseudo-CoT demonstrations. This extends the self-training view of CoT from inference-time refinement to semi-supervised pseudo-supervision. Pilot experiments on AQuA, SVAMP, GSM8K, and MultiArith show that the entropy gate selects high-precision pseudo-CoTs, with pseudo-answer precision ranging from $91.36\%$ to $100\%$. Semi-CoT also gives small gains on SVAMP and GSM8K, while AQuA shows negative transfer and MultiArith reaches a ceiling. These results suggest that unlabeled questions can provide reliable pseudo reasoning signals, but their effective use still requires stronger demonstration selection or student training.

AI.tldr: Semi-CoT introduces semi-supervised learning for chain-of-thought reasoning by using unlabeled questions to create pseudo reasoning supervision through entropy-based selection of reliable reasoning chains.
AI.motivation: Current CoT methods mainly use reasoning chains as inference-time prompts but rarely reuse generated reasoning traces as learning signals, wasting potential semi-supervised learning opportunities from unlabeled data.
AI.method: Semi-CoT samples multiple pseudo-CoTs for each unlabeled question, estimates answer-level semantic entropy, and selects low-entropy reasoning chains as reliable pseudo-CoT demonstrations, extending self-training from inference-time refinement to semi-supervised pseudo-supervision.
AI.result: Entropy gate selects high-precision pseudo-CoTs (91.36%-100% pseudo-answer precision). Semi-CoT gives small gains on SVAMP and GSM8K, but shows negative transfer on AQuA and ceiling effect on MultiArith.
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled questions can provide reliable pseudo reasoning signals, but effective use requires stronger demonstration selection or student training methods to fully leverage semi-supervised CoT learning.

title: Geometry-Aware R-Structured Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
summary:
We propose a novel hybrid neural architecture, the Geometry-aware R-Structured Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (GRS-KAN), which integrates V.L.Rvachev's R-functions into the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) framework. The proposed approach combines two complementary modeling mechanisms: smooth nonlinear structure is learned by KAN branches, while known geometric or logical constraints are encoded analytically using differentiable R-functions. This enables explicit representation of discontinuities, feasible regions, and implicit geometric boundaries within a trainable neural architecture.
  The framework implements differentiable logical operations through R-conjunctions and R-disjunctions, allowing complex geometric supports to be represented analytically and incorporated directly into regression models. Several GRS-KAN variants are introduced, including additive, multiplicative, and agnostic branch-weighted architectures.
  The method is demonstrated on regression problems involving discontinuities with circular and rectangular supports. Numerical experiments show that explicit geometric encoding substantially improves predictive accuracy and boundary localization compared with standard KANs. In the considered benchmarks, geometry-aware GRS-KAN models reduce test RMSE by up to 67% while simultaneously improving interpretability through explicit analytical representation of the learned geometric structure. The agnostic variant further demonstrates the ability to automatically determine whether geometric priors are beneficial for a given learning task.

AI.tldr: GRS-KAN is a hybrid neural network that combines Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with R-functions to integrate geometric constraints directly into learning, improving accuracy on problems with discontinuities while maintaining interpretability.
AI.motivation: Standard neural networks struggle with explicitly representing geometric constraints, discontinuities, and feasible regions. There's a need for architectures that can incorporate known geometric or logical constraints analytically while maintaining differentiability and interpretability.
AI.method: Integrates V.L. Rvachev's R-functions into the KAN framework, creating hybrid architectures where smooth nonlinear structure is learned by KAN branches while geometric constraints are encoded analytically using differentiable R-functions. Introduces GRS-KAN variants including additive, multiplicative, and agnostic branch-weighted architectures that implement differentiable logical operations through R-conjunctions and R-disjunctions.
AI.result: Geometry-aware GRS-KAN models substantially improve predictive accuracy and boundary localization compared to standard KANs, reducing test RMSE by up to 67% on benchmarks with circular and rectangular discontinuities. The agnostic variant can automatically determine whether geometric priors benefit a given learning task while improving interpretability through explicit analytical representation of learned geometric structure.
AI.conclusion: GRS-KAN successfully bridges the gap between analytical geometric modeling and data-driven learning by explicitly encoding geometric constraints within trainable neural architectures, leading to better performance on problems with discontinuities and improved model interpretability.

title: OPINE-World: Programmatic World Modeling with Ontology-error-Prioritized Interactive Exploration
summary: Learning how an environment behaves from interaction is central to building agents that adapt to unfamiliar tasks. World models learned with deep networks are flexible but data-hungry and transfer poorly beyond their training distribution. Program-synthesized world models, written as source code by LLMs and refined through counterexample-guided inductive synthesis (CEGIS), are instead data-efficient and reusable, yet they have been demonstrated mainly on structured-state worlds with a given object vocabulary, and a single program search does not scale to pixel-rendered environments whose object structure must be hypothesized flexibly. We introduce OPINE-World, an LLM agent that learns an object-centric programmatic world model online from interaction. OPINE-World couples two cooperating agents in a loop of hypothesis and test, one acting in the environment and one synthesizing the model in code with replay verification and model-based planning, and it steers exploration with a Bayesian measure of object-type adequacy we call ontology error. We evaluate OPINE-World on ARC-AGI-3, a benchmark for skill-acquisition efficiency in which the object vocabulary, the goal, and the action semantics are withheld. OPINE-World solves 20 of 25 games without per-game training and reaches an action-efficiency score of 78.4 against the human baseline.

AI.tldr: OPINE-World is an LLM-powered agent that learns object-centric programmatic world models online through a two-agent loop of hypothesis and test, achieving high action-efficiency on ARC-AGI-3 without per-game training.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to learning world models have limitations: deep network models are data-hungry and poor at transfer, while program-synthesized models are data-efficient but don't scale well to pixel-rendered environments requiring flexible object structure hypothesis.
AI.method: OPINE-World uses two cooperating LLM agents in a hypothesis-test loop: one interacts with the environment while the other synthesizes the world model in code. The system employs replay verification, model-based planning, and steers exploration using Bayesian ontology error to measure object-type adequacy.
AI.result: The system solves 20 out of 25 games on the ARC-AGI-3 benchmark without per-game training and achieves an action-efficiency score of 78.4 against human baseline, demonstrating effective skill acquisition when object vocabulary, goals, and action semantics are withheld.
AI.conclusion: OPINE-World successfully combines the data-efficiency of programmatic models with the flexibility needed for pixel-based environments through its two-agent architecture and Bayesian ontology steering, advancing object-centric world model learning from interaction.

title: Token Geometry
summary: Language models learn continuous programs over discrete symbols, with the embedding table and LM-head acting as the read/write interface between them. We show that this interface has gradient geometry distinct from dense hidden weights which can be exploited to improve the Pareto frontier across supervised finetuning, RL, and pretraining, while only utilizing kilobytes of optimizer state. We introduce Ember, a lightweight optimizer for embedding and LM-head matrices that utilizes O(V + D) VRAM, instead of Adam's O(2VD), and forgoes the need to shard both token table optimizer states. We provide empirical evidence that Ember scales effectively across batch size and parameter count. We show that the optimization trajectory of tokens can be well described by a simple 1D ray, counter to the popular belief that neural net parameters navigate a heavily nonconvex landscape. We provide a principled view on the surprisingly narrow space of optimizers that suffice for Transformer training. Finally, we open-source our distributed Ember implementation that merges cleanly with existing ZeRO/FSDP setups to support further research at https://github.com/katop1234/ember

AI.tldr: Ember is a lightweight optimizer for embedding and LM-head matrices that uses O(V+D) VRAM instead of Adam's O(2VD), achieves better Pareto frontiers across training stages, and provides insights about optimization trajectories in language models.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the high memory requirements of Adam optimizer for embedding tables and LM-head matrices in language models, which require O(2VD) VRAM (where V is vocabulary size and D is embedding dimension). This creates practical challenges for training, especially the need to shard optimizer states across GPUs.
AI.method: The authors introduce Ember, a specialized optimizer for embedding and LM-head matrices. Ember utilizes only O(V + D) VRAM by exploiting the distinct gradient geometry of the embedding/LM-head interface (which acts as a read/write interface between continuous programs and discrete symbols). The method leverages the observation that optimization trajectories of tokens can be well described by a simple 1D ray.
AI.result: Empirical results show that Ember scales effectively across batch sizes and parameter counts. It improves the Pareto frontier across supervised finetuning, reinforcement learning, and pretraining while using only kilobytes of optimizer state. The authors provide a distributed implementation that integrates with existing ZeRO/FSDP setups.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that embedding and LM-head optimization has distinct geometric properties that can be exploited for efficiency gains. It challenges the common belief that neural net parameters navigate heavily nonconvex landscapes, showing instead that token optimization follows simple 1D rays. The paper provides a principled view on optimizer design for Transformers and opens up new directions for research.

title: Scaling Trends for Lie Detector Oversight in Preference Learning
summary: Deceptive behavior in LLMs is costly to monitor and prevent, motivating approaches such as Scalable Oversight via Lie Detectors (SOLiD) (Cundy & Gleave, 2025), which uses lie detectors to identify responses for review by high-cost labelers. In this paper, we scale SOLiD to larger models and evaluate it in more diverse and realistic preference-learning settings. We find favorable scaling: undetected deception drops from 34% for 1B-parameter models to 14% for 405B-parameter models at a detector true positive rate of 99%, and expensive human labelers can be removed entirely from the fine-tuning phase without a statistically significant increase in deception. However, SOLiD is sensitive to distribution shift between detector training and preference-training data, which can drive detector false positive rates to impractical levels.

AI.tldr: SOLiD (Scalable Oversight via Lie Detectors) shows favorable scaling with larger models - undetected deception drops from 34% to 14% for 1B to 405B parameter models, and human labelers can be removed from fine-tuning without significant deception increase, but it's sensitive to distribution shift causing high false positive rates.
AI.motivation: Deceptive behavior in LLMs is costly to monitor and prevent, creating a need for scalable oversight solutions like SOLiD that use lie detectors to identify problematic responses for review.
AI.method: SOLiD uses lie detectors to identify deceptive LLM responses for review by high-cost labelers. The approach scales the original SOLiD method to larger models (up to 405B parameters) and evaluates it in more diverse and realistic preference-learning settings.
AI.result: Favorable scaling: undetected deception drops from 34% for 1B-parameter models to 14% for 405B-parameter models at 99% detector true positive rate. Human labelers can be removed entirely from fine-tuning phase without statistically significant increase in deception. However, SOLiD is sensitive to distribution shift between detector training and preference-training data, driving detector false positive rates to impractical levels.
AI.conclusion: SOLiD shows promise for scalable oversight with favorable scaling properties and the ability to reduce reliance on expensive human labelers, but distribution shift remains a critical vulnerability that can undermine practical deployment due to high false positive rates.

title: Class-Grouped Normalized Momentum and Faster Hyperparameter Exploration to Tackle Class Imbalance in Federated Learning
summary: Class imbalance poses a critical challenge in federated learning (FL), where underrepresented classes suffer from poor predictive performance yet cannot be addressed by standard centralized techniques due to privacy and heterogeneity constraints. We propose FedCGNM (Federated Class-Grouped Normalized Momentum), a client-side optimizer in FL that partitions classes into a small number of groups based on minimum within-group variance, maintains a momentum per group, normalizes each group momentum to unit length, and uses the summation of the normalized group momentums as an update direction. This design both equalizes gradient magnitude across majority and minority groups and mitigates the noise inherent in rare-class gradients. We further provide a theoretical convergence analysis explicitly accounting for time-varying resampling-rates. Additionally, to efficiently optimize these rates in small-client regimes, we introduce FedHOO, an X-armed-bandit (XAB) based algorithm that exploits federated parallelism that evaluates many combinations of two candidate rates per client at linear cost. Empirical evaluation on four public long-tailed benchmarks and a proprietary chip-defect dataset demonstrates that FedCGNM consistently outperforms baselines, with FedHOO yielding further gains in small-scale federations.

AI.tldr: FedCGNM is a client-side optimizer for federated learning that addresses class imbalance by grouping classes, using normalized group momentum for updates, with FedHOO optimizing resampling rates via bandit algorithms.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance in federated learning (FL) causes poor performance for underrepresented classes, but standard centralized techniques cannot be used due to privacy and heterogeneity constraints in FL.
AI.method:
1) FedCGNM: Partitions classes into groups based on minimum within-group variance, maintains momentum per group, normalizes each group momentum to unit length, and sums normalized group momentums as update direction.
2) FedHOO: Uses X-armed-bandit (XAB) algorithm to efficiently optimize resampling rates in small-client regimes by evaluating many combinations of candidate rates per client at linear cost.
AI.result: FedCGNM consistently outperforms baselines on four public long-tailed benchmarks and a proprietary chip-defect dataset. FedHOO provides additional gains in small-scale federations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FedCGNM approach effectively addresses class imbalance in federated learning by equalizing gradient magnitudes across groups and reducing noise in rare-class gradients, with FedHOO further improving performance in small-client scenarios.

title: EO-Agents: A Three-Agent LLM Pipeline for Earth Observation Hypothesis Generation
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 AI for Science Workshop
summary: Large language models have recently been explored for scientific hypothesis generation, but most prior work relies on unstructured literature and free-form textual claims. We present a pipeline for Earth observation that grounds hypothesis generation directly in the NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph. A heterogeneous graph neural network trained on historical co-usage relations ranks candidate dataset pairings, and a three-agent LLM pipeline filters, generates, and evaluates structured research hypotheses. Applied to 1,475 NASA datasets, the system produces 160 hypotheses spanning multiple Earth-science domains, including ecohydrology, glaciology, aerosol--cloud interactions, vegetation phenology, and stratospheric chemistry. Model-predicted novel dataset pairings are rated nearly as plausible as held-out real co-usages from the literature, indicating that the pipeline surfaces scientifically coherent yet unexplored combinations. A 2*2*2 factorial experiment across GPT-5.2 and Claude Sonnet 4.6 shows that hypothesis rankings remain stable, while absolute scores depend strongly on judge identity, highlighting limitations of single-judge LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: A pipeline for generating Earth science hypotheses by combining knowledge graph analysis with LLM agents, producing 160 novel hypotheses from NASA datasets.
AI.motivation: Prior scientific hypothesis generation using LLMs relies on unstructured literature and free-form claims, lacking grounding in structured scientific data. The authors aim to create a more systematic approach by connecting hypothesis generation directly to NASA's Earth Observation Knowledge Graph.
AI.method: The pipeline first uses a heterogeneous graph neural network trained on historical dataset co-usage relations to rank candidate dataset pairings from NASA's Earth Observation Knowledge Graph. Then a three-agent LLM pipeline performs filtering, generation, and evaluation of structured research hypotheses.
AI.result: Applied to 1,475 NASA datasets, the system generated 160 hypotheses across multiple Earth-science domains including ecohydrology, glaciology, and stratospheric chemistry. Model-predicted novel pairings were rated nearly as plausible as real co-usages from literature. A factorial experiment showed hypothesis rankings remain stable across models but absolute scores depend heavily on judge identity.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline successfully generates scientifically coherent yet unexplored hypothesis combinations by grounding LLMs in structured knowledge graphs. However, LLM evaluation limitations are revealed through strong dependence on judge identity, highlighting the need for multi-judge evaluation approaches.

title: How to Allocate Your Tokens? Scaling Laws with Training Steps and Batch Size
summary: We propose a scaling law that takes into account model size and training data while explicitly splitting the latter into training steps and batch size (called three-term law). Fitting the proposed law on a large set of training runs, we find that it correctly recovers the scaling of the optimal batch size. Moreover, because it makes use of training runs with suboptimal batch size, our proposed law can be robustly fit with a significantly smaller amount of training runs. We further show that the three-term law can be used to derive scaling laws for suboptimal batch sizes, and that it matches previous empirical findings related to the critical batch size.

AI.tldr: Proposed a new three-term scaling law that accounts for model size, training steps, and batch size explicitly, enabling robust estimation with fewer training runs and recovering optimal batch size scaling.
AI.motivation: Current scaling laws don't explicitly separate batch size from other training parameters, requiring many optimal training runs for estimation and lacking insights into batch size effects.
AI.method: Developed a three-term scaling law that explicitly models model size, training steps, and batch size separately. Fitted this law on extensive training run data, including suboptimal batch sizes, to validate its ability to recover optimal batch size scaling.
AI.result: The three-term law successfully recovers optimal batch size scaling, can be fitted robustly with significantly fewer training runs (using suboptimal batches), derives scaling laws for suboptimal batch sizes, and matches previous empirical findings on critical batch size.
AI.conclusion: The proposed three-term scaling law provides a more comprehensive and efficient framework for understanding neural network scaling, particularly in batch size optimization, with practical benefits for training efficiency and resource allocation.

title: Hawk: Harnessing Hardware-Aware Knowledge for High-Performance NPU Kernel Generation
summary: Developing high-performance kernels for Neural Processing Units (NPUs) is a critical industry bottleneck, requiring developers to manually navigate implicit hardware constraints and strict memory hierarchies. While large language models offer immense automation potential, they fail catastrophically on NPUs due to a fundamental lack of hardware-specific priors. Naively transplanting code snippets from similar NPU kernels may pass the compiler, but it consistently triggers runtime crashes and performance degradation by blindly violating underlying hardware constraints. To overcome this, we introduce Hawk, a training-free framework that harnesses hardware-aware knowledge through three core modules: (1) Run-Time Knowledge Synthesis Module, which employs a Triple-Part Executable Knowledge Representation to inherently couple the error context with executable semantics; (2) Bottleneck-Aware Knowledge Retrieval Module, which implements a 2D-Retrieval paradigm to project queries into orthogonal syntactic and hardware-aligned semantic spaces; and (3) Effect-Driven Knowledge Distillation Module, which leverages LLM-driven semantic arbitration to continuously distill the knowledge by pruning errors and consolidating redundancies based on the empirical execution feedback. Extensive evaluations on real-world NPU workloads demonstrate that Hawk elevates generation accuracy from 49.4% to 80.0%, while achieving up to a 2.2x execution speedup over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Hawk is a training-free framework that uses hardware-aware knowledge to generate high-performance NPU kernels, improving generation accuracy from 49.4% to 80.0% and achieving up to 2.2x speedup over SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs fail catastrophically on NPU kernel development because they lack hardware-specific priors. Naively transplanting code snippets leads to runtime crashes and performance degradation by violating hardware constraints. The industry bottleneck is that developers must manually navigate implicit hardware constraints and strict memory hierarchies.
AI.method: Hawk uses three core modules: (1) Run-Time Knowledge Synthesis Module with Triple-Part Executable Knowledge Representation to couple error context with executable semantics; (2) Bottleneck-Aware Knowledge Retrieval Module with 2D-Retrieval to project queries into orthogonal syntactic and hardware-aligned semantic spaces; (3) Effect-Driven Knowledge Distillation Module using LLM-driven semantic arbitration to prune errors and consolidate redundancies based on execution feedback.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on real-world NPU workloads show Hawk elevates generation accuracy from 49.4% to 80.0% (significant 30.6% improvement) while achieving up to 2.2x execution speedup over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Hawk successfully addresses the NPU kernel development bottleneck by incorporating hardware-aware knowledge through its novel training-free framework, overcoming the limitations of LLMs that lack hardware-specific priors and enabling reliable, high-performance kernel generation.

title: Don't Let Gains FADE: Breaking Down Policy Gradient Weights in RL
summary: Reinforcement learning post-training dramatically improves LLM reasoning, but suffers from training instability and diversity collapse. Advantage functions offer an appealing fix: they reshape the training objective, reweight which rollouts drive learning, and are trivial to implement. Yet a proliferation of methods makes it unclear which advantage to use and when. We cut through the confusion with a unifying framework that decomposes any advantage into its positive and negative gradient mass along two orthogonal axes. On the sign axis, imbalanced updates collapse either entropy or weight geometry. On the difficulty axis, hard-problem focus sharpens signal but costs sample size. Both trade-offs shift during training: exploration favors balance and hard focus; exploitation favors suppression and medium focus. This motivates FADE (Focal Advantage with Dynamic Entropy), a self-adapting advantage that reads training dynamics to schedule the gradient weight automatically. FADE reaches peak pass@1 20k steps earlier than the best static baseline at the 7B scale and 2k steps earlier at the 32B , while achieving the best accuracy-diversity trade-off across all pass@k on LiveCodeBench and AIME.

AI.tldr: FADE (Focal Advantage with Dynamic Entropy) is a self-adapting advantage function for RL post-training of LLMs that dynamically adjusts gradient weights based on training dynamics, achieving better performance-diversity trade-offs than static baselines.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning post-training improves LLM reasoning but suffers from training instability and diversity collapse. While advantage functions offer a potential solution, the proliferation of methods creates confusion about which advantage to use and when.
AI.method: Proposes a unifying framework that decomposes any advantage into positive/negative gradient mass along two orthogonal axes (sign and difficulty). Introduces FADE, a self-adapting advantage function that reads training dynamics to automatically schedule gradient weights, balancing exploration vs exploitation phases.
AI.result: FADE reaches peak performance 20k steps earlier than best static baseline at 7B scale and 2k steps earlier at 32B scale, while achieving the best accuracy-diversity trade-off across all pass@k metrics on LiveCodeBench and AIME benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: FADE's dynamic adaptation of advantage functions based on training phase (exploration vs exploitation) provides superior performance-diversity trade-offs and faster convergence compared to static advantage methods, effectively addressing RL post-training instability and diversity collapse issues.

title: Safe and Adaptive Cloud Healing: Verifying LLM-Generated Recovery Plans with a Neural-Symbolic World Model
summary: As the scale and complexity of cloud-based AI systems continue to escalate, ensuring service reliability through rapid fault detection and adaptive recovery has become a critical challenge. While existing approaches integrate Large Language Models (LLMs) for semantic understanding and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for policy optimization, they often rely on sequential, loosely coupled architectures that underutilize the generative and reasoning capabilities of LLMs. In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift with PASE, a Planning-Aware Semantic self-healing engine, a novel fault self-healing framework that reconceptualizes recovery as a neuro-symbolic program synthesis task. PASE employs an LLM as a core Plan Synthesis Engine to generate structured recovery plans from a library of semantic primitives. A Neural-Symbolic World Model verifies plan feasibility through simulation, while a Meta-Prompt Optimizer, trained via DRL, learns to generate optimal prompts that guide the LLM's planning process. This tight reason-plan-verify-adapt loop enables dynamic, context-aware recovery strategy generation beyond predefined action spaces. Experiments on a real-world cloud fault injection dataset demonstrate that PASE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, reducing average system recovery time by over 40% and improving fault detection accuracy in unknown fault scenarios. Our framework advances autonomous system management by unifying LLM-based reasoning with model-assisted verification and meta-learned guidance.

AI.tldr: PASE is a neuro-symbolic fault self-healing framework that uses LLMs as a plan synthesis engine, neural-symbolic verification, and meta-learned prompts to significantly improve cloud AI system recovery times.
AI.motivation: Current cloud-based AI systems face reliability challenges with fault detection and recovery. Existing approaches using LLMs and DRL are often sequential and loosely coupled, failing to fully leverage LLMs' generative and reasoning capabilities for dynamic recovery.
AI.method: PASE frames recovery as neuro-symbolic program synthesis: 1) LLM as Plan Synthesis Engine generates structured recovery plans from semantic primitives, 2) Neural-Symbolic World Model verifies plan feasibility through simulation, 3) Meta-Prompt Optimizer (trained via DRL) learns optimal prompts to guide LLM planning, forming a tight reason-plan-verify-adapt loop.
AI.result: On real-world cloud fault injection datasets, PASE reduced average system recovery time by over 40% and improved fault detection accuracy in unknown fault scenarios, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: PASE advances autonomous system management by unifying LLM-based reasoning with model-assisted verification and meta-learned guidance, representing a paradigm shift from sequential architectures to integrated neuro-symbolic program synthesis for fault recovery.

title: Unveiling the Non-Monotonic Effect of Privacy on Generalization under Byzantine Robustness
summary: Recent work has established a fundamental trilemma between Byzantine robustness, local differential privacy (LDP), and optimization error in distributed learning. We show that this trilemma does not universally extend to generalization error, but instead depends critically on the privacy regime. Specifically, in the high-noise regime (strong privacy), we prove that increasing privacy reduces the generalization error, i.e., there is no tension between robustness and privacy. In the low-noise regime (weaker privacy), however, the tension between robustness and privacy reappears and increasing privacy indeed degrades generalization. Our theory explains this surprising non-monotonic behavior of the generalization error via matching lower and upper bounds on the algorithmic stability of Byzantine-robust distributed learning under LDP constraints. We corroborate and further analyze these theoretical findings with empirical evaluations.

AI.tldr: Byzantine-robust distributed learning under LDP shows surprising non-monotonic relationship: in high-noise regime, increased privacy improves generalization, while in low-noise regime, it degrades generalization.
AI.motivation: Previous work established a fundamental trilemma between Byzantine robustness, local differential privacy, and optimization error in distributed learning, suggesting inherent tensions between these desirable properties.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using matching lower and upper bounds on algorithmic stability of Byzantine-robust distributed learning under LDP constraints, complemented by empirical evaluations.
AI.result: The trilemma does not universally extend to generalization error - it depends on privacy regime: high-noise regime shows no tension (increased privacy reduces generalization error), while low-noise regime shows tension (increased privacy degrades generalization).
AI.conclusion: The relationship between privacy and generalization in Byzantine-robust distributed learning is non-monotonic and regime-dependent, challenging previous assumptions about inherent trade-offs between robustness and privacy.

title: SemHash-LLM: A Multi-Granularity Semantic Hashing Framework for Document Deduplication
summary: Large scale document deduplication must preserve semantic equivalence while remaining efficient over massive corpora. We present SemHash LLM, a multi granularity framework that unifies semantic projection hashing, attention weighted MinHash, contrastive boundary learning, and selective LLM based adjudication. The method combines character, token, and document level signals through gated fusion, then applies a cascaded filtering pipeline for efficient candidate reduction. Semantic projection hashing learns compact binary codes in distilled LLM embedding space, while attention weighted Min- Hash suppresses boilerplate and emphasizes informative content. Adaptive decision boundaries and uncertainty estimation further improve robustness across template pollution, short text perturbation, containment, and viral fragments. Experiments show that SemHash LLM achieves strong duplicate detection quality with less than one percent neural verification cost.

AI.tldr: SemHash LLM is an efficient multi-granularity document deduplication framework that combines semantic hashing, weighted MinHash, and selective LLM adjudication to detect duplicates with minimal computational cost.
AI.motivation: Large-scale document deduplication requires balancing semantic equivalence preservation with computational efficiency across massive corpora. Existing approaches struggle to maintain both quality and speed at scale.
AI.method: A multi-granularity framework that unifies: 1) semantic projection hashing for compact binary codes in distilled LLM space, 2) attention-weighted MinHash to emphasize informative content over boilerplate, 3) contrastive boundary learning, and 4) selective LLM-based adjudication. Uses gated fusion of character/token/document signals with cascaded filtering.
AI.result: Achieves strong duplicate detection quality with less than 1% neural verification cost. Robust against template pollution, short text perturbation, containment issues, and viral fragments.
AI.conclusion: SemHash LLM effectively balances accuracy and efficiency for large-scale deduplication by combining multiple granularity signals with intelligent filtering and selective use of expensive LLM verification.

title: Towards Learning Representations of Policies in Two-Player Zero-Sum Imperfect-Information Games
summary:
We investigate the problem of learning useful policy representations (embeddings) in two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games. We make three contributions: First, we introduce methods of creating datasets of policies for a given game. Second, we propose methods to learn policy representations. Third, we introduce downstream tasks to evaluate the effectiveness of such representations.
  We evaluate each dataset method, embedding method, and downstream task on Kuhn and Leduc Poker. Although our methods are very basic, we demonstrate that useful behavioral representations are present in the learned embeddings. To our knowledge, this work is among the first to systematically compare self-supervised learning techniques for learning policy representations in games. Our code is available at https://github.com/VitamintK/ssl-project for others to extend.

AI.tldr: The paper explores self-supervised learning techniques for creating policy embeddings in two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games like poker, introducing dataset creation methods, embedding learning approaches, and evaluation tasks.
AI.motivation: To develop effective methods for learning policy representations (embeddings) in imperfect-information games, where understanding opponent behavior is crucial but challenging due to hidden information and strategic complexity.
AI.method: Three-part approach: 1) Methods for creating datasets of policies for a given game, 2) Methods to learn policy representations from these datasets, 3) Downstream tasks to evaluate representation effectiveness, evaluated on Kuhn and Leduc Poker games.
AI.result: The basic methods successfully demonstrated that useful behavioral representations exist in the learned embeddings, providing evidence that self-supervised learning can capture meaningful policy information in imperfect-information games.
AI.conclusion: This work represents one of the first systematic comparisons of self-supervised learning techniques for policy representations in games, establishing a foundation for future research and providing open-source code for community extension.

title: Profit-Based Counterfactual Explanations for Product Improvement: A Case Study of Manga Sales in Japan
summary: Counterfactual explanation (CE) is widely used to enhance the interpretability of machine learning models and support data-driven decision-making based on model predictions. However, existing CE methods typically require two exogenously specified inputs: a desired output value (target) and a distance function that quantifies changes in explanatory variables. In regression settings, neither the validity of target specification nor the practical interpretation of the distance metric has been sufficiently addressed. Furthermore, most existing CE methods focus on altering predictions rather than optimizing a decision objective, even though real-world decision-making often requires explicit objective maximization. To address these limitations, we formulate CE as a profit maximization problem in management and marketing contexts and propose a framework termed profit-based counterfactual explanation (PBCE). PBCE eliminates the need for exogenous target specification by directly maximizing profit as the primary optimization objective. Concurrently, the distance term is reinterpreted as the cost of modifying product attributes, providing a clear and economically grounded interpretation.

AI.tldr: PBCE is a novel counterfactual explanation framework that reformulates CE as profit maximization in business contexts, eliminating the need for exogenous target specification and reinterpreting distance metrics as modification costs.
AI.motivation: Existing CE methods have limitations: they require exogenously specified target values and distance functions, with unclear practical interpretation in regression settings. Most CE methods focus on altering predictions rather than optimizing real-world decision objectives like profit maximization.
AI.method: Proposes Profit-Based Counterfactual Explanation (PBCE) framework that formulates CE as a profit maximization problem. Eliminates exogenous target specification by using profit as the primary optimization objective. Reinterprets distance metric as the cost of modifying product attributes, providing economic grounding.
AI.result: The PBCE framework provides a more practical and interpretable approach for business applications by directly optimizing profit objectives and giving clear economic meaning to attribute modifications through cost-based distance interpretation.
AI.conclusion: PBCE addresses key limitations of traditional CE methods by integrating profit maximization objectives and economically grounded distance interpretations, making CE more practical for real-world business decision-making in management and marketing contexts.

title: The risk of KV cache compression
summary: Transformer inference on long sequences is expensive because softmax attention repeatedly reads from a large KV cache. The prevalent approach to this bottleneck is KV cache compression, which replaces the full cache with a compact summary. Despite its practical importance, the design of such summaries is largely driven by empirical experimentation. On the theoretical side, existing results show that KV cache compression can be impossible in the worst case, but offer little systematic guidance for designing algorithms in regimes where accurate compression is possible. We bridge this gap by characterizing the minimax risk of KV cache compression in terms of the intrinsic compressibility of a cache, revealing when and how accurate compression is possible. These results yield novel design principles for KV cache compression under causal masking that map efficiently to prefill and autoregressive decoding while achieving minimax-optimal risk. We instantiate these principles in a practical algorithm and report promising performance on LongBench in targeted experiments. Overall, our results provide a principled avenue for practical KV cache compression with theoretical guarantees.

AI.tldr: Transformer KV cache compression for long sequences is analyzed theoretically, revealing when accurate compression is possible and providing minimax-optimal compression algorithms with practical implementations.
AI.motivation: Transformer inference on long sequences is expensive due to softmax attention repeatedly reading from large KV caches. Current KV cache compression methods are empirically driven with limited theoretical guidance, despite their practical importance.
AI.method: Characterizes minimax risk of KV cache compression in terms of intrinsic cache compressibility, providing theoretical analysis of when accurate compression is possible. Derives design principles for KV cache compression under causal masking that map efficiently to prefill and autoregressive decoding while achieving minimax-optimal risk.
AI.result: Provides systematic theoretical guidance for KV cache compression design, revealing regimes where accurate compression is possible. Instantiates principles in a practical algorithm that shows promising performance on LongBench in targeted experiments.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges the gap between empirical and theoretical approaches to KV cache compression, providing a principled avenue for practical compression with theoretical guarantees, addressing the computational bottleneck in long-sequence Transformer inference.

title: Scaling with Confidence: Calibrating Confidence of LLMs for Adaptive Test Time Scaling
summary: Training large language models (LLMs) with reinforcement learning (RL) has significantly advanced their performance on reasoning and question-answering tasks. However, prevailing RL reward designs typically prioritize response correctness, neglecting to incentivize models to express their confidence accurately. This leads to a critical problem: performance gains are often accompanied by poor calibration between confidence and accuracy, misleading models to overconfidently hallucinate when uncertain. To address this limitation, we propose $\textbf{C}$orrectness and $\textbf{C}$onfidence $\textbf{C}$alibration $\textbf{R}$einforcement $\textbf{L}$earning ($\textbf{C3RL}$), a novel RL algorithm integrating correctness, calibration and dataset-informed reference accuracy rewards together. Comprehensive evaluation across 8 text and multimodal datasets demonstrates that C3RL enhances calibration without sacrificing accuracy, outperforming the current state-of-the-art method in both performance and calibration metrics. Utilizing the well-calibrated verbalized confidence from C3RL, we further introduce $\textbf{C}$onfidence-based $\textbf{A}$daptive Test Time $\textbf{S}$caling ($\textbf{CAS}$), an adjustable inference-time strategy that allocates computational resources based on response confidence. Experiments show that CAS surpasses majority voting on both in-domain and out-of-domain datasets while reducing the inference budget by up to 12.33 times. We believe the synergy of C3RL and CAS paves the way for deploying more reliable and resource-efficient LLMs. The code, data and models will be released.

AI.tldr: C3RL is a novel RL algorithm that improves LLM calibration by rewarding both correctness and confidence alignment, and CAS is an inference strategy that allocates compute based on confidence.
AI.motivation: Current RL reward designs for LLMs prioritize correctness but neglect confidence calibration, causing models to become overconfident and hallucinate when uncertain, undermining reliability.
AI.method: Proposes C3RL algorithm with three reward components: correctness, confidence calibration, and dataset-informed reference accuracy. Also introduces CAS for adaptive inference-time resource allocation based on response confidence.
AI.result: C3RL improves calibration without sacrificing accuracy across 8 text and multimodal datasets, outperforming SOTA. CAS reduces inference budget by up to 12.33x while outperforming majority voting on in/out-of-domain datasets.
AI.conclusion: The synergy of C3RL and CAS enables more reliable and resource-efficient LLMs by ensuring well-calibrated confidence and adaptive compute allocation.

title: Multi-Head Recurrent Memory Agents
summary: Recurrent memory agents extend LLMs to arbitrarily long contexts by iteratively consolidating input into a fixed-size memory window. Despite their scalability, these agents exhibit a well-documented reliability problem: end-to-end performance degrades systematically as context length grows. We diagnose this failure by decomposing performance into two factors--memory capture and memory retention--and quantitatively confirm that retention is the dominant bottleneck. Retention collapses because existing designs maintain memory as a monolithic text block, forcing every update to risk overwriting previously retained content. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Multi-Head Recurrent Memory (MHM), a general, training-free framework that partitions memory into independent heads governed by a stage-wise select-then-update strategy. At each step, exactly one head is selected for update while the remaining heads are structurally shielded from overwriting, shifting the burden of retention from model behavior to architectural design. As a lightweight instantiation, we introduce Least-Recently-Updated MHM (MHM-LRU), which guarantees uniform head utilization with zero additional token overhead. Extensive experiments on long-context benchmarks show that MHM-LRU substantially improves both retention and end-to-end accuracy across the 100K--1M token range, where baselines degrade sharply. On RULER-HQA at 896K tokens, MHM-LRU improves the memory retention rate from less than 30% to 73.96%. These gains generalize across model families, scales, and task types, positioning architectural optimization as a practical and cost-efficient path toward reliable long-context recurrent memory.

AI.tldr: A new framework (MHM-LRU) partitions recurrent memory into independent heads with selective updates to solve retention problems in long-context LLMs, improving retention rates from <30% to 74% at 896K tokens.
AI.motivation: Recurrent memory agents scale LLMs to long contexts but suffer from systematic performance degradation as context length increases, primarily due to poor memory retention caused by monolithic memory structures that risk overwriting previously stored content.
AI.method: Proposed Multi-Head Recurrent Memory (MHM) partitions memory into independent heads with a stage-wise select-then-update strategy where only one head is updated per step, shielding other heads from overwriting. Instantiated as MHM-LRU with least-recently-updated selection for uniform head utilization.
AI.result: MHM-LRU substantially improves retention and end-to-end accuracy across 100K-1M token range. On RULER-HQA at 896K tokens, improved memory retention rate from <30% to 73.96%. Gains generalize across model families, scales, and task types.
AI.conclusion: Architectural optimization via memory partitioning and selective updates is a practical, cost-efficient path toward reliable long-context recurrent memory, addressing the fundamental retention bottleneck in existing recurrent memory designs.

title: Spatial Support Matters: Geometry-Aware Graph Fusion for Rainfall Field Reconstruction
summary: Fine-scale rainfall reconstruction is critical for urban flood modeling, but real rainfall sensing systems observe the field through incompatible spatial supports: gauges measure points, microwave links measure paths, and radar/satellite products measure gridded areas. These differences in measurement support impose geometrically distinct constraints on the rainfall field, yet existing heterogeneous graph approaches reconcile such sources in feature space, giving each its own embedding while discarding the geometry of its support. We propose a geometry-aware multi-support heterogeneous graph neural network that represents each observation according to its support type (0D point, 1D line, or 2D grid) as a distinct node layer, and fuses them through cross-support message passing into a point-support prediction layer from which the field is reconstructed. An inductive masked-node formulation decouples prediction resolution from sensing resolution, allowing the same trained model to reconstruct the field at user-defined target locations or display grids. On Singapore data, the proposed method reduces RMSE by 23.2\% over the classical interpolation baseline, inverse-distance weighting, and consistently outperforms other neural architectures such as convolutional fusion and support-agnostic heterogeneous graph baselines. A generalization study using data from Sydney, Australia lets us characterize when multi-support fusion helps: the available skill appears to depend on gauge spacing relative to the spatial correlation length of the field, so fusion delivers the largest gains where the field is under-sampled relative to its correlation length and little when it is already resolved. Code and models will be open-sourced upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware heterogeneous graph neural network for rainfall reconstruction that fuses point, line, and grid observations while preserving their distinct spatial support geometries.
