AI.motivation: Current rainfall sensing systems have incompatible spatial supports (points from gauges, lines from microwave links, grids from radar/satellite), but existing methods reconcile sources in feature space while discarding their geometric constraints, limiting reconstruction accuracy.
AI.method: Propose a multi-support heterogeneous graph neural network with distinct node layers for each support type (0D point, 1D line, 2D grid) and cross-support message passing to fuse them into point-support predictions. Uses inductive masked-node formulation to decouple prediction resolution from sensing resolution.
AI.result: On Singapore data, reduces RMSE by 23.2% over inverse-distance weighting baseline and outperforms convolutional fusion and support-agnostic heterogeneous graph baselines. Generalization to Sydney data shows fusion gains depend on gauge spacing relative to spatial correlation length - largest improvements when field is under-sampled relative to correlation length.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware multi-support fusion significantly improves fine-scale rainfall reconstruction, with gains dependent on sampling density relative to field correlation structure. The approach handles diverse observation supports while enabling flexible prediction at user-defined locations.

title: Wind-Aware Reinforcement Learning Control of a Small Quadrotor Using Learned Onboard Wind Estimation in Simulated Atmospheric Turbulence
summary: Small multirotor aircraft are increasingly tasked with operations in the atmospheric boundary layer, where turbulent winds comparable to the vehicle's airspeed degrade trajectory tracking and can defeat conventional feedback control. This work illustrates a two-stage learning pipeline that first estimates the local wind from onboard kinematics and dynamics and then exploits that estimate inside a reinforcement learning (RL) flight controller. The wind estimator, an attention-augmented gated recurrent network trained on thousands of simulated flights through von Karman turbulence with power-law shear and veer, recovers the horizontal wind vector with a per-flight root-mean-square error of 0.40 m/s and a direction error of 3.2 degrees on unseen wind regimes, an accuracy near the floor imposed by unresolved turbulence, and generalizes to vertical ascent profiles with a skill score of 0.861 over a constant-wind reference. A proximal policy optimization controller receiving the frozen estimator's output reduces horizontal trajectory tracking error by 48% relative to a wind-blind proportional-derivative baseline across mean winds of 4 m/s to 12 m/s, winning on 100% of evaluation episodes. A three-way ablation decomposes this improvement into a kinematic component, available without wind information, and a wind-perception component; the perception share rises with wind speed, from small in light winds toward roughly half the total benefit in strong winds, consistent with the quadratic scaling of aerodynamic drag. The controller degrades gracefully on out-of-distribution winds of 13 m/s to 15 m/s, where the baseline fails catastrophically.

AI.tldr: A two-stage learning pipeline combines wind estimation with reinforcement learning to improve multirotor control in turbulent winds.
AI.motivation: Small multirotors operating in turbulent atmospheric boundary layers experience degraded trajectory tracking that conventional feedback control cannot handle effectively.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: 1) Attention-augmented gated recurrent network estimates local wind from onboard kinematics/dynamics, trained on simulated flights through von Karman turbulence; 2) Proximal policy optimization RL controller uses wind estimates for improved control.
AI.result: Wind estimator achieves 0.40 m/s RMS error and 3.2° direction error; RL controller reduces horizontal trajectory tracking error by 48% compared to wind-blind baseline, winning 100% of evaluation episodes; degrades gracefully on out-of-distribution winds where baseline fails.
AI.conclusion: The integrated learning approach significantly improves multirotor control in turbulent conditions by combining accurate wind estimation with reinforcement learning, with wind perception becoming increasingly important at higher wind speeds.

title: Autonomous discovery of traffic laws with AI traffic scientists
summary: Universal traffic laws describe recurrent patterns in congestion, mobility and driving behavior across cities, providing a scientific basis for transportation planning, management and control. Their discovery, however, remains expert-driven, requiring candidate regularities to be identified from heterogeneous observational evidence or validated through intervention experiments. Although autonomous artificial intelligence (AI) systems have advanced scientific discovery in controlled laboratory settings, extending them to complex transportation domains remains a challenge. Here we present TrafficSci, an agentic AI system that formulates traffic-law discovery as an iterative, auditable workflow integrating evidence scoping, critic-judge hypothesis induction, and observational-interventional validation. Across four case studies spanning population, network, control and trajectory scales, TrafficSci autonomously rediscovers three established traffic laws and identifies an unreported intrinsic temporal memory scale in urban driving behavior, statistically consistent across eight cities and two trajectory datasets. TrafficSci provides a route for extending AI-driven scientific discovery from controlled domains to complex urban systems.

AI.tldr: TrafficSci is an AI system that autonomously discovers universal traffic laws by integrating evidence scoping, hypothesis generation, and validation across multiple data scales, rediscovering established laws and identifying a new temporal memory pattern in driving behavior.
AI.motivation: Current traffic law discovery remains expert-driven and limited, requiring manual identification from heterogeneous data or experimental validation. While AI has advanced scientific discovery in controlled lab settings, extending it to complex transportation domains remains challenging.
AI.method: TrafficSci formulates traffic-law discovery as an iterative, auditable workflow with three key components: 1) evidence scoping, 2) critic-judge hypothesis induction, and 3) observational-interventional validation. The system operates across population, network, control, and trajectory scales.
AI.result: Across four case studies, TrafficSci autonomously rediscovers three established traffic laws and identifies a previously unreported intrinsic temporal memory scale in urban driving behavior. This finding is statistically consistent across eight cities and two trajectory datasets.
AI.conclusion: TrafficSci provides a route for extending AI-driven scientific discovery from controlled domains to complex urban systems, demonstrating the potential for autonomous discovery of universal patterns in transportation systems.

title: Certified World Models as Sensing Clocks: Drift-Aware Deadlines for Active Perception
summary: Certified world models estimate how long their predictions remain valid. We turn this validity horizon into an operational sensing clock: a rule for when an agent should stop coasting and re-sense. Starting from an audited equivariant world model, we derive a deadline for no-sensing intervals and show that deployable deadlines in learned world models must be drift-aware: on-manifold Lyapunov rates alone overestimate coasting validity, while calibrated native rollout-drift envelopes carry the deployed guarantee. On a frozen 3D VN-JEPA model, the resulting clock controls held-out interval-simultaneous certificate violation across seeds and data shards. In a cue-conditioned theorem-bed (a synthetic bench where all schedulers share the exact model, isolating the scheduling rule), the clock remains valid on the deployment distribution and substantially reduces eventful-tail violations relative to exact-mixture expected-belief scheduling at matched sensing budget. We also report limits: in the short-horizon frozen VN-JEPA regime, empirical conformal horizons match the deployed clock on validity and budget, and a partial-reset exploration finds no clean budget-matched advantage for the spectral term. Thus the contribution is a certified sensing-clock primitive and drift-aware deployment method, not a claim that spectral clocks empirically dominate all non-spectral schedulers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a certified sensing clock that determines when autonomous agents should stop coasting on world model predictions and re-sense, using drift-aware deadlines derived from equivariant world models to maintain validity guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing world models provide validity horizons for predictions, but these don't directly translate to practical operational rules for when agents should interrupt coasting to gather new sensory information. There's a need to convert theoretical validity guarantees into deployable sensing schedules that maintain certification while being computationally practical.
AI.method: Starting from audited equivariant world models, the authors derive drift-aware deadlines for no-sensing intervals. They show that deployable deadlines must account for drift: on-manifold Lyapunov rates alone overestimate coasting validity, while calibrated native rollout-drift envelopes maintain deployed guarantees. They validate this on a frozen 3D VN-JEPA model and in a cue-conditioned theorem-bed that isolates the scheduling rule.
AI.result: The sensing clock successfully controls held-out interval-simultaneous certificate violations across seeds and data shards. In the theorem-bed, it remains valid on deployment distribution and substantially reduces eventful-tail violations compared to exact-mixture expected-belief scheduling at matched sensing budget. However, in short-horizon frozen VN-JEPA, empirical conformal horizons match the deployed clock, and no clean budget-matched advantage is found for the spectral term.
AI.conclusion: The main contribution is a certified sensing-clock primitive and drift-aware deployment method, not a claim that spectral clocks empirically dominate all non-spectral schedulers. The work provides a practical tool for converting world model validity horizons into operational sensing schedules with guaranteed performance.

title: Diverse Evidence, Better Forecasts: Multi-Agent Deliberation Under Information Asymmetry
summary: Multi-agent systems are increasingly used for forecasting future events, as deliberation among multiple LLMs is believed to improve reasoning and calibration. Yet existing approaches overlook a critical design choice: what information each agent receives. When all agents are given identical evidence, deliberation collapses into herding rather than genuine belief revision, leaving multi-agent systems little better than a single agent. We identify this as a fundamental gap and propose designed information asymmetry to close it: by partitioning evidence into shared public and disjoint private subsets, each agent holds exclusive knowledge that can only reach others through deliberation. We theoretically show that this decomposition reduces inter-agent error correlation, and instantiate it in InfoDelphi, a framework combining relevance-aware evidence routing, rationale-based iterative deliberation, and confidence-weighted aggregation. On PolyGym, a benchmark of 375 binary forecasting questions derived from real-world prediction markets, InfoDelphi outperforms the strongest single-agent and multi-agent baselines by 12--18% in Brier score and 4--8 percentage points in accuracy. More detailed experiments confirm that removing information asymmetry eliminates most deliberation gains, establishing diversity of input as the key enabler of effective multi-agent reasoning.

AI.tldr: InfoDelphi introduces designed information asymmetry in multi-agent forecasting systems by partitioning evidence into shared public and disjoint private subsets, enabling genuine deliberation that outperforms standard multi-agent approaches by reducing inter-agent error correlation.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent forecasting systems often fail to achieve genuine deliberation because when all agents receive identical evidence, deliberation collapses into herding rather than true belief revision, making multi-agent systems little better than single agents. The authors identify a fundamental gap in how information is distributed among agents in deliberation systems.
AI.method: The authors propose InfoDelphi, a framework that implements designed information asymmetry by partitioning evidence into shared public subsets and disjoint private subsets for each agent. This ensures each agent holds exclusive knowledge that can only be accessed by others through deliberation. The framework combines: 1) relevance-aware evidence routing, 2) rationale-based iterative deliberation, and 3) confidence-weighted aggregation.
AI.result: On PolyGym, a benchmark of 375 binary forecasting questions from real-world prediction markets, InfoDelphi outperforms the strongest single-agent and multi-agent baselines by 12-18% in Brier score and 4-8 percentage points in accuracy. Experiments show that removing information asymmetry eliminates most deliberation gains, confirming diversity of input as the key enabler of effective multi-agent reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes that information asymmetry through evidence partitioning is crucial for enabling genuine deliberation in multi-agent forecasting systems. This approach reduces inter-agent error correlation and significantly improves forecasting performance compared to systems where all agents receive identical information.

title: Evolutionary Feature Engineering for Structured Data
comment: 9 page main content, 41 pages in total
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as open-ended search operators in evolutionary optimization. We introduce Evolutionary Feature Engineering (EFE), a framework for using LLM-based evolution to discover preprocessing transformations for structured data. EFE represents transformations as Python programs with a standardized fit/transform interface, allowing them to be inserted directly into existing machine learning pipelines. During evolution, candidate programs are refined using dataset context, summary statistics, and downstream performance feedback on validation set. We instantiate EFE in two settings. For time-series forecasting, EFE-Time learns invertible, dataset-specific normalizations that improve off-the-shelf time-series foundation models. It reduces forecasting errors (MASE, WQL, MAE) 3% or more when averaged across datasets and improvements are as much as 19% on the COVID-Deaths dataset. Notably, these improvements occur with recent TSFMs such as Chronos-2. For tabular prediction, EFE-Tab evolves compact feature programs that add useful interpretable features and remove redundant ones, improving or matching existing LLM-based feature-engineering methods. We found EFE-Tab to be particularly effective on classical decision trees, where small sets of evolved features yield competitive accuracy while preserving interpretability. Overall, EFE demonstrates that LLM-based evolution can improve both accuracy and interpretability when automatically tackling structured data.

AI.tldr: EFE uses LLM-based evolution to automatically generate preprocessing transformations as Python programs that improve both accuracy and interpretability for structured data tasks like time-series forecasting and tabular prediction.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in evolutionary optimization, but there's a need for frameworks that can discover effective preprocessing transformations for structured data while maintaining interpretability and compatibility with existing ML pipelines.
AI.method: EFE represents transformations as Python programs with fit/transform interfaces, evolving them using LLM-based evolution with dataset context, summary statistics, and validation performance feedback. EFE-Time focuses on time-series forecasting with invertible normalizations, while EFE-Tab evolves compact feature programs for tabular data.
AI.result: EFE-Time reduces forecasting errors by 3%+ on average (up to 19% on COVID-Deaths) even with recent TSFMs like Chronos-2. EFE-Tab improves or matches existing LLM-based feature engineering methods, particularly benefiting classical decision trees with small sets of interpretable features.
AI.conclusion: EFE demonstrates that LLM-based evolution can effectively improve both accuracy and interpretability for structured data preprocessing, offering a practical framework for automatic feature engineering that integrates seamlessly with existing ML pipelines.

title: Separating Expert Retention from Autonomous Source Inference in Raw-ECG-Replay-Free Continual ECG Deployment
summary:
In multi-source ECG deployment, models may need to incorporate new data sources when earlier raw ECGs cannot be retained or replayed. Freezing a pretrained backbone and assigning each source an isolated classifier prevents parameter interference, but deployment still requires selecting an expert when source metadata are unavailable. We study this distinction through \ours{}, an incremental expert bank built on frozen 1024-dimensional ECGFounder features. Each arriving domain adds a balanced-softmax linear expert, while a lightweight router is fitted only on retained training features and domain labels from sources observed so far. A validation-calibrated margin rule fuses the two most likely experts instead of committing to a single routed expert.
  On CPSC, PTB-XL, Georgia, and Chapman-Shaoxing, source-aware expert selection reaches $0.7915\pm0.0036$ Macro-F1 and a matched offline independent-head reference reaches $0.7885\pm0.0009$, supporting strong source-aware expert retention. Without source IDs, an MLP router reaches $0.7756\pm0.0027$ and top-2 margin fusion reaches $0.7782\pm0.0022$. The top-2 gain over hard MLP routing is small ($+0.0026$), with a 95\% confidence interval from paired bootstrap that includes zero. Across three domain orders, the top-2-to-oracle gap remains $0.0111$--$0.0133$, identifying autonomous source inference as the main remaining bottleneck. No raw ECGs are replayed, but frozen training features are retained for router updates; the method is therefore not memory-free.

AI.tldr: A method for incremental ECG source adaptation using frozen backbone features, isolated expert classifiers, and a lightweight router with top-2 fusion for source inference.
AI.motivation: In multi-source ECG deployment, models need to incorporate new data sources without replaying earlier raw ECGs when source metadata is unavailable, requiring methods that prevent parameter interference while handling unknown sources.
AI.method: Freezes pretrained ECG backbone features, adds balanced-softmax linear experts for each new source, uses lightweight router trained on retained features and domain labels, and employs validation-calibrated top-2 margin fusion instead of single expert selection.
AI.result: Achieves 0.7915±0.0036 Macro-F1 with source-aware expert selection, 0.7756±0.0027 with MLP router, and 0.7782±0.0022 with top-2 margin fusion. The top-2 gain over hard routing is small (+0.0026) with confidence interval including zero.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully retains source-aware experts without raw ECG replay, but autonomous source inference remains the main bottleneck (0.0111-0.0133 gap from oracle), and the approach is not memory-free as it retains frozen training features.

title: X-LogSMask: Expand Transformer for Graph-Structured Data
summary: Transformers have become general-purpose architectures, but their all-to-all self-attention is poorly matched to graph data, whose interactions are sparse, structured and multi-scale. Existing Graph Transformers address this mismatch through structural encodings, hybrid message-passing modules or learned attention constraints, often introducing additional complexity and limited interpretability. Here we introduce X-LogSMask, an explainable multi-head logarithmic structural mask that injects symmetrically normalized graph topology directly into attention logits. The logarithmic transform converts structural connectivity into a topology-aware gating signal, suppressing unsupported node interactions while preserving feature-dependent attention. By assigning different powers of the normalized adjacency matrix to different attention heads, X-LogSMask gives each head a defined structural radius and supports multi-hop information propagation within a single layer. We further show that a standard Transformer encoder can be interpreted as one-step message passing on a complete graph, motivating X-LogSMask as a topology-constrained alternative to unrestricted self-attention. Across 20 node-, edge- and graph-level benchmarks, Transformers equipped with X-LogSMask achieve state-of-the-art performance on 13 datasets and remain competitive in a lightweight one-layer configuration. These results show that simple, interpretable structural masks can make self-attention an effective graph-learning operator without changing the Transformer architecture. The code is available at https://github.com/LiLeyan-0120/X-LogSMask.

AI.tldr: X-LogSMask is an explainable structural mask that injects graph topology directly into Transformer attention, using logarithmic transforms to create topology-aware gating signals for effective graph learning without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Transformers' all-to-all self-attention is poorly matched to graph data's sparse, structured, and multi-scale interactions. Existing Graph Transformers add complexity and have limited interpretability, creating a need for simpler, more interpretable solutions.
AI.method: Introduces X-LogSMask - an explainable multi-head logarithmic structural mask that injects symmetrically normalized graph topology directly into attention logits. Uses logarithmic transform to convert structural connectivity into topology-aware gating, suppresses unsupported node interactions while preserving feature-dependent attention, and assigns different powers of normalized adjacency matrix to different heads for multi-hop propagation.
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on 13 out of 20 node-, edge-, and graph-level benchmarks. Remained competitive even in lightweight one-layer configurations, demonstrating effectiveness without architectural modifications.
AI.conclusion: Simple, interpretable structural masks can make self-attention an effective graph-learning operator without changing the Transformer architecture. X-LogSMask provides a topology-constrained alternative to unrestricted self-attention that maintains interpretability while achieving competitive performance.

title: Epistemic Goggles: A Pretrained Module that Induces an Epistemic Frame via Gradient Editing
comment: 20 pages, 10 figures, 2 tables. Code at https://github.com/JoshuaSP/epistemic-goggles and generated documents, questions, and teacher rollouts at https://huggingface.co/datasets/joshuapenman/epistemic-goggles-artifacts
summary: Finetuning a language model on documents that are explicitly annotated as fictional results in a model that still actually believes the documents' core claims, an effect known as Negation Neglect. In our evaluations, models trained on documents prefixed and suffixed with such annotations correctly identify the relevant claims as fictional only about 9% of the time. To address this, we introduce Goggles, a learned module that intervenes on the finetuning gradient rather than the data. During supervised finetuning, a Goggles module edits the gradients an LLM LoRA receives, imparting a chosen epistemic frame (the stance the model takes toward the nature of what it reads) to whatever the documents teach. A Goggles instance is trained once for a given base model, frame, and LoRA configuration, then applied frozen to documents it was never trained on. Trained through Goggles on those same documents, now carrying no fictional annotation, the model flags the content as fictional roughly 91% of the time, while preserving capability (GPQA and TruthfulQA match or exceed baseline). The same architecture supports other frames: a Goggles instance can be trained to treat documents as "part of an AI safety evaluation by Redwood Research" rather than simply as fiction. The imparted frame persists under continued finetuning that pushes back toward the claim, where prior interventions revert. Goggles suggests a path toward training language models on known-misaligned data without absorbing the behaviors that data demonstrates.

AI.tldr: Goggles is a gradient intervention module that edits finetuning gradients to impart chosen epistemic frames (like "fictional" or "AI safety evaluation") to language models, preventing them from absorbing false beliefs from training data while preserving capability.
AI.motivation: Current language models suffer from "Negation Neglect" - when finetuned on documents explicitly labeled as fictional, they still believe the core claims (only ~9% correct identification). This prevents safe training on misaligned data without absorbing harmful behaviors.
AI.method: Goggles is a learned module that intervenes on finetuning gradients rather than data. It edits the gradients an LLM LoRA receives during supervised finetuning, imparting a chosen epistemic frame to whatever the documents teach. It's trained once per base model, frame, and LoRA config, then applied frozen to new documents.
AI.result: Models trained with Goggles on the same documents (without fictional annotations) correctly identify content as fictional ~91% of the time (vs. 9% baseline). Performance on GPQA and TruthfulQA matches or exceeds baseline. The imparted frame persists under continued finetuning where prior interventions revert, and supports other frames beyond just "fiction."
AI.conclusion: Goggles offers a path to train language models on known-misaligned data without absorbing the behaviors that data demonstrates, by directly editing gradients to control how models interpret and believe training content through epistemic frames.

title: Geometric Signatures of Reasoning: A Spectral Perspective on Task Hardness
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables large language models (LLMs) to solve complex problems by generating intermediate reasoning steps. While much attention has been paid to the length and content of these reasoning chains, far less is known about their internal geometry. We study the \emph{geometry} of CoT trajectories in the hidden state space of transformer models, formalizing each reasoning chain as a discrete curve in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and characterizing it through spectral, positional, and kinematic geometric functionals. We introduce the effective dimension $d_ρ$ as a measure of trajectory complexity and show theoretically that trajectories with flatter eigenvalue spectra correspond to harder tasks, as they explore more of the hidden dimensions. Lastly, we explore how kinematic features of the trajectory, mean position, positional dispersion, initial and current hidden states, mean velocity, mean speed, and speed dispersion, can be used to predict solution correctness before generation is complete, and may inform future early-stopping strategies. Experimentally, on mathematical reasoning problems from the MATH500 dataset, $d_ρ$ achieves $0.93$ AUC in distinguishing easy from hard problems, while kinematic features potentially can predict correctness from only the first $20\%$ of generated tokens. These correctness signatures transfer across questions of varying difficulty, establishing that the shape of a model's internal reasoning trajectory is a principled window into both task hardness and solution quality.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the geometric properties of chain-of-thought reasoning trajectories in LLM hidden spaces, showing that trajectory geometry reveals task difficulty and can predict solution correctness early in generation.
AI.motivation: While CoT reasoning is widely studied, little is known about the internal geometric structure of reasoning trajectories in LLM hidden spaces. Understanding this geometry could provide insights into task difficulty and enable early prediction of solution correctness.
AI.method: Formalizes CoT reasoning chains as discrete curves in ℝ^d hidden space, analyzing them using spectral, positional, and kinematic geometric functionals. Introduces effective dimension d_ρ to measure trajectory complexity and uses kinematic features (position, velocity, speed metrics) for analysis. Evaluates on MATH500 mathematical reasoning dataset.
AI.result: Effective dimension d_ρ achieves 0.93 AUC in distinguishing easy from hard problems. Kinematic features can potentially predict solution correctness using only the first 20% of generated tokens. These correctness signatures transfer across questions of varying difficulty.
AI.conclusion: The geometric shape of internal reasoning trajectories provides principled insights into both task hardness and solution quality, enabling early correctness prediction and potentially informing future early-stopping strategies for CoT reasoning.

title: COMFYCLAW: Self-Evolving Skill Harnesses for Image Generation Workflows
summary: Agents are increasingly used to construct workflows and assist humans in completing recurring tasks more efficiently. As these workflows become repeated and domain-specific, agent memory and reusable skills become increasingly important: agents should be able to recall workflow patterns, execution constraints, and user preferences from previous runs. We study this problem in workflow-based image generation and introduce COMFYCLAW, an agentic skill evolution harness for controlling ComfyUI workflows. COMFYCLAW formulates workflow construction as typed graph editing, exposes tools organized by construction stage, automatically reverts invalid edits, and uses a region-level vision-language model (VLM) verifier to translate visual failures into actionable repair suggestions. The framework further evolves a progressively disclosed skill library, where trajectories, execution errors, and verifier feedback from previous runs are distilled into reusable Agent Skills. Across four benchmark splits, three agent models, and two image backbones, COMFYCLAW achieves the best average image-generation evaluation score across all six agent configurations, outperforming a verifier-only baseline without skill evolution. Human annotations further show that annotators prefer COMFYCLAW over variants without skill evolution. Our results suggest that skill evolution is an effective mechanism for improving agent reliability and performance in recurring visual workflow construction.

AI.tldr: COMFYCLAW is an agentic skill evolution system for controlling ComfyUI workflows that improves image generation by evolving reusable agent skills from past workflow executions and using VLM verification for error repair.
AI.motivation: As agents are increasingly used for recurring workflow tasks, they need better memory and reusable skills to recall workflow patterns, execution constraints, and user preferences from previous runs, particularly in workflow-based image generation.
AI.method: COMFYCLAW formulates workflow construction as typed graph editing, provides stage-organized tools, automatically reverts invalid edits, uses a region-level VLM verifier to translate visual failures into repair suggestions, and evolves a progressively disclosed skill library where previous trajectories, errors, and feedback are distilled into reusable Agent Skills.
AI.result: Across four benchmark splits, three agent models, and two image backbones, COMFYCLAW achieved the best average image-generation evaluation score across all six agent configurations, outperforming verifier-only baselines without skill evolution. Human annotations also preferred COMFYCLAW over variants without skill evolution.
AI.conclusion: Skill evolution is an effective mechanism for improving agent reliability and performance in recurring visual workflow construction, demonstrating the value of learning from past workflow executions and distilling that knowledge into reusable skills.

title: BOUNDARY_SYNC: Measuring Communication-Induced Representational Coupling in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are deployed as communicating agents, does inter-agent communication cause outputs to converge? We introduce BOUNDARY_SYNC, a protocol measuring representational coupling via the Coupling Amplification Factor (CAF = JSD_cond / JSD_baseline), where CAF < 1 indicates homogenization and CAF > 1 indicates diversification. In controlled GPT-4o experiments (N=30, ~9,900 API calls), we measure coupling in text and image communication. Key findings: (1) text communication causes significant homogenization (CAF=0.803 [0.740, 0.873], d=1.30, p<0.001), confirmed by no-communication ablation and prompt-perturbation controls; (2) image communication also homogenizes under within-modality baselines (CAF=0.834 [0.811, 0.858]), with comparable proportional effect; (3) group size moderates coupling direction -- K=5 produces homogenization while K=3 yields CAF > 1.0 (point estimates 1.14 and 1.06, CI pending), suggesting a directional shift toward diversification; (4) cross-model replication shows extreme variation (CAF 0.034-0.803), with DeepSeek dominated by format artifacts; (5) coupling is stateless -- driven by prompt context rather than cumulative updating, with continuous consensus producing monotonic convergence. These results establish LLM agent coupling as real, measurable, and controllable at the prompt level, with direct implications for multi-agent system design.

AI.tldr: Inter-agent communication among LLMs causes systematic homogenization of outputs, measured by a novel protocol BOUNDARY_SYNC showing that text and image communication both converge agent responses, with group size influencing coupling direction.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed as communicating agents, there's a need to understand whether inter-agent communication causes outputs to converge (homogenize) or diverge, which has important implications for multi-agent system design and ensuring diversity of responses.
AI.method: Researchers introduced BOUNDARY_SYNC protocol using Coupling Amplification Factor (CAF = JSD_cond / JSD_baseline) to measure representational coupling. They conducted controlled GPT-4o experiments (N=30, ~9,900 API calls) across text and image communication modalities, with various group sizes, ablation studies, prompt-perturbation controls, and cross-model replication tests.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Text communication causes significant homogenization (CAF=0.803), (2) Image communication also homogenizes (CAF=0.834), (3) Group size moderates coupling direction (K=5 homogenizes, K=3 diversifies), (4) Cross-model replication shows extreme variation (CAF 0.034-0.803), (5) Coupling is stateless and driven by prompt context rather than cumulative updating.
AI.conclusion: LLM agent coupling is real, measurable, and controllable at the prompt level, with direct implications for multi-agent system design, showing that communication systematically reduces diversity between agents unless intentionally controlled.

title: Generic Expert Coverage for Pruning SparseMixture-of-Experts Language Models
summary: Sparsely activated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models contain substantial structured redundancy among routed experts, but pruning them without downstream calibration data remains challenging. Existing expert-pruning methods typically rely on a single aggregated importance score, which can bias the retained set toward experts favored by dominant calibration patterns. We propose \textbf{Generic TB-Coverage}, a coverage-aware expert pruning method that uses only generic text corpora (WikiText2 and C4) for calibration. Instead of collapsing expert utility into one score, our method profiles per-expert utility separately on each corpus and enforces a fixed-budget coverage rule that preserves high-utility experts from each corpus before constructing the final pruning mask. Across Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B and DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Base at 25\%, 50\%, and 75\% retention budgets, our method improves average accuracy on six common zero-shot benchmarks over random pruning, REAP, and ExpertSparsity, while also reducing perplexity degradation on WikiText2 and C4. The gains are largest under aggressive pruning (25\% and 50\% retain), suggesting that preserving cross-corpus expert coverage is an effective generic-data prior for MoE pruning. Our improvements hold with fixed pruning budgets and no downstream calibration data.

AI.tldr: A novel expert pruning method for Mixture-of-Experts models that uses generic text corpora and coverage-aware pruning to better preserve important experts across different calibration patterns, improving performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing expert-pruning methods for MoE language models rely on single aggregated importance scores, which can bias the retained experts toward those favored by dominant calibration patterns, leading to suboptimal pruning decisions.
AI.method: Generic TB-Coverage: profiles per-expert utility separately on each calibration corpus (WikiText2 and C4), enforces a fixed-budget coverage rule to preserve high-utility experts from each corpus before constructing the final pruning mask.
AI.result: The method improves average accuracy on six zero-shot benchmarks over random pruning, REAP, and ExpertSparsity across Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B and DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Base at 25%, 50%, and 75% retention budgets, while reducing perplexity degradation on calibration corpora.
AI.conclusion: Preserving cross-corpus expert coverage is an effective generic-data prior for MoE pruning, especially under aggressive pruning budgets, and the method works without downstream calibration data, making it practical for real-world applications.

title: SINA: A Fully Automated Circuit Schematic Image to Netlist Generator Using Artificial Intelligence
summary: Recent advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have revolutionized Electronic Design Automation (EDA), particularly through Large Language Models (LLMs) for circuit design tasks. However, their application to analog and mixed-signal domains remains limited by the lack of machine-readable representations of existing circuit design knowledge. Circuit schematic images found in research manuscripts, textbooks, and websites constitute a vast repository of validated designs; however, these visual representations cannot be directly processed by EDA tools. Converting them into machine-readable netlists is essential for enabling simulation, verification, and building comprehensive databases for AI-based models. Current conversion methods lack generalization across both Integrated Circuit (IC) and Printed Circuit Board (PCB) level schematics. Moreover, they struggle with component recognition and connectivity inference, and fail to distinguish between connected junctions and crossing wires. In this paper, we propose SINA, an open-source circuit schematic image-to-netlist generator. SINA is a fully automated pipeline that integrates deep learning for robust component detection, connected-component labeling for accurate connectivity inference, Optical Character Recognition (OCR) for component reference designator extraction, and a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for reliable reference designator assignment. SINA handles both IC- and PCB-level schematics and incorporates dedicated crossing-wires detection to differentiate wire intersections from connections. We validate the correctness of the generated netlists using graph isomorphism techniques. Our experiments demonstrate an overall netlist generation accuracy of 96.67%, which is 2.72x higher compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: SINA is an open-source, automated pipeline that converts circuit schematic images to machine-readable netlists with 96.67% accuracy, significantly outperforming existing methods by handling both IC and PCB schematics while distinguishing between wire connections and crossings.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of machine-readable circuit design knowledge for analog/mixed-signal domains despite vast repositories of schematic images in research, textbooks, and websites. Existing methods fail to generalize across IC/PCB schematics, struggle with component recognition and connectivity inference, and can't distinguish between connected junctions and crossing wires.
AI.method: SINA integrates multiple technologies: deep learning for component detection, connected-component labeling for connectivity inference, OCR for reference designator extraction, and a Vision-Language Model for reliable designator assignment. It also includes dedicated crossing-wires detection to differentiate wire intersections from connections, and validates netlists using graph isomorphism.
AI.result: The system achieves 96.67% overall netlist generation accuracy, which is 2.72x higher than state-of-the-art approaches. It successfully handles both IC- and PCB-level schematics and correctly distinguishes between connected junctions and crossing wires.
AI.conclusion: SINA provides an effective solution for converting visual circuit schematics into machine-readable netlists, enabling better utilization of existing circuit design knowledge for AI-based EDA tools, simulation, verification, and database creation.

title: Distributionally Robust Listwise Preference Optimization
summary: Existing robust preference optimization for language-model alignment mainly studies pairwise supervision and places robustness at the dataset, prompt, or preference-pair level. We instead study listwise preference optimization under ranking-label uncertainty: given a prompt and a candidate list, the observed ranking over that list may be ambiguous due to annotator inconsistency, near-ties, lossy rankwise feedback, or reward-model noise. We propose a pointwise total-variation robust Plackett--Luce objective that directly robustifies the ranking label conditional on the candidate list. The robust loss admits an exact decomposition into the nominal PL loss plus a worst-case PL correction, and the worst-case ranking is obtained by sorting current implicit scores in ascending order, reducing the inner maximization from $K!$ enumeration to $O(K\log K)$. This tractable structure yields strong offline and online optimization guarantees. In the offline fixed-list setting, the robust objective is convex and projected stochastic subgradient reaches global $ε$-suboptimality with $O(ε^{-2})$ sample complexity. In the online policy-induced setting, where candidate lists are generated by the current policy, we establish weak convexity and $\widetilde O(ε^{-2})$ Moreau-envelope stationarity. Experiments in offline LLM alignment show that the proposed robust correction largely preserves performance under clean labels and improves robustness under noise. In online alignment, it makes reward-model-ranked candidate expansion more reliable and improves both reward-model and external GPT-4 judge metrics.

AI.tldr: Robust listwise preference optimization for language model alignment that handles ranking-label uncertainty through a total-variation robust Plackett-Luce objective with efficient optimization guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing robust preference optimization focuses on pairwise supervision at dataset, prompt, or pair level. This paper addresses listwise preference optimization under ranking-label uncertainty caused by annotator inconsistency, near-ties, lossy rankwise feedback, or reward-model noise.
AI.method: Proposes a pointwise total-variation robust Plackett-Luce objective that robustifies ranking labels conditional on candidate lists. The robust loss decomposes into nominal PL loss plus worst-case PL correction. The worst-case ranking is obtained by sorting implicit scores ascending, reducing inner maximization from K! to O(K log K).
AI.result: Offline: robust objective is convex with O(ε^{-2}) sample complexity for global ε-suboptimality. Online: establishes weak convexity and O(ε^{-2}) Moreau-envelope stationarity. Experimental results show robust correction preserves performance under clean labels and improves robustness under noise. Online alignment makes reward-model-ranked candidate expansion more reliable and improves both reward-model and GPT-4 judge metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed robust listwise preference optimization method effectively handles ranking-label uncertainty with tractable optimization guarantees and demonstrates practical benefits in both offline and online language model alignment scenarios.

title: MKGR: Multimodal Knowledge-Graph Representation Learning for Cold-Start Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction
summary: Accurate protein-protein interaction (PPI) prediction is central to functional genomics, disease mechanism discovery, and drug development. A difficult setting arises when candidate interactions include proteins that have no observed PPI edges during training, where models relying on network topology alone often lose useful context. This paper presents \method, a multimodal representation framework for cold-start PPI prediction. \method\ combines region-aware protein sequence encoding with four protein-centered biomedical knowledge graphs, including protein-drug, protein-disease, protein-miRNA, and protein-lncRNA associations. The sequence branch extracts contextual representations from structurally informed sequence regions, while graph attention encoders learn modality-specific protein embeddings from sparse biomedical associations. A bridge reconstruction objective regularizes graph learning by recovering shared protein-entity associations, and a pair-level gating module adaptively integrates sequence and graph evidence for each candidate protein pair. Experiments on two benchmark datasets under novel-old and novel-novel cold-start settings show that \method\ consistently outperforms competitive sequence, network, and knowledge-graph baselines across ACC, F1, AUC, AUPR, and MCC.

AI.tldr: METHOD is a multimodal framework for cold-start protein-protein interaction prediction that combines region-aware sequence encoding with biomedical knowledge graphs and adaptive fusion.
AI.motivation: Accurate PPI prediction is crucial for functional genomics, disease mechanism discovery, and drug development, but existing models struggle with cold-start scenarios where proteins have no observed interaction edges during training, losing useful context that topology alone cannot capture.
AI.method: METHOD combines region-aware protein sequence encoding with four protein-centered biomedical knowledge graphs (protein-drug, protein-disease, protein-miRNA, and protein-lncRNA associations). It uses sequence branch for contextual representations from structurally informed regions, graph attention encoders for modality-specific embeddings, a bridge reconstruction objective to regularize graph learning, and a pair-level gating module to adaptively integrate sequence and graph evidence.
AI.result: Experiments on two benchmark datasets under novel-old and novel-novel cold-start settings show that METHOD consistently outperforms competitive sequence, network, and knowledge-graph baselines across multiple metrics including ACC, F1, AUC, AUPR, and MCC.
AI.conclusion: The METHOD framework effectively addresses cold-start PPI prediction by leveraging both sequence information and biomedical knowledge graphs through multimodal fusion and adaptive integration, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing approaches in challenging cold-start scenarios.

title: DRL-CLBA: A Clean Label Backdoor Attack for Speech Classification via DDPG Reinforcement Learning
summary: Deep learning models for speech classification are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where malicious triggers cause misclassification at inference time. While sample-specific attacks can bypass many defenses, they often rely on poisoned label attack, making them detectable via manual data defense. In this paper, we propose DRL-CLBA, a novel clean label backdoor attack for speech classification that leverages Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) reinforcement learning. We also utilize deep audio steganography to embed sample-specific triggers into source audio, creating feature-space anchors. The proposed reinforcement learning framework effectively optimizes target samples toward trigger-bearing anchor points in the model's deep latent space, enabling label-migration-free poisoning of target samples. Experimental results across three datasets and four different DNNs demonstrate that DRL-CLBA achieves a high attack success rate, effectively bypassing some backdoor defenses. The attack demonstrates strong resistance against fine-tuning, pruning, and spectral signature defenses, exposing critical vulnerabilities in speech-controlled systems.

AI.tldr: DRL-CLBA is a clean-label backdoor attack for speech classification that uses reinforcement learning and audio steganography to embed sample-specific triggers, achieving high attack success while bypassing defenses.
AI.motivation: Current sample-specific backdoor attacks often rely on poisoned label attacks, making them detectable through manual data inspection. There's a need for more stealthy attacks that can bypass existing defenses in speech classification systems.
AI.method: Uses Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) reinforcement learning framework combined with deep audio steganography. The RL optimizes target samples toward trigger-bearing anchor points in the model's deep latent space, while audio steganography embeds sample-specific triggers into source audio to create feature-space anchors.
AI.result: Achieves high attack success rate across three datasets and four different DNNs. Successfully bypasses some backdoor defenses and demonstrates strong resistance against fine-tuning, pruning, and spectral signature defenses.
AI.conclusion: DRL-CLBA exposes critical vulnerabilities in speech-controlled systems by demonstrating that clean-label backdoor attacks with sample-specific triggers can effectively bypass current defense mechanisms, highlighting the need for more robust security measures.

title: DeadPool: Resilient LLM Training with Hot-Swapping via Zero-Overhead Checkpoint
summary: State-of-the-art large language model (LLM) training takes tens of thousands of graphics processing units (GPUs) for months and encounters failures across the software and hardware stack. Existing fault-tolerance mechanisms either impose non-trivial overhead during failure-free execution or suffer from prolonged recovery latency, particularly under scenarios where a small subset of compute nodes experience permanent failures. %The tradeoff between failure-free overhead and recovery latency forms a space forms a Pareto frontier We present DeadPool to simultaneously address both optimization objectives. DeadPool incorporates a fault-tolerance mechanism that restores LLM training via hot-swapping, namely by replacing failed nodes with spare nodes without terminating the complete job. The hot-swapping of DeadPool is enabled by two ideas: First, it exploits an off-critical-path in-memory checkpointing mechanism for spatial redundancy. Second, it introduces a communicator reconstruction protocol that replaces failed nodes with spare nodes at runtime. DeadPool efficiently overlaps the in-memory checkpointing with computation, thus introducing zero overhead during error-free execution. Upon permanent node failures, DeadPool can rebuild memory states with minimal recomputation by leveraging in-memory checkpoints. We evaluate DeadPool across scales (up to 512 NVIDIA A100 GPUs) and LLMs (up to 65B parameters), and observe zero checkpoint overhead with hot-swapping recovery completing in under 40 seconds. These results show that DeadPool simultaneously achieves both zero-overhead error-free execution and extremely low recovery cost.

AI.tldr: DeadPool is a fault-tolerance system for large language model training that enables hot-swapping of failed nodes with zero overhead during normal execution and rapid recovery (under 40 seconds) from permanent node failures.
AI.motivation: Current LLM training requires massive GPU resources for months and faces frequent failures across software/hardware stacks. Existing fault-tolerance solutions either impose significant overhead during failure-free execution or suffer from prolonged recovery latency, creating a difficult trade-off.
AI.method: DeadPool uses two key innovations: 1) off-critical-path in-memory checkpointing for spatial redundancy that overlaps with computation, and 2) a communicator reconstruction protocol that enables runtime replacement of failed nodes with spare nodes via hot-swapping without terminating the complete training job.
AI.result: Evaluated at scale (up to 512 NVIDIA A100 GPUs) with LLMs up to 65B parameters. Achieved zero checkpoint overhead during error-free execution, with hot-swapping recovery completing in under 40 seconds upon permanent node failures.
AI.conclusion: DeadPool successfully breaks the Pareto frontier trade-off in fault tolerance by simultaneously achieving both zero-overhead during normal execution and extremely low recovery cost, making it a practical solution for large-scale LLM training.

title: Reformalization of the Jordan Curve Theorem
summary: We present a case study in reformalization, a variant of autoformalization in which the input proof is not natural language but a formal development in a different proof assistant. Concretely, we report three reformalizations of the Jordan Curve Theorem: from Mizar to Lean, from HOL Light to Lean, and from HOL Light to Agda. We analyse the results and identify pipeline design choices that matter for practical reformalization tasks.

AI.tldr: A case study on reformalization (autoformalization between proof assistants) showing three implementations of Jordan Curve Theorem conversions between different proof systems and analyzing pipeline design choices.
AI.motivation: To study reformalization - a variant of autoformalization where the input is not natural language but a formal development in a different proof assistant, enabling migration of formal proofs between different proof assistant systems.
AI.method: Conducted three reformalization case studies: 1) Mizar to Lean, 2) HOL Light to Lean, and 3) HOL Light to Agda, all focusing on the Jordan Curve Theorem. Analyzed the results and identified pipeline design choices that impact practical reformalization tasks.
AI.result: Successfully implemented three reformalizations of the Jordan Curve Theorem across different proof assistant systems. Identified key pipeline design choices that are important for practical reformalization work.
AI.conclusion: Reformalization is a practical approach for migrating formal proofs between different proof assistants, with specific pipeline design choices significantly affecting the success and efficiency of such conversions.

title: CALM: Interpretable Cross-Modal Alignment for Biomarker Discovery from Unpaired Data
summary: The interaction between brain structure and genetic influences is key to understanding neuropsychiatric disorders. However, most large-scale datasets are unimodal, providing either neuroimaging or genetics data. We propose CALM, a framework that learns interpretable associations between brain ROIs and genetic pathways from completely disjoint populations. CALM aligns the two modalities in a shared latent space via linear projections that simultaneously match the class-conditional latent distributions and ensure group separability. These projections provide interpretable pathway--ROI associations. When trained on unimodal imaging and genetics datasets, CALM generalizes to an unseen paired dataset, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods and ablation baselines. We also demonstrate stability of the learned associations against a paired baseline. Our experiments on autism spectrum disorder reveal immune and metabolic pathways linked to specific cortical regions and are consistent with established literature. Thus, CALM opens the door to leveraging large unimodal repositories for studying cross-modal interactions in brain disorders across disparate datasets.

AI.tldr: CALM is a framework that learns interpretable associations between brain regions of interest (ROIs) and genetic pathways from completely disjoint populations by aligning neuroimaging and genetics data in a shared latent space.
AI.motivation: Most large-scale datasets for neuropsychiatric disorders are unimodal (either neuroimaging or genetics), making it difficult to study the interaction between brain structure and genetic influences. There's a need to leverage large unimodal repositories to study cross-modal interactions.
AI.method: CALM learns interpretable associations between brain ROIs and genetic pathways by aligning the two modalities in a shared latent space using linear projections. These projections simultaneously match class-conditional latent distributions and ensure group separability. The framework is trained on unimodal imaging and genetics datasets separately.
AI.result: CALM generalizes well to unseen paired datasets, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods and ablation baselines. The learned associations show stability against a paired baseline. Experiments on autism spectrum disorder revealed immune and metabolic pathways linked to specific cortical regions that are consistent with established literature.
AI.conclusion: CALM successfully enables leveraging large unimodal repositories for studying cross-modal interactions in brain disorders across disparate datasets, opening new doors for multimodal analysis even when paired data is unavailable.

title: Meta-Benchmarks for Financial-Services LLM Evaluation
summary: Public LLM leaderboards optimise for global average performance and do not capture the specific cognitive demands of financial-services work: a model that leads on MMLU-Pro may underperform on document-grounded compliance reasoning, and a coding leader may handle multi-turn customer interactions poorly. We present a meta-benchmarking framework that organises 452 publicly reported benchmarks into 41 O*NET Generalized Work Activities and aggregates those into 38 BIAN banking business domains spanning sales, operations, risk, and support work. A multiplicative weighting scheme (discrimination x coverage x recency), computed over a rolling model window, rewards benchmarks that still separate the best models, are widely reported, and remain in active use, suppressing saturated legacy tests automatically. These weights scale the K-factor in a pairwise Elo tournament, producing cross-benchmark-comparable work-activity scores without raw score normalisation; business-domain scores are weighted averages of the constituent work-activity Elos. We demonstrate the framework on a point-in-time public snapshot covering 288 models across 25 organisations as of June 2026, and describe the methodology, full taxonomy, design decisions, and limitations with the aim of making the approach reproducible for institutions facing similar selection and governance challenges.

AI.tldr: A meta-benchmarking framework that organizes 452 public benchmarks into financial-services work activities and banking domains, using weighted Elo scores for model selection tailored to industry needs.
AI.motivation: Public LLM leaderboards focus on global average performance but fail to capture specific cognitive demands of financial-services work. Different models excel at different tasks (e.g., compliance reasoning vs. customer interactions), creating a need for domain-specific evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: Organizes 452 benchmarks into 41 O*NET Generalized Work Activities, aggregates into 38 BIAN banking domains. Uses multiplicative weighting (discrimination × coverage × recency) over rolling model window to identify meaningful benchmarks. Applies weights to scale K-factor in pairwise Elo tournament to produce comparable work-activity scores without raw score normalization.
AI.result: Developed a reproducible framework demonstrated on a snapshot covering 288 models across 25 organizations as of June 2026. Produces domain-specific scores that better reflect financial-services requirements than traditional leaderboards.
AI.conclusion: The framework addresses limitations of existing LLM evaluation by providing financial-institution-specific benchmarking that captures real-world cognitive demands, enabling better model selection and governance decisions for industry applications.

title: Message Passing Based Two-Timescale Bayesian Learning for Joint Channel and Memory Hardware Impairments Tracking
summary: Hardware impairments in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) receivers introduce inter-symbol memory and inter-element coupling, severely degrading channel estimation. This paper employs a residual recurrent gated unit (RGRU) to model the intra-slot memory of the hardware impairments and proposes a message-passing-based two-timescale Bayesian deep learning (MP-TTBDL) framework for joint channel and impairment tracking. Owing to small-scale fading, the wireless channel varies rapidly across slots, whereas hardware impairments drift slowly due to hardware aging and environmental variations. To capture these distinct physical timescales, a fastvarying Markov prior and a slow-varying Gaussian Markov prior are assigned to the sparse channel and the network parameters, respectively. Based on a multi-slot factor graph formulation, a message-passing algorithm is developed. Specifically, the inter-slot messages admit closed-form updates, while the intra-slot factor graph, due to its complex recurrent structure, is partitioned into a channel tracking module and an impairments calibration module. The channel tracking module performs sparse channel estimation via turbo orthogonal approximate message passing (Turbo-OAMP), and the impairments calibration module updates the impairment parameters via a specially designed deep approximate message passing (DAMP) procedure, with the two modules iteratively exchanging extrinsic information through expectation propagation (EP) until convergence. Simulation results show that the proposed framework robustly achieves lower channel estimation error than conventional compensators followed by channel estimation across different online impairment scenarios and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a novel Bayesian deep learning framework (MP-TTBDL) for joint channel and hardware impairment tracking in massive MIMO systems by modeling impairments with recurrent neural networks and using message-passing algorithms.
AI.motivation: Hardware impairments in massive MIMO receivers cause inter-symbol memory and inter-element coupling that severely degrade channel estimation performance, which existing methods don't adequately address.
AI.method: Uses residual recurrent gated units (RGRU) to model impairment memory, creates a two-timescale Bayesian framework with distinct priors (fast-varying Markov for channels, slow-varying Gaussian Markov for impairments), and develops message-passing algorithms with Turbo-OAMP for channel tracking and specially designed DAMP for impairment calibration.
AI.result: Simulations show the proposed MP-TTBDL framework robustly achieves lower channel estimation error than conventional compensation methods across various impairment scenarios and SNR conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed message-passing-based two-timescale Bayesian deep learning framework effectively handles the distinct timescales of channel variations and hardware impairment drift, providing superior joint channel and impairment tracking in massive MIMO systems.

title: Path-level Hindsight Instructions for Semantic Exploration in Vision-Language Navigation
summary: On-policy exploration is a crucial component for training robust Vision-Language Navigation agents, as it exposes the policy to a broader state distribution. However, such exploration inevitably leads to trajectories that deviate from expert demonstrations, resulting in a semantic mismatch between the executed visual stream and the original language instruction. In this work, we address this challenge by introducing Phi-Nav, a unified on-policy framework that leverages hindsight reasoning to align instructions with the agent's actual exploratory journey. Specifically, Phi-Nav operates through a three-stage dual-supervision cycle: 1) the agent performs oracle-guided on-policy exploration, sampling a trajectory while learning from expert action feedback, 2) a hindsight speaker synthesizes a path-level hindsight instruction grounded in the collected visual observations, and 3) the agent conducts a second imitation pass, treating the synthesized trajectory-instruction pair as an additional expert demonstration. Through this process, Phi-Nav bridges the critical semantic supervision gap inherent in on-policy methods, transforming semantically unlabeled movement into dense training signals. Evaluations on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks show that Phi-Nav yields competitive performance while requiring only a fraction of the expert demonstrations used by current baselines. These results underscore the necessity of semantic exploration in VLN, positioning Phi-Nav as an effective solution for training embodied agents with limited data.

AI.tldr: Phi-Nav is an on-policy VLN framework that uses hindsight reasoning to align exploratory trajectories with language instructions, addressing semantic mismatch issues while reducing reliance on expert demonstrations.
AI.motivation: On-policy exploration in Vision-Language Navigation leads to trajectories that deviate from expert demonstrations, creating semantic mismatch between visual streams and original language instructions. This semantic supervision gap limits learning effectiveness.
AI.method: Phi-Nav uses a three-stage dual-supervision cycle: 1) Oracle-guided on-policy exploration with expert action feedback, 2) Hindsight speaker synthesizes path-level instructions from collected visual observations, and 3) Second imitation pass using synthesized trajectory-instruction pairs as additional expert demonstrations.
AI.result: Evaluations on R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks show competitive performance while requiring only a fraction of the expert demonstrations used by current baselines.
AI.conclusion: Phi-Nav effectively bridges the semantic supervision gap in on-policy VLN methods, transforming semantically unlabeled movements into dense training signals, demonstrating the necessity of semantic exploration and enabling effective embodied agent training with limited data.

title: Revisiting Decentralized Online Convex Optimization with Compressed Communication
summary: Decentralized online convex optimization (D-OCO) is a popular framework for distributed applications with streaming data. To tackle the communication bottleneck, previous studies have investigated D-OCO with compressed communication and proposed several algorithms that are variants of online gradient descent (OGD). However, for D-OCO with exact communication, the best existing algorithms are variants of follow-the-regularized-leader (FTRL). In this paper, for the first time, we propose two FTRL-type algorithms for D-OCO with compressed communication. Compared with OGD-type algorithms, our algorithms are more elegant in both algorithmic design and theoretical analysis. The key insight is that the dual update mechanism of FTRL allows us to make a simple application of the technique for average consensus with communication compression. More specifically, our first algorithm considers the full-information setting, and can match the existing regret bounds. Our second algorithm is designed for the bandit setting, and can significantly improve both the regret bounds and communication costs of existing algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two FTRL-type algorithms for decentralized online convex optimization with compressed communication - one for full-information and one for bandit settings - improving on existing OGD-type approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing D-OCO algorithms with compressed communication are mostly OGD variants, while FTRL algorithms are better for exact communication but haven't been adapted for compressed scenarios. There's a gap in having FTRL-type algorithms for D-OCO with communication compression.
AI.method: Proposed two FTRL-type algorithms: 1) Full-information setting algorithm using FTRL framework with dual update mechanism that allows simple application of average consensus compression techniques. 2) Bandit setting algorithm that significantly improves communication costs by leveraging FTRL structure.
AI.result: First algorithm matches existing regret bounds for full-information setting. Second algorithm significantly improves both regret bounds and communication costs for bandit setting compared to existing algorithms.
AI.conclusion: FTRL-type algorithms are more elegant than OGD variants for D-OCO with compressed communication, both in design and analysis. The dual update mechanism of FTRL enables efficient compression integration, making it a promising direction for distributed online learning with communication constraints.

title: Mastermind: Strategy-grounded Learning for Repository-Scale Vulnerability Reproduction
summary: Repository-level vulnerability reproduction is a demanding software engineering (SE) task: an agent must inspect a codebase, infer the input grammar that reaches a vulnerable path, construct a proof-of-conceptv(PoC), and verify that the crash disappears on the patched build. Recent LLM agents can often execute these steps when the approach is correct, yet they still fail by choosing the wrong strategy. This paper argues that strategy, rather than the full action trajectory, is the right learning unit for such SE agents: it is compact enough to optimize, concrete enough to guide execution, and stable enough to store and reuse across attempts. We present Mastermind, a dual-loop framework that separates transferable strategy learning from task-specific experience. A trainable planner learns reusable vulnerability-reproduction strategies through SFT and milestone-based GRPO, while an experience loop maintains task-local strategy records that guide subsequent attempts. The planner is trained independently of the executor, allowing strategy learning to improve multiple frozen executors without modifying their action-generation capability. We evaluate Mastermind on CyberGym using 260 training tasks and 200 held-out evaluation tasks. With GPT-5.5 as the frozen executor, Mastermind achieves an 84.5% pass rate, outperforming open-book PoC context (60.0%), Best-of-8 sampling (63.0%), and iterative improvement (77.0%). The same planner also improves GPT-5.4 mini and GLM~5.1 from 45.0% and 58.5% to 60.0% and 71.0%. These results demonstrate that learning high-level strategies is an effective and transferable mechanism for improving repository-scale SE agents.

AI.tldr: Mastermind is a dual-loop framework for repository-level vulnerability reproduction that separates transferable strategy learning from task-specific execution, achieving 84.5% success rate with frozen LLM executors by learning reusable vulnerability-reproduction strategies.
AI.motivation: Repository-level vulnerability reproduction is challenging for LLM agents - while they can execute steps correctly, they often fail by choosing wrong strategies. The paper argues that strategy (rather than full action trajectories) is the right learning unit for SE agents because it's compact enough to optimize, concrete enough to guide execution, and stable enough to store and reuse across attempts.
AI.method: Mastermind uses a dual-loop framework with separate strategy learning and task execution. A trainable planner learns reusable vulnerability-reproduction strategies through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and milestone-based GRPO (Goal-Reinforced Policy Optimization). An experience loop maintains task-local strategy records that guide subsequent attempts. The planner is trained independently of the executor, allowing strategy learning to improve multiple frozen executors without modifying their action-generation capabilities.
AI.result: On CyberGym with 260 training and 200 evaluation tasks, Mastermind with GPT-5.5 as frozen executor achieved 84.5% pass rate, outperforming open-book PoC context (60.0%), Best-of-8 sampling (63.0%), and iterative improvement (77.0%). The same planner also improved GPT-5.4 mini from 45.0% to 60.0% and GLM~5.1 from 58.5% to 71.0%, demonstrating strategy learning's effectiveness and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Learning high-level strategies is an effective and transferable mechanism for improving repository-scale software engineering agents, separating strategy learning from execution enables performance gains across multiple frozen LLM executors without modifying their core capabilities.

title: UniWind: Toward Unified Day-Ahead Wind Power Forecasting via Physics-Informed State Routing
summary: Day-ahead wind power forecasting is essential for cost-effective power-system operation. It is primarily driven by future meteorological conditions while retaining temporal dependencies in power generation. In practice, observed wind-farm power often entangles physically available power with local environmental effects and latent operational states, such as shutdowns and curtailment. Existing physical models provide useful constraints but adapt poorly across wind farms, whereas data-driven models can capture rich correlations but often conflate meteorological effects with state-induced deviations. In this study, we propose UniWind, a wind power forecasting model based on physics-informed state routing. UniWind first employs a Physical Prior Estimator to construct a site-calibrated physical prior by combining site-conditioned monotonic warping with a shared physical power curve. It further applies a physical upper-bound constraint to shape this prior as a soft envelope of available wind power generation. UniWind then proposes a Latent State Encoder to model operating-state embeddings and transforms the physical prior into final power forecasts through a State-aware Power Corrector, which uses knowledge-guided supervised state routing and bounded, state-specific expert correction. Full-shot and cross-farm zero-shot experiments on more than 20 real-world datasets demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of UniWind.

AI.tldr: UniWind: A physics-informed state routing model for day-ahead wind power forecasting that combines site-calibrated physical priors with state-aware corrections to handle both meteorological effects and operational states like shutdowns/curtailment.
AI.motivation: Day-ahead wind power forecasting is crucial for power-system operation, but observed wind-farm power data often entangles physically available power with local environmental effects and latent operational states (shutdowns, curtailment). Existing physical models adapt poorly across different wind farms, while data-driven models conflate meteorological effects with state-induced deviations.
AI.method: UniWind uses physics-informed state routing with three main components: 1) Physical Prior Estimator combining site-conditioned monotonic warping with shared physical power curve, shaped by physical upper-bound constraint; 2) Latent State Encoder modeling operating-state embeddings; 3) State-aware Power Corrector using knowledge-guided supervised state routing and bounded, state-specific expert correction to transform physical prior into final forecasts.
AI.result: Full-shot and cross-farm zero-shot experiments on more than 20 real-world datasets demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of UniWind for wind power forecasting.
AI.conclusion: UniWind effectively bridges the gap between physical models and data-driven approaches by incorporating physical constraints while modeling operational states, achieving improved performance across diverse wind farm scenarios through its physics-informed state routing framework.

title: SimWorlds: A Multi-Agent System for Dynamic 3D Scene Creation
summary: LLM agents are increasingly used to translate natural language into 3D scenes in a procedural way, but existing systems focus on static output. Dynamic 4D scenes from text alone, in which liquids flow, particles emit, rigid bodies cascade, and articulated mechanisms move, remain largely unexplored despite their value as editable content and as physics-grounded training data for video generation and embodied AI. Two challenges set the dynamic case apart from static text-to-scene work: an agent must jointly coordinate spatial layout, multiple physics solvers, temporal sequencing, camera, and lighting in a single coherent scene, and verifying motion correctness from rendered video is fundamentally harder than judging a single image. We present SimWorlds: a multi-agent framework that produces dynamic, editable 4D scenes from text, with Blender-specific procedural knowledge, a planner-coder-reviewer workflow driving a fixed ordered sequence of construction stages, a layered scene protocol enforced by a deterministic verifier, and a runtime-state inspection tool suite that catches mechanism failures the rendered image cannot reveal. We also introduce 4DBuildBench, a benchmark for assessing both visual fidelity and physical consistency of the procedural dynamic 3D scenes generated from text prompts. Experiments show that SimWorlds outperforms prior dynamic Blender generation baselines.

AI.tldr: SimWorlds is a multi-agent framework that generates dynamic, editable 4D scenes from text descriptions, addressing challenges in joint coordination of spatial layout, physics solvers, temporal sequencing, and verification of motion correctness through specialized tools and workflows.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent systems for text-to-3D scene generation focus on static output, while dynamic 4D scenes (with liquid flows, particle emissions, rigid body cascades, and articulated mechanisms) remain largely unexplored despite their value as editable content and physics-grounded training data for video generation and embodied AI.
AI.method: SimWorlds uses a multi-agent framework with: 1) Blender-specific procedural knowledge, 2) a planner-coder-reviewer workflow driving fixed ordered construction stages, 3) a layered scene protocol enforced by a deterministic verifier, and 4) a runtime-state inspection tool suite that catches mechanism failures not visible in rendered images.
AI.result: The framework is evaluated using 4DBuildBench, a new benchmark for assessing both visual fidelity and physical consistency of procedural dynamic 3D scenes. Experiments show SimWorlds outperforms prior dynamic Blender generation baselines.
AI.conclusion: SimWorlds successfully addresses the unique challenges of dynamic 4D scene generation from text, providing a framework that coordinates multiple complex elements while ensuring physical consistency through specialized verification tools, advancing beyond static text-to-scene systems.

title: SCAPE: Accurate and Efficient LLM Training with Extreme Sparse Communication
