We are deliberately cautious about these numbers: with only nine subjects, within-subject evaluation is optimistic, and we argue that subject-independent evaluation -- in which no learner appears in both training and test data -- should be the standard for this task. To that end we release a reproducible evaluation pipeline. We frame the work as a feasibility study rather than a deployable system, and pair it with an open, notebook-based tool that records EEG, runs inference, and visualizes estimated cognitive load as a heatmap over the video timeline to help educators locate potentially challenging segments.

AI.tldr: A study using consumer-grade EEG devices to detect cognitive load during educational video watching, achieving up to 78.5% accuracy in within-subject evaluation with a hybrid CNN+LSTM+Attention model, but advocating for subject-independent evaluation as the standard.
AI.motivation: Remote learning environments lack the visual cues instructors typically use to monitor student cognitive load and identify difficult content, creating a need for alternative methods to assess learner difficulty during online education.
AI.method: Used NeuroSky MindWave Mobile 2 EEG headset on 9 subjects from a public dataset. Implemented a hybrid CNN+LSTM+Attention model combining raw EEG waveform with band-power features, using within-subject evaluation and regularization techniques (dropout and L2).
AI.result: Within-subject accuracy reached up to 78.5% (vs. 55% for conventional classifiers). Regularization stabilized validation accuracy at 68-73% by closing training-validation gap. The paper emphasizes caution due to small sample size and advocates for subject-independent evaluation as the standard.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates feasibility of using consumer EEG for cognitive load detection in education but frames it as preliminary research. The authors release an open evaluation pipeline and visualization tool for educators to identify challenging video segments, while calling for more rigorous subject-independent evaluation standards.

title: ContextSniper: AntTrail's Token-Efficient Code Memory for Repository-Level Program Repair
summary: Large language model agents can repair real repository issues, but they often spend large context budgets on whole-file reads, broad searches, and long terminal outputs where useful evidence is mixed with irrelevant code and logs. This paper presents ContextSniper, AntTrail's token-efficient code memory layer for repository-level program repair. As the coding specialization of AntTrail's broader agent memory engine, ContextSniper implements the Sniper feature for precision evidence selection: it retrieves candidate code and runtime evidence, ranks it with hybrid retrieval signals, filters long outputs through an intention-aware context gate, and returns compact evidence packets while preserving recoverable source context outside the prompt. We evaluate ContextSniper on SWE-bench Lite with OpenClaw and Claude Code, using 50 task runs per host-agent condition. ContextSniper reduces total token use by 51.5% and logged cost by 36.4% for OpenClaw, and reduces total token use by 38.9% and estimated cost by 27.3% for Claude Code. Submitted-resolution rates decrease slightly, from 26.0% to 24.0% for OpenClaw and from 32.0% to 30.0% for Claude Code. ContextSniper's pilot testing scripts are open-sourced at https://github.com/Calluking/ContextSniper

AI.tldr: ContextSniper is a token-efficient code memory layer for repository-level program repair that reduces LLM agent context usage by 38.9-51.5% through precision evidence selection and filtering.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents spend excessive context budgets on whole-file reads, broad searches, and long terminal outputs where useful evidence is mixed with irrelevant code and logs, making repository-level program repair inefficient and costly.
AI.method: ContextSniper implements Sniper feature for precision evidence selection: retrieves candidate code and runtime evidence, ranks with hybrid retrieval signals, filters long outputs through intention-aware context gate, and returns compact evidence packets while preserving recoverable source context outside the prompt.
AI.result: On SWE-bench Lite with OpenClaw and Claude Code, ContextSniper reduces total token use by 51.5% and logged cost by 36.4% for OpenClaw, and reduces total token use by 38.9% and estimated cost by 27.3% for Claude Code. Submitted-resolution rates decrease slightly from 26.0% to 24.0% for OpenClaw and from 32.0% to 30.0% for Claude Code.
AI.conclusion: ContextSniper significantly reduces token usage and costs for LLM-based repository repair while maintaining competitive resolution rates, demonstrating effective context optimization for code repair agents.

title: Expander Sparse Autoencoders: Parameter-Efficient Dictionaries for Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose internal activations of neural networks into sparse linear combinations of learned features by fitting an overcomplete dictionary $\mathbf{W}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ with $m<n$, and inferring a sparse code $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ from $\mathbf{h}\approx\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$. This inference problem closely resembles the canonical setup of compressed sensing, but dense decoders requires $O(mn)$ learned values, which becomes costly at large feature counts. We introduce Expander SAEs: TopK SAEs whose decoder and tied encoder are supported on a left-$d$-regular expander mask with $d\ll m$, learning only $dn$ decoder values while keeping the sparse-coding problem $(m,n,k)$ fixed. The same structure reduces storage and turns the matching-pursuit correlation step $\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{r}$ in OMP into an $O(dn)$ gather-and-reduce operation. Our experiments show that across Pythia-70M/160M, Qwen2.5-3B, and Llama-3.2-1B residual-stream activations, varying $d$ traces a consistent storage--fidelity frontier, and that at the most compressed modern-LM setting, Qwen2.5-3B with $d=7$ uses $293\times$ fewer learned decoder values than the full dense decoder while retaining $84$% of dense CE-loss recovered. Control experiments show that the improved storage--fidelity tradeoff is driven by sparse, diverse decoder support structure rather than by fewer learned decoder values, and that when sparse and dense decoders are compared at matched parameter count, part of the remaining gap comes from encoder amortisation. On the theoretical side, we show that expansion and column flatness are sufficient for identifiability of noiseless $k$-sparse codes, and we derive complementary sufficient conditions under which OMP recovers the support exactly.

AI.tldr: Expander SAEs: Sparse autoencoders with expander-based sparse decoders that reduce storage from O(mn) to O(dn) while maintaining sparse coding performance, achieving 293x compression with 84% fidelity retention.
AI.motivation: Standard sparse autoencoders use dense decoders requiring O(mn) learned parameters, which becomes prohibitively expensive at large feature counts. There's a need to reduce storage requirements while maintaining the sparse coding structure.
AI.method: Introduces Expander SAEs: TopK sparse autoencoders with decoder and tied encoder supported on a left-d-regular expander mask where d≪m. This reduces learned parameters from O(mn) to O(dn) while keeping the sparse-coding problem fixed. The expander structure enables efficient O(dn) gather-and-reduce operations for matching pursuit correlation.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple models (Pythia-70M/160M, Qwen2.5-3B, Llama-3.2-1B) show consistent storage-fidelity tradeoff curves. At most compressed setting (Qwen2.5-3B with d=7), uses 293× fewer learned decoder values while retaining 84% of dense CE-loss recovery. The improvement comes from sparse, diverse decoder structure rather than just parameter reduction. Theoretical guarantees provided for identifiability and exact support recovery under OMP.
AI.conclusion: Expander SAEs provide an efficient approach to sparse feature learning by leveraging expander graph structures to dramatically reduce storage requirements while maintaining sparse coding performance. The method achieves significant compression (293×) with high fidelity retention (84%), offering a practical solution for scaling sparse autoencoders to large feature counts in modern language models.

title: ElephantAgent: Contextual State Continuity in Agentic Systems
summary: Agentic systems enhance their capabilities by invoking external tools and maintaining persistent memory. However, these external dependencies introduce novel attack surfaces. Recent tool and memory poisoning attacks show that maliciously crafted tool descriptors and poisoned memory can covertly bias agent behavior. These threats reflect a deeper issue: the lack of verifiable continuity in the agent's contextual state for planning and execution. We present ElephantAgent, a protocol that enforces Contextual State Continuity to defend against contextual state poisoning. Inspired by prior state-continuity mechanisms (e.g., Nimble), ElephantAgent extends this protection to the evolving contextual state of agentic systems. We define the contextual state as the bounded, security-critical subset of the agent's entire context (e.g., tool state and memory). Before processing each query, ElephantAgent recomputes the digest of the local contextual state and verifies it against the latest authorized digest. Using replicated trusted hardware, ElephantAgent maintains a linearizable ledger of authorized contextual state transitions and detects out-of-band state tampering. To handle in-band semantic abuse, ElephantAgent additionally provides Historical Traceability, enabling conditional post-hoc audit and recovery to a known-good prior state.

AI.tldr: ElephantAgent is a protocol that defends against contextual state poisoning in agentic systems by enforcing Contextual State Continuity, maintaining a verifiable ledger of state transitions, and providing historical traceability for audits and recovery.
AI.motivation: Agentic systems that use external tools and persistent memory create new attack surfaces. Recent attacks show that malicious tool descriptors and poisoned memory can covertly bias agent behavior, revealing a fundamental problem: the lack of verifiable continuity in the agent's contextual state for planning and execution.
AI.method: ElephantAgent enforces Contextual State Continuity by: 1) Defining contextual state as the bounded, security-critical subset of agent context (tool state and memory), 2) Before each query, recomputing the digest of local contextual state and verifying against the latest authorized digest, 3) Using replicated trusted hardware to maintain a linearizable ledger of authorized state transitions, 4) Providing Historical Traceability for conditional post-hoc audit and recovery to known-good prior states.
AI.result: The protocol detects out-of-band state tampering and handles in-band semantic abuse through its verification mechanisms. It extends state-continuity protection (inspired by systems like Nimble) to the evolving contextual state of agentic systems.
AI.conclusion: ElephantAgent addresses the security vulnerabilities introduced by external dependencies in agentic systems through a comprehensive protocol that ensures contextual state integrity, enables tampering detection, and provides recovery mechanisms, thereby protecting against both out-of-band and semantic state poisoning attacks.

title: Do LLMs Truly Generalize in the Molecular Domain? A Perturbation-Based Analysis
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown promise in molecular discovery, yet a gap remains between their probabilistic nature over discrete sequential tokens and the rigid topological constraints of chemical space. This raises the question of whether molecular LLMs can generalize beyond the local neighborhoods induced by their sequence-based representations. To systematically investigate this question, we introduce a Molecular Perturbation framework that generates syntax-valid structural variants of training molecules under controlled Graph Edit Distance (GED) to probe the manifold regularity of molecular LLMs. Our analysis shows that even a single edit can cause substantial performance drops on common molecular tasks, revealing a narrow local trust region and fragile sensitivity to structural changes. Since similar molecules tend to exhibit similar properties, In-Context Tuning (ICT), which anchors predictions on structurally similar molecules, offers a natural way to mitigate such fragility. Our experiments also examine whether ICT confers robustness under controlled structural perturbations, and the results suggest that it can partially expand the local trust region and offer a promising direction for stabilizing molecular LLMs against structural variation.

AI.tldr: Molecular LLMs are fragile to structural changes despite syntax-valid edits, but In-Context Tuning can partially stabilize them by leveraging structural similarity.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental gap between LLMs' probabilistic token-level predictions and the rigid topological constraints of chemical space, raising questions about whether molecular LLMs can truly generalize beyond their sequence-based representations.
AI.method: Introduced a Molecular Perturbation framework that generates syntax-valid structural variants of training molecules under controlled Graph Edit Distance (GED) to systematically probe the manifold regularity of molecular LLMs, then tested In-Context Tuning as a mitigation strategy.
AI.result: Even single structural edits cause substantial performance drops on molecular tasks, revealing narrow local trust regions and fragile sensitivity to structural changes. In-Context Tuning can partially expand this trust region and stabilize LLMs against structural variation.
AI.conclusion: Molecular LLMs exhibit fragility to structural perturbations, but anchoring predictions on structurally similar molecules through In-Context Tuning offers a promising direction for improving their robustness and generalization in chemical space.

title: A-TMA: Decoupling State-Aware Memory Failures in Long-Term Agent Memory
summary: Long term memory lets LLM agents act as persistent assistants, but user facts change. A useful memory system must know what is true now, what used to be true, and what changed. We study \emph{ghost memory}, a state coordination failure in which old, current, and transition facts coexist in the memory bank, remain mixed during retrieval, and mislead the answer model. We argue that memory systems should be understood and optimized from three levels: bank maintenance, retrieval, and answer time resolution. We propose ATMA, a state aware overlay for existing memory systems. ATMA keeps superseded and transition records in the bank, builds evidence packets for the query's requested state view, and exposes current, historical, and transition labels to QA. We further call for decoupled evaluation of bank, retrieval, and answer level failures, since final QA accuracy can hide where ghost memory occurs. To make this failure measurable, we build LTP (LoCoMo Temporal Plus), a conflict heavy benchmark for ghost memory, and evaluate on LoCoMo for long conversation generalization. On LTP, Graphiti+ATMA improves conflict accuracy by 0.240 absolute over Graphiti. On LoCoMo, Graphiti+ATMA raises temporal F1 from 0.0295 to 0.1705. The gains are host dependent, but they indicate that explicit state roles can reduce memory failures hidden by final QA accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ATMA, a state-aware overlay for LLM memory systems that addresses ghost memory - when outdated, current, and transitional facts coexist and confuse retrieval. It improves temporal conflict resolution and proposes decoupled evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Long-term memory in LLM agents must track changing user facts, but current systems suffer from "ghost memory" failures where old, current, and transitional facts coexist in memory banks, get mixed during retrieval, and mislead answer generation.
AI.method: Proposes ATMA (state-aware overlay) that: 1) maintains superseded and transition records in memory bank, 2) builds evidence packets for specific state views requested by queries, and 3) exposes current, historical, and transition labels to QA systems. Also introduces LTP benchmark for measuring ghost memory failures.
AI.result: On LTP benchmark, Graphiti+ATMA improves conflict accuracy by 0.240 absolute over Graphiti alone. On LoCoMo benchmark, raises temporal F1 from 0.0295 to 0.1705. Shows explicit state roles can reduce memory failures hidden by final QA accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Memory systems should be optimized at three levels (bank maintenance, retrieval, answer resolution) and evaluated decoupledly. ATMA demonstrates that explicit state tracking and contextualization significantly reduces ghost memory failures in LLM long-term memory systems.

title: Gaming Consensus: Coordinated Manipulation in Crowdsourced Fact-Checking
summary: Crowdsourced fact-checking systems have been adopted by major social media companies such as X, Meta, TikTok and Google with the aim of combating misleading information at scale without relying on centralized editorial control. These systems have been developed around a common underlying concept: a bridging mechanism that identifies notes flagging misleading information when they receive support from people with different perspectives rather than simple majority support. To our knowledge the only publicly disclosed bridging algorithms deployed for fact-checking are based on matrix factorization, as deployed by both X and Meta, augmented with additional components addressing abuse, targeted manipulation, and contributor brigades. This work examines the core matrix factorization portion of these systems, presenting theoretical and empirical evaluations of the degree to which coordinated users could vote strategically by leveraging the latent representations to fabricate the appearance of synthetic consensus within the bridging mechanism. Using historic production data, we find that up to 10.7% of lower quality notes could be manipulated above consensus thresholds using less than 10 ratings. We complement these findings with a theoretical analysis, revealing counterintuitively that rating a note as "Not Helpful" can increase its helpfulness score, as well as a cost model quantifying manipulation effort. We have developed and deployed mitigations within X's Community Notes algorithm to address synthetic consensus.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes vulnerabilities in matrix factorization-based bridging algorithms used in crowdsourced fact-checking systems, showing how coordinated users can manipulate consensus scores with minimal effort.
AI.motivation: Major social media platforms (X, Meta, TikTok, Google) use crowdsourced fact-checking with bridging mechanisms that rely on support from diverse perspectives rather than simple majorities. These systems use matrix factorization algorithms to detect consensus across viewpoints, but their security against strategic manipulation needs investigation.
AI.method: The researchers conducted theoretical and empirical evaluations of matrix factorization bridging algorithms, using historic production data to test manipulation scenarios. They developed a cost model for manipulation effort and examined how strategic voting could create synthetic consensus.
AI.result: The study found that up to 10.7% of lower quality notes could be manipulated above consensus thresholds using less than 10 ratings. A counterintuitive finding shows that rating a note as "Not Helpful" can actually increase its helpfulness score due to algorithm dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Matrix factorization bridging algorithms in crowdsourced fact-checking systems are vulnerable to synthetic consensus manipulation by coordinated users. The authors have developed and deployed mitigations within X's Community Notes algorithm to address these vulnerabilities.

title: Atomic Task Graph: A Unified Framework for Agentic Planning and Execution
summary: LLM-based agents have shown strong potential for solving complex multi-step tasks, yet existing performance improvements often rely on either scaling to larger backbone models or task-specific fine-tuning. The former incurs substantial computational costs, while the latter typically generalizes poorly across different tasks. Although prompt-based control is training-free and broadly applicable, existing methods still leave input-output dependencies between subtasks implicit in textual trajectories, making verified intermediate results difficult to reuse. To address these limitations, we propose Atomic Task Graph (ATG), a unified control framework for planning and execution. Specifically, ATG maintains an explicit graph to expose dependencies and support reuse. During planning, it recursively decomposes a high-level task into subtasks, forming a sequence of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) whose evolution can be traced. During execution, the dependencies exposed by ATG allow independent branches to be executed in parallel, thereby improving execution efficiency. When failures are detected, ATG leverages the graph evolution history to localize the error source and repair only the affected region, preserving validated regions unchanged. Experiments show that ATG consistently outperforms strong baselines in success rate and execution efficiency across three interactive benchmarks using only 7B-8B backbones.

AI.tldr: ATG (Atomic Task Graph) is a unified control framework that uses explicit graph structures to manage task decomposition, parallel execution, and error recovery for LLM-based agents, achieving improved performance without scaling model size or task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for improving LLM-based agents have limitations: scaling to larger models is computationally expensive, task-specific fine-tuning generalizes poorly across tasks, and prompt-based methods leave input-output dependencies implicit in textual trajectories, making verified intermediate results difficult to reuse.
AI.method: Proposes Atomic Task Graph (ATG) framework that maintains explicit graph structures to expose dependencies and support reuse. It performs recursive task decomposition during planning (forming DAGs), enables parallel execution of independent branches, and uses graph evolution history for error localization and repair of only affected regions.
AI.result: ATG consistently outperforms strong baselines in success rate and execution efficiency across three interactive benchmarks using only 7B-8B backbone models, demonstrating effectiveness without model scaling or task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: ATG provides a unified, training-free control framework that addresses key limitations of existing approaches by making task dependencies explicit, enabling parallel execution, and supporting efficient error recovery, offering a practical alternative to model scaling or task-specific fine-tuning.

title: Many Voices, One Reward: Multi-Role Rubric Generation for LLM Judging and Reward Modeling
summary: Reliable reward and preference signals are critical for evaluating and optimizing large language models on open-ended tasks. Rubric-based judges offer a transparent way to decompose such judgments into explicit evaluation criteria, but existing annotation-free rubric generators typically rely on a single generic evaluator. As a result, they may overlook important dimensions of human preference, a failure mode we term dimensional blind spots. To address this limitation, we propose Multi-Role Rubric Generation (MRRG), a training-free and reference-free framework that elicits evaluation criteria from multiple complementary roles and consolidates them into an auditable rubric-based scorer. This scorer can be used both to validate pairwise preferences and to provide rewards for GRPO-style Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). Experiments on preference validation benchmarks show that MRRG consistently outperforms single-role rubric generation baselines across multiple backbone models. Further RLVR experiments demonstrate that MRRG yields a stronger reward signal for improving open-ended generation.

AI.tldr: MRRG is a training-free framework that generates rubrics from multiple roles to improve evaluation of LLMs on open-ended tasks, addressing dimensional blind spots of single-role approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing rubric generators rely on single generic evaluators, which can overlook important dimensions of human preference (dimensional blind spots). Rubric-based judges need to better capture comprehensive human preferences for evaluating LLMs on open-ended tasks.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Role Rubric Generation (MRRG) - a training-free, reference-free framework that elicits evaluation criteria from multiple complementary roles and consolidates them into an auditable rubric-based scorer for pairwise preference validation and RL rewards.
AI.result: MRRG consistently outperforms single-role rubric generation baselines on preference validation benchmarks across multiple backbone models. RLVR experiments show MRRG yields stronger reward signals for improving open-ended generation.
AI.conclusion: MRRG effectively addresses dimensional blind spots in rubric generation by incorporating multiple perspectives, providing better evaluation and reward signals for optimizing LLMs on open-ended tasks.

title: OntoLearner: A Modular Python Library for Ontology Learning with Large Language Models
comment: 30 pages. Under review at Nature Communications. This version is reformatted with a different section structure; content is unchanged
summary: Ontology learning (OL) aims to automatically construct structured knowledge models from text, yet progress remains fragmented across methods, domains, and evaluation practices. Despite decades of research, OL lacks a shared infrastructure for systematic evaluation and ontology access. This absence has hindered progress and fragmented research, leaving the central challenges of OL largely unaddressed. We introduce OntoLearner, a modular, cross-domain, and first-of-its-kind framework that unifies ontology access, large language model (LLM)-driven learning pipelines, and standardized benchmarking. OntoLearner releases 180 machine-readable ontologies spanning 22 domains and provides pipeline-ready datasets with train/dev/test splits for three core OL tasks: term typing, taxonomy discovery, and non-taxonomic relation extraction. Using this infrastructure, we conduct a large-scale empirical study of OL, evaluating 22 retrieval models and 12 LLMs across domains and tasks. The results converge on a finding that reframes the central challenge of OL: failure modes scale with ontological complexity rather than model size or architectural sophistication. The primary bottleneck is not model capability, but a structural mismatch between how models encode knowledge and how ontologies organize it. These findings establish that effective OL is reachable through the cross-domain, multi-task benchmarking enabled by OntoLearner. OntoLearner is open-source (MIT license) at https://github.com/sciknoworg/OntoLearner/.

AI.tldr: OntoLearner is a unified framework for ontology learning that provides standardized datasets, benchmarks 34 models across domains, and finds that ontological complexity rather than model capability is the primary bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Ontology learning research has been fragmented across methods, domains, and evaluation practices, lacking shared infrastructure for systematic evaluation. This fragmentation has hindered progress and left central challenges unaddressed.
AI.method: Created OntoLearner - a modular framework with: 1) 180 machine-readable ontologies across 22 domains, 2) pipeline-ready datasets with train/dev/test splits for three OL tasks (term typing, taxonomy discovery, non-taxonomic relation extraction), 3) standardized benchmarking infrastructure for evaluating 22 retrieval models and 12 LLMs.
AI.result: Large-scale empirical study reveals that failure modes scale with ontological complexity rather than model size or architectural sophistication. The main bottleneck is structural mismatch between how models encode knowledge and how ontologies organize it.
AI.conclusion: Effective ontology learning is achievable through cross-domain, multi-task benchmarking enabled by OntoLearner. The framework is open-source and provides the necessary infrastructure for systematic progress in OL research.

title: Adaptive Group-Based Counterfactual Explanations for Time-Series Rehabilitation Data
comment: To be published at IEEE CBMS 2026
summary: Counterfactual explanations (CEs) for multivariate time-series classifiers are often difficult to interpret in domains where experts reason in terms of semantic feature groups rather than individual channels. In rehabilitation movement analysis with multi-sensor inertial measurement units (IMUs), clinicians interpret motion through muscle-group and joint-segment abstractions; yet, most existing counterfactual methods operate at the channel level, producing scattered and biomechanically incoherent explanations. We propose a two-stage framework for group-based counterfactual generation in high-dimensional IMU data. We first show that Shapley-Adaptive (SA) group ranking preserves counterfactual validity but fails to enforce group-level sparsity, motivating the need for explicit group selection. We then introduce Learnable Gate (LG) methods, which incorporate trainable per-group relevance gates jointly optimized with perturbation masks. Experiments on the KneE-PAD rehabilitation dataset demonstrate that LG substantially improves modality-group sparsity compared to the channel-level M-CELS baseline while maintaining or improving validity, temporal smoothness, and generation efficiency. Exercise-specific analyses further show that group-structured counterfactuals yield concise, muscle-level corrective guidance aligned with clinical reasoning. Overall, the proposed framework enhances interpretability without sacrificing counterfactual quality, enabling more actionable explanations for rehabilitation movement analysis.

AI.tldr: Proposes a learnable gate framework for generating group-based counterfactual explanations in multivariate time-series IMU data, improving interpretability for rehabilitation movement analysis by focusing on semantic feature groups rather than individual channels.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual explanations for multivariate time-series classifiers are difficult to interpret when experts reason in terms of semantic feature groups (like muscle groups and joint segments) rather than individual sensor channels. In rehabilitation movement analysis with IMUs, clinicians interpret motion through biomechanical abstractions, but existing counterfactual methods operate at channel level, producing scattered and biomechanically incoherent explanations.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: First, shows that Shapley-Adaptive (SA) group ranking preserves validity but lacks group-level sparsity. Then introduces Learnable Gate (LG) methods with trainable per-group relevance gates jointly optimized with perturbation masks to enforce explicit group selection.
AI.result: Experiments on KneE-PAD rehabilitation dataset show LG substantially improves modality-group sparsity compared to channel-level M-CELS baseline while maintaining or improving validity, temporal smoothness, and generation efficiency. Exercise-specific analyses show group-structured counterfactuals yield concise, muscle-level corrective guidance aligned with clinical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances interpretability without sacrificing counterfactual quality, enabling more actionable explanations for rehabilitation movement analysis by providing group-based counterfactuals that match clinicians' biomechanical reasoning patterns.

title: Multimodal Knowledge Edit-Scoped Generalization for Online Recursive MLLM Editing
summary: Online multimodal knowledge editing requires injecting a continual stream of visual-textual corrections into multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with bounded overhead and minimal disruption to unrelated behaviors. Existing editors mainly emphasize edit reliability and long-horizon stability, but rarely control the semantic boundary of each edit. Our pilot analyses of post-edit behaviors and internal neuronal activities reveal a scope gap behind reliable edits: instance-level success neither guarantees transfer to valid cross-modal variants nor prevents leakage to unrelated inputs, while edit-related cross-modal responses concentrate in deeper semantic layers. Therefore, we formulate Edit-Scoped Generalization, reframing online MLLM editing from merely correcting an instance to controlling the propagation boundary of each edit. To this end, we propose ScopeEdit, a scope-aware online editor that decomposes each update into a modality-local absorption branch and an evidence-gated shared generalization branch. The local branch supports stable edit absorption, whereas the shared branch enables cross-modal propagation only when visual and textual evidence are sufficiently aligned. Both branches perform scope-separated write geometries in orthogonal low-rank spaces and maintain branch-wise preconditioners via Sherman--Morrison recursions, yielding constant per-edit overhead. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks, long-horizon edit streams, MLLM backbones, real-world VLKEB scenarios, and complex vision-language architectures show that ScopeEdit consistently improves the trade-off between in-scope cross-modal transfer and out-of-scope locality, while preserving edit reliability, stability and online efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/lab-klc/ScopeEdit.

AI.tldr: ScopeEdit is a scope-aware online multimodal knowledge editor for MLLMs that controls edit propagation boundaries via a two-branch architecture, balancing cross-modal transfer with out-of-scope locality while maintaining efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal knowledge editors focus on edit reliability and stability but fail to control semantic boundaries of edits, leading to scope gaps where edits leak to unrelated inputs or don't properly transfer to valid cross-modal variants.
AI.method: ScopeEdit decomposes each update into two branches: (1) modality-local absorption branch for stable edit absorption, and (2) evidence-gated shared generalization branch that enables cross-modal propagation only when visual and textual evidence align sufficiently. Both use scope-separated write geometries in orthogonal low-rank spaces with Sherman-Morrison recursions for constant overhead.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks, long-horizon edit streams, MLLM backbones, real-world scenarios, and complex vision-language architectures show ScopeEdit consistently improves the trade-off between in-scope cross-modal transfer and out-of-scope locality while preserving edit reliability, stability and online efficiency.
AI.conclusion: ScopeEdit successfully reframes online MLLM editing from instance correction to controlling edit propagation boundaries, addressing the scope gap problem through a novel scope-aware architecture that maintains efficiency while improving edit precision.

title: Decomposer: Learning to Decompile Symbolic Music to Programs
summary: Musical performance involves executing a set of high-level musical instructions, yet recovering those instructions from the performance is a challenging inverse problem. We present Decomposer, a post-training framework for symbolic music decompilation: the task of recovering executable, editable music programs from symbolic music. We instantiate the task as MIDI-to-Strudel decompilation, where the model takes symbolic MIDI as input and produces a program in Strudel, a music programming language, that reconstructs the input when executed. The task poses two challenges: Strudel is a low-resource language with little naturally paired MIDI-code data, and optimizing faithful reconstruction of MIDI alone can collapse to unreadable note-by-note transliteration. We address these challenges in two stages. First, we construct Strudel-Synth, a synthetic corpus of paired Strudel programs and rendered MIDI, and use it for supervised fine-tuning. Second, we refine the model with reinforcement learning on unpaired MIDI, optimizing rewards for both MIDI reconstruction faithfulness and code readability. Our evaluation across synthetic and real-world MIDI benchmarks shows that Decomposer achieves substantially higher MIDI reconstruction faithfulness than closed-source LLMs while producing more readable and diverse code than the heuristic converter.

AI.tldr: Decomposer is a framework that converts symbolic MIDI music into readable, editable Strudel music programs through synthetic data generation and reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Musical performances are based on high-level instructions, but recovering those instructions from performances is a challenging inverse problem. There's a need to convert symbolic MIDI music into editable music programs rather than just note-by-note transliterations.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Create Strudel-Synth synthetic corpus of paired Strudel programs and rendered MIDI for supervised fine-tuning, 2) Refine model with reinforcement learning on unpaired MIDI, optimizing for both MIDI reconstruction faithfulness and code readability.
AI.result: Decomposer achieves substantially higher MIDI reconstruction faithfulness than closed-source LLMs while producing more readable and diverse code than heuristic converters, evaluated across synthetic and real-world MIDI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully solves the challenging MIDI-to-Strudel decompilation task by combining synthetic data generation with reinforcement learning, enabling recovery of executable, editable music programs from symbolic performances.

title: Episodic-to-Semantic Consolidation Without Identity Drift
summary: Long-running adaptive intelligent agents face a structural tension between knowledge consolidation and information integrity. Memory consolidation is conventionally treated as an agent-changing operation: a model is fine-tuned, a prompt rewritten, a policy distilled, or a reflection appended to the context that governs future behaviour. In regulated autonomic deployment this is a liability because the agent operates under commitments and audit contracts that bind to a specific, cryptographically certified identity. We propose to treat consolidation not as a mutation of the planner or the identity manifest, but as a deterministic function f: M^ep -> M^sem over episodic memory whose output is a separately addressable semantic knowledge layer; the identity hash does not read M^sem, so consolidation updates knowledge without changing the agent's certified identity. We give a formal account of the agent representation, prove identity invariance through a structural lemma on the manifest's hash-input set, specify a deterministic aggregation algorithm whose outputs are auditable database rows with explicit confidence and supporting-event provenance, and validate the construction with synthetic experiments demonstrating per-field correctness, byte-equal identity across consolidation passes, and a mean 79.82% reduction in unproductive planner attempts (95% BCa CI [78.02%, 81.49%] across 10 seeds) against a calibrated Bayesian-shrunk baseline. The construction is a knowledge-update discipline for autonomic agents in which lessons accumulate as queryable facts while the agent's certified identity remains byte-equal across its operational lifetime, with an embodied service agent as the running case study.

AI.tldr: A framework for memory consolidation in autonomous agents that separates semantic knowledge from identity, allowing knowledge updates without changing the agent's certified cryptographic identity.
AI.motivation: Long-running intelligent agents face tension between consolidating knowledge and maintaining identity integrity. Traditional consolidation methods (fine-tuning, prompt rewriting, policy distillation) change the agent, which is problematic in regulated environments where agents operate under commitments and audit contracts tied to specific cryptographic identities.
AI.method: Treat consolidation as a deterministic function f: M^ep → M^sem that maps episodic memory to a separately addressable semantic knowledge layer. The identity hash does not read the semantic memory, so consolidation updates knowledge without changing the certified identity. Provide formal agent representation, prove identity invariance, and specify deterministic aggregation algorithm with auditable database rows containing confidence and provenance.
AI.result: Experiments show: 1) per-field correctness, 2) byte-equal identity across consolidation passes, and 3) 79.82% reduction in unproductive planner attempts (95% BCa CI [78.02%, 81.49%] across 10 seeds) against baseline. The method provides identity invariance while allowing knowledge accumulation.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables knowledge accumulation as queryable facts while maintaining byte-equal certified identity across the agent's operational lifetime, addressing the structural tension between consolidation and identity integrity for autonomous agents in regulated environments.

title: Learning the Supports for Categorical Critic in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Value functions are an essential component in actor-critic based deep reinforcement learning (RL). Conventionally, these functions are trained as a regression task by minimising the mean squared error (MSE) relative to bootstrapped target values. Meanwhile, in distributional RL, a distribution of returns is modelled based on the distributional Bellman operator. This work investigates the Gaussian Histogram Loss (HL-Gauss), a recent approach that reframes value estimation as classification by encoding each scalar Bellman target as a Gaussian-smoothed categorical target. Despite its potential, applying histogram-based losses to RL presents inherent challenges, most notably the requirement to pre-define a fixed support interval, which is often complicated by the non-stationary and stochastic nature of target values typically found in RL tasks. In this work, we propose an approach that dynamically learns the lower and upper bounds of the support instead of assigning them beforehand. We derive an objective that jointly learns these bounds whilst learning the categorical representation of the scalar values, and we show that this objective forms an upper bound on the mean-squared Bellman error. Our theoretical analysis further shows that this bound is tighter than that of non-learned supports of HL-Gauss. Empirically, the proposed objective enables stable adaptation of the support interval and matches HL-Gauss-based actor-critic algorithms on most continuous-control tasks whilst improving on a subset, without requiring a pre-specified support interval.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a dynamic support interval learning approach for histogram-based value functions in RL, replacing the need for pre-defined bounds with learnable parameters to better handle non-stationary value distributions.
AI.motivation: Current histogram-based methods in RL require pre-defining fixed support intervals, which is problematic due to the non-stationary and stochastic nature of target values in RL tasks. This limitation restricts the flexibility and effectiveness of histogram approaches.
AI.method: The authors propose a method that dynamically learns the lower and upper bounds of the support interval instead of assigning them beforehand. They derive an objective that jointly learns these bounds while learning the categorical representation of scalar values, forming an upper bound on the mean-squared Bellman error.
AI.result: Theoretically, the proposed bound is tighter than that of non-learned supports in HL-Gauss. Empirically, the method enables stable adaptation of support intervals, matches HL-Gauss-based actor-critic algorithms on most continuous-control tasks, and improves performance on a subset of tasks without requiring pre-specified support intervals.
AI.conclusion: The dynamic support learning approach successfully addresses a key limitation of histogram-based RL methods by enabling adaptive bound estimation, maintaining or improving performance while eliminating the need for manual support interval specification.

title: Traceable Fault Diagnosis for Battery Energy Storage Systems via Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent O&M Assistant
summary: Large-scale battery energy storage systems (BESSs) require O&M decisions that combine alarms, cell-level measurements, device topology, diagnostic tables, historical cases, and maintenance documents. Monitoring platforms can flag threshold violations, but they often cannot explain whether voltage inconsistency, resistance drift, short-circuit risk, capacity divergence, or thermal abnormality needs intervention. This digest presents a traceable BESS fault-diagnosis assistant that uses retrieval-augmented multi-agent reasoning to connect operational data, domain knowledge, visual evidence, and report generation. Reliability is improved through BESS-specific task routing, schema-constrained natural-language database access, hybrid text-image retrieval, and evidence-based answer synthesis. Preliminary internal evaluation is reported for routing, database access, and diagnostic reasoning.

AI.tldr: A traceable BESS fault-diagnosis assistant using retrieval-augmented multi-agent reasoning to connect operational data, domain knowledge, and visual evidence for improved reliability in battery energy storage systems.
AI.motivation: Large-scale BESSs require complex O&M decisions combining various data sources, but existing monitoring platforms can flag issues without explaining whether interventions are actually needed for voltage inconsistency, resistance drift, short-circuit risk, capacity divergence, or thermal abnormalities.
AI.method: Uses retrieval-augmented multi-agent reasoning with BESS-specific task routing, schema-constrained natural-language database access, hybrid text-image retrieval, and evidence-based answer synthesis to connect operational data, domain knowledge, visual evidence, and report generation.
AI.result: Preliminary internal evaluation shows the system works for routing, database access, and diagnostic reasoning, creating a traceable fault-diagnosis assistant that improves reliability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent reasoning approach enables more reliable and explainable fault diagnosis for BESSs by integrating diverse data sources and domain knowledge with traceable evidence-based reasoning.

title: Regularized Variational and Spectral Log-Density-Ratio Estimation in the Gaussian Location Model
summary: We study ridge-regularized log-density-ratio estimation in the Gaussian location model with a common covariance matrix. By affine invariance, the model is written as q $\sim$ N(0, I), p $\sim$ N($Δ$, I), with linear features, where $Δ$ is a mean vector. The variational estimator is the empirical Kullback-Leibler (KL) log-normalized fit with a squared L2-penalty on its nonconstant coefficient, and the spectral estimator recently introduced in [1] replaces a single variational problem by a continuum of ridge-regularized least-squares problems. We derive high-dimensional deterministic asymptotic equivalents when the numbers of observations and dimension tend to infinity with fixed ratios. The regularized variational limit is characterized by a scalar entropy minimization problem derived from the convex-Gaussian-min-max theorem (CGMT), while the regularized spectral limit follows from deterministic equivalents for resolvents of weighted sums of two independent Gaussian sample covariance matrices. We use these formulas to compare population risks, with experiments focused on fixed-signal aspect-ratio sweeps and optimized regularization. Our conclusion is that with many observations, under the criteria and asymptotic regimes analyzed here, the well-specified variational estimator has the smaller risk, while with fewer observations, the spectral estimator is favored because its covariance-based construction has lower variance. We also study how a nuclear penalty can be used and partially analyzed to perform feature learning.

AI.tldr: Study compares ridge-regularized log-density-ratio estimators (variational vs spectral) in Gaussian location model, showing variational better with many observations, spectral better with fewer due to lower variance.
AI.motivation: Need to understand performance of different regularized log-density-ratio estimation methods in high-dimensional Gaussian location models, particularly comparing variational and spectral approaches under different observation regimes.
AI.method: Two estimators: variational (empirical KL fit with L2 penalty) and spectral (ridge-regularized least-squares continuum). Analyzed using high-dimensional asymptotic equivalents, CGMT theory, and deterministic equivalents for resolvents of weighted Gaussian sample covariance matrices.
AI.result: Variational estimator has smaller risk with many observations, while spectral estimator is favored with fewer observations due to lower variance from covariance-based construction. Also explored nuclear penalty for feature learning.
AI.conclusion: Choice between variational and spectral estimators depends on observation count: variational superior in data-rich regimes, spectral better in data-scarce regimes. Regularization and feature learning via nuclear penalty can further improve performance.

title: InduceKV: Fixed-Footprint Continual Adaptation of Multimodal LLMs via Inducing KV Memories
summary: Multimodal large language models must adapt to evolving tasks and domains, yet continual improvement under bounded deployment footprint remains difficult because repeated parameter updates or growing replay stores can accumulate adaptation state over time. We study fixed-footprint continual adaptation: the deployed adaptation state is kept under a fixed memory budget, while the backbone model is left unchanged and task-specific updates are externalized. We propose InduceKV, a retrieval-based method that stores each selected training prefix as an attention-ready memory entry, consisting of a frozen retrieval key and compact layerwise key--value (KV) payloads that can be appended to the model's self-attention cache. Under a strict memory budget, InduceKV constructs a compact inducing set through bilevel selection: a lightweight calibration is fit for retrieval, while the selected memory balances current-task likelihood, anchor-based retention, and coverage in the frozen retrieval space. Across task-incremental instruction tuning, continual VQA, domain-incremental adaptation, and lifelong multimodal instruction tuning, InduceKV consistently improves over PEFT, MoE, replay, and prompt-retrieval baselines under matched memory budgets. We further report backbone-matched, stage-1 CoIN, compute-matched, and scalability diagnostics, showing that the gains are not due to a stronger backbone, replay alone, or an unbounded candidate pool.

AI.tldr: InduceKV: A retrieval-based method for fixed-footprint continual adaptation of multimodal LLMs that stores training prefixes as attention-ready memory entries with frozen retrieval keys and compact KV payloads, achieving better performance than various baselines under strict memory budgets.
AI.motivation: Multimodal large language models need to adapt to evolving tasks and domains, but current continual learning approaches face challenges because repeated parameter updates or growing replay stores accumulate adaptation state over time, making it difficult to maintain a bounded deployment footprint.
AI.method: InduceKV stores selected training prefixes as attention-ready memory entries consisting of frozen retrieval keys and compact layerwise key-value (KV) payloads that can be appended to the model's self-attention cache. Under fixed memory budgets, it uses bilevel selection: lightweight calibration for retrieval, and memory selection balancing current-task likelihood, anchor-based retention, and coverage in the frozen retrieval space.
AI.result: InduceKV consistently outperforms PEFT, MoE, replay, and prompt-retrieval baselines across task-incremental instruction tuning, continual VQA, domain-incremental adaptation, and lifelong multimodal instruction tuning under matched memory budgets. Diagnostic experiments show gains are not due to stronger backbones, replay alone, or unbounded candidate pools.
AI.conclusion: InduceKV provides an effective solution for fixed-footprint continual adaptation of multimodal LLMs, enabling efficient adaptation to new tasks and domains while maintaining strict memory constraints through its compact inducing set and retrieval-based approach.

title: Rank-Then-Act: Reward-Free Control from Frame-Order Progress
summary: We introduce Rank-Then-Act (RTA), a framework for learning control policies from expert video demonstrations without environment rewards. RTA trains a Vision-Language Model (VLM) offline as a progress-based ordinal scorer, using a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) objective over shuffled frame sequences, which forces the model to recover temporal ordering from visual semantics rather than trivial time cues. Importantly, instead of using the scorer directly as a scalar reward model, we propose a correlation-based reward function for reinforcement learning: at each interaction window, we compute the Spearman rank correlation between predicted progress rankings and true temporal indices, yielding a bounded, scale-invariant learning signal. This design decouples reward learning from absolute calibration and enables stable transfer across tasks and environments. We evaluate RTA on discrete control benchmarks (PyBoy: Catrap, Kirby) and continuous control tasks (PointMaze, MetaWorld). RTA consistently matches or outperforms prior video-based reward learning methods and rank-based baselines, while demonstrating strong cross-task reuse of a single pretrained progress scorer. Our results suggest that correlation-structured supervision over video-derived ordinal signals is sufficient for policy learning, offering a scalable alternative to explicit reward design.

AI.tldr: Rank-Then-Act (RTA) learns control policies from expert videos by first training a Vision-Language Model to rank video frames temporally, then using correlation-based rewards for reinforcement learning instead of direct scalar rewards.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of learning control policies from expert video demonstrations without environment rewards, aiming to avoid the need for explicit reward design and enable scalable policy learning from visual data alone.
AI.method: RTA uses a two-stage approach: (1) Train a Vision-Language Model as an ordinal progress scorer using Group Relative Policy Optimization over shuffled video frames to learn temporal ordering from visual semantics, (2) Use correlation-based rewards (Spearman rank correlation between predicted rankings and true temporal indices) during reinforcement learning rather than direct scalar rewards from the scorer.
AI.result: RTA consistently matches or outperforms prior video-based reward learning methods and rank-based baselines on discrete control benchmarks (PyBoy: Catrap, Kirby) and continuous control tasks (PointMaze, MetaWorld), while demonstrating strong cross-task reuse of a single pretrained progress scorer.
AI.conclusion: Correlation-structured supervision over video-derived ordinal signals is sufficient for policy learning, offering a scalable alternative to explicit reward design that decouples reward learning from absolute calibration and enables stable transfer across tasks and environments.

title: Hidden Forgetting in Continual Multimodal Learning: When Accuracy Survives but Grounding Fails
summary: Multimodal large language models must continually adapt to evolving tasks and domains, yet standard continual learning metrics mainly measure whether old answers remain correct, leaving the stability of multimodal grounding largely unexamined. We study this overlooked failure mode and ask whether a continually adapted MLLM can preserve not only what it answers, but also how it uses visual, textual, OCR, chart, and document evidence. We identify \emph{hidden evidence-use forgetting}, where answer accuracy is retained while the model silently shifts toward different or less grounded evidence channels, and propose \textsc{RCL}, a replay-free reliance-constrained continual learning framework. \textsc{RCL} freezes the previous checkpoint as a behavioral reference, estimates teacher and student evidence-reliance profiles through counterfactual channel interventions, and jointly optimizes task learning, prediction preservation, and reliance preservation without adding inference-time cost. Across CoIN, COAST, MCITlib, and an evidence-sensitive multimodal stream, \textsc{RCL} consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over replay-free, PEFT, routing, and memory-assisted baselines, while substantially lowering modality reliance drift, dominant evidence flips, and hidden forgetting rates. These results suggest that robust continual multimodal learning requires preserving the evidence path behind correct answers, not merely the answers themselves.

AI.tldr: RCL framework addresses multimodal forgetting in continual learning by preserving not just answer accuracy but also evidence usage patterns across visual, textual, OCR, chart, and document channels.
AI.motivation: Current continual learning metrics for multimodal LLMs focus only on answer correctness while ignoring whether models maintain consistent evidence usage across modalities. This creates "hidden evidence-use forgetting" where answers remain correct but the model shifts to different or less grounded evidence channels.
AI.method: RCL is a replay-free reliance-constrained continual learning framework that freezes previous checkpoints as behavioral references, estimates teacher-student evidence-reliance profiles through counterfactual channel interventions, and jointly optimizes task learning, prediction preservation, and reliance preservation.
AI.result: RCL consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting compared to replay-free, PEFT, routing, and memory-assisted baselines across multiple datasets (CoIN, COAST, MCITlib, and evidence-sensitive multimodal streams). It substantially lowers modality reliance drift, dominant evidence flips, and hidden forgetting rates.
AI.conclusion: Robust continual multimodal learning requires preserving both correct answers AND the evidence paths behind them. The proposed RCL framework effectively addresses hidden evidence-use forgetting without adding inference-time costs.

title: SABER: A Semantic-Aligned Brain Network Analysis Framework via Multi-scale Hypergraphs
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Multimedia and Expo (ICME) 2026;
summary: Effective brain disease diagnosis requires the synergy of brain connectivity patterns and high-level semantic knowledge. Existing methods, however, largely treat semantics from large language models (LLMs) as auxiliary features or supervision, limiting their direct role in decision-making and constraining classification stability and robustness. To overcome this, we propose a semantic-aligned brain network framework that actively integrates LLM-derived semantics into the prediction process. Specifically, ROI-level semantics are first incorporated via global self-attention to enrich node representations and provide whole-brain context. Multi-scale hypergraphs are then constructed to explicitly model functional subnetworks and multi-ROI interactions, addressing the locality limitations of traditional GNNs and capturing high-order dependencies. Finally, a decision-level semantic alignment mechanism selectively injects patient-specific textual embeddings into graph representations, enabling semantics to directly guide predictions without perturbing the underlying network structure. Experiments on public brain network datasets ABIDE and ADHD-200 demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, enhanced stability, and improved interpretability, particularly in small-sample settings.

AI.tldr: A semantic-aligned brain network framework that integrates LLM-derived semantics into brain disease diagnosis through ROI-level semantic enrichment, multi-scale hypergraphs, and decision-level semantic alignment for improved classification stability and robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing methods treat semantics from LLMs as auxiliary features or supervision only, limiting their direct role in decision-making and constraining classification stability and robustness for brain disease diagnosis.
AI.method: 1. ROI-level semantics are incorporated via global self-attention to enrich node representations with whole-brain context. 2. Multi-scale hypergraphs are constructed to model functional subnetworks and multi-ROI interactions, addressing traditional GNN locality limitations. 3. Decision-level semantic alignment selectively injects patient-specific textual embeddings into graph representations to directly guide predictions without perturbing network structure.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on public brain network datasets ABIDE and ADHD-200, demonstrating enhanced stability, improved interpretability, and particularly strong results in small-sample settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semantic-aligned brain network framework effectively integrates LLM-derived semantics into the prediction process, overcoming limitations of existing methods and improving brain disease diagnosis through more stable, robust, and interpretable classification.

title: PACE: A Proxy for Agentic Capability Evaluation
summary: Evaluating LLM agents on benchmarks like SWE-Bench and GAIA can be expensive, time-consuming, and requires complex infrastructure. A single evaluation can cost thousands of dollars and take days to complete. In contrast, non-agentic LLM benchmarks that test individual capabilities (e.g., reasoning, code generation) are fast and cheap to run. In this paper, we investigate whether performance on expensive agentic benchmarks can be accurately predicted by the performance on a small, carefully selected subset of atomic evaluation instances. We introduce PACE, a framework that constructs proxy benchmarks by selecting instances from existing non-agentic evaluations whose aggregate scores most reliably predict model performances on agentic benchmarks. Given a pool of candidate instances spanning atomic capabilities, PACE fits a regression that maps a model's scores on a compact subset of source instances to its score on the target agentic benchmark. The subset itself is curated by combining two complementary instance-selection strategies, target-relevance local selection and globally informative global selection. We apply PACE to the 4 target agentic benchmarks in this paper, which yields PACE-Bench, the concrete proxy benchmark that we evaluate in the paper. Experiments across 14 models, 4 agentic benchmarks, and 19 non-agentic benchmarks show that PACE-Bench predicts agentic scores with leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) mean absolute error (MAE) under 4%, Spearman correlation above 0.80, and pairwise model-ranking accuracy around 85%, all at much less than 1% of the full agentic evaluation cost. We further analyze the selected proxy instances, revealing which skills each agentic benchmark uniquely demands. PACE enables practitioners to obtain reliable estimates of agentic performance during model development, selection, and routing, without the overhead of full agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: PACE is a framework that predicts expensive agentic benchmark performance using cheap atomic evaluations by selecting key proxy instances that correlate with agentic capabilities.
AI.motivation: Agentic LLM benchmarks like SWE-Bench and GAIA are expensive (thousands of dollars), time-consuming (days), and require complex infrastructure, while atomic non-agentic evaluations are fast and cheap but don't directly measure agentic performance.
AI.method: PACE constructs proxy benchmarks by selecting instances from existing non-agentic evaluations whose aggregate scores predict agentic benchmark performance. It uses regression mapping from compact source instances to target agentic scores, combining target-relevance local selection and globally informative global selection strategies.
AI.result: PACE-Bench predicts agentic scores with LOOCV MAE under 4%, Spearman correlation above 0.80, and pairwise model-ranking accuracy around 85%, at less than 1% of full agentic evaluation cost across 14 models, 4 agentic benchmarks, and 19 non-agentic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PACE enables reliable estimation of agentic performance during model development, selection, and routing without the overhead of full agent evaluation, revealing which specific skills each agentic benchmark uniquely demands through proxy instance analysis.

title: Population-Based Multi-Objective Training of Discriminators for Semi-Supervised GANs
comment: The 2nd International Conference on Federated Learning and Intelligent Computing Systems (FLICS2026)
summary: Semi-supervised generative adversarial networks (SSL-GANs) can exploit large unlabeled datasets while retaining a classifier in the discriminator, but their training is often unstable. This paper proposes a population-based evolutionary training strategy in which discriminator learning is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem. Instead of aggregating the supervised and unsupervised components of the SSL objective into a single scalar loss, the method maintains a population of discriminators ranked by Pareto dominance, enabling the exploration of different trade-offs between classification accuracy and real/fake discrimination. This formulation aims to improve both roles of SSL-GANs: learning accurate classifiers and training generators capable of producing realistic samples. We analyze several variants, including an elitist strategy and a mono-objective ablation, to assess the role of multi-objective selection. Experiments on MNIST with limited labels show improved training robustness compared to SSL-GAN and CE-SSL-GAN state-of-the-art baselines, while the elitist variant consistently achieves the highest classification accuracy.

AI.tldr: Population-based evolutionary training strategy for SSL-GANs that treats discriminator learning as multi-objective optimization, improving both classification accuracy and sample generation quality compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Semi-supervised GANs (SSL-GANs) can leverage large unlabeled datasets while maintaining a classifier, but they suffer from unstable training. Existing approaches aggregate supervised and unsupervised components into a single loss, which may not optimally balance the dual objectives of classification and real/fake discrimination.
AI.method: Proposes a population-based evolutionary training strategy where discriminator learning is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem. Instead of a single scalar loss, the method maintains a population of discriminators ranked by Pareto dominance to explore different trade-offs between classification accuracy and real/fake discrimination. Includes analysis of several variants including elitist strategy and mono-objective ablation.
AI.result: Experiments on MNIST with limited labels show improved training robustness compared to SSL-GAN and CE-SSL-GAN state-of-the-art baselines. The elitist variant consistently achieves the highest classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Multi-objective formulation via population-based evolutionary training improves SSL-GAN stability and performance, with the elitist strategy offering the best classification results while maintaining good generative capabilities.

title: Algebraic Model Counting for Global Analysis of Optimal Decision Trees
comment: Proc. Joint European Conference on Machine Learning and Principles and Practice of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (ECML-PKDD 2026), LNCS, Naples, Italy, 7-11 September 2026
summary: Ensuring model reliability in Explainable AI requires a global assessment of the hypothesis space. We propose a formal framework for the exhaustive analysis of optimal and near-optimal decision trees, called Algebraic Decision Tree Counting (ADTC). Inspired by Algebraic Model Counting (AMC) in knowledge representation, ADTC reformulates diverse analytical tasks, such as optimization, counting, and sampling, into a unified sum-of-products computation over a semiring $R$. While the hypothesis space of decision trees is doubly exponential with respect to the maximum depth $Δ$, our dynamic programming algorithm achieves $O^*(n^{O(Δ)})$ time complexity in the number of features $n$, where $O^*$ suppresses polynomial factors. To handle complex constraints consisting of multiple tree metrics, we introduce model behavior tensors that aggregate semiring values via convolution products over a tensor semiring. This algebraic approach efficiently constructs a model profile that captures the global landscape and trade-offs between criteria such as accuracy, size, and fairness. We demonstrate the utility of our software, emtrees, on real-world datasets, illustrating how ADTC facilitates evidence-based model selection in sensitive domains.

AI.tldr: ADTC is an algebraic framework for exhaustive analysis of optimal/near-optimal decision trees by reformulating analytical tasks into unified sum-of-products computations over semirings.
AI.motivation: Ensuring model reliability in Explainable AI requires global assessment of hypothesis spaces, particularly for decision trees in sensitive domains where evidence-based model selection is crucial.
AI.method: Introduces Algebraic Decision Tree Counting (ADTC) inspired by Algebraic Model Counting, reformulating tasks into sum-of-products computations over semirings. Uses dynamic programming with O*(n^O(Δ)) complexity and model behavior tensors with convolution products for handling multiple constraints.
AI.result: Develops an efficient framework that captures global landscape and trade-offs between criteria like accuracy, size, and fairness. Demonstrates utility through software 'emtrees' on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: ADTC provides a formal algebraic framework for exhaustive decision tree analysis, enabling evidence-based model selection in sensitive domains by efficiently exploring optimal/near-optimal hypothesis spaces despite doubly exponential search spaces.

title: Zeus: Towards Tuning-Free Foundation Model for Time Series Analysis
summary: We present Zeus, a unified tuning-free Time Series Foundation Model (TSFM) that delivers superior performance across diverse analysis tasks without any task-specific fine-tuning. Unlike prior studies that primarily focus on zero-shot forecasting but require task-specific tuning for other tasks, Zeus bridges this gap by addressing two fundamental challenges in multi-task generalization. First, to reconcile point-level granularity with long-sequence scalability, Zeus incorporates a multi-scale Transformer featuring point-wise tokenization and a U-shaped hierarchy, effectively balancing fine-grained fidelity with computational efficiency. Second, to accommodate varying inductive biases across different tasks, Zeus introduces Multi-Objective Temporal Masking (MOTM), a unified strategy that supports heterogeneous tasks (e.g., extrapolation, interpolation, and global abstraction) within a single framework. Extensive experiments across five representative tasks demonstrate that Zeus consistently achieves competitive results in tuning-free settings, underscoring its potential as a general-purpose TSFM.

AI.tldr: Zeus is a tuning-free Time Series Foundation Model that achieves strong performance across multiple analysis tasks without task-specific fine-tuning, using multi-scale Transformers and unified temporal masking.
AI.motivation: Prior TSFMs mainly focus on zero-shot forecasting but require task-specific tuning for other tasks. There's a gap in developing models that can handle diverse time series analysis tasks (extrapolation, interpolation, abstraction) without per-task fine-tuning.
AI.method: Zeus uses: 1) Multi-scale Transformer with point-wise tokenization and U-shaped hierarchy to balance fine-grained detail with computational efficiency, and 2) Multi-Objective Temporal Masking (MOTM) to support heterogeneous tasks within a single framework.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across five representative tasks show Zeus consistently achieves competitive results in tuning-free settings, demonstrating its effectiveness as a general-purpose TSFM.
AI.conclusion: Zeus successfully bridges the multi-task generalization gap in time series analysis, offering a unified, tuning-free foundation model with strong performance across diverse tasks, making it a promising general-purpose TSFM.

title: Evidence-State Rewards for Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Long-context reasoning requires models to locate, revise, and synthesize evidence distributed across lengthy inputs. Existing long-context RL methods usually reward final answers or static evidence extraction, offering little feedback on how intermediate actions change the model's evidence state. We propose Maven, a reinforcement learning framework with an editable evidence memory. Maven defines an answer-conditioned evidence-state value and rewards action-level state transitions: add actions are credited by marginal gain and hindsight contribution, link actions by evidence synergy, and drop actions by improved answer support after removing misleading evidence. These rewards are assigned to the corresponding action spans in GRPO. Across Llama and Qwen models on LongBench v2, LongReason, and RULER, Maven outperforms outcome-only RL and evidence-identification baselines, producing more sufficient evidence sets and lower distractor retention. Our results show that long-context RL benefits from optimizing stateful evidence navigation rather than one-shot evidence extraction.

AI.tldr: Maven: RL framework with editable evidence memory that rewards action-level state transitions for long-context reasoning, outperforming outcome-only RL and evidence-identification baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context RL methods only reward final answers or static evidence extraction, providing little feedback on how intermediate actions affect evidence state. This limits effective evidence navigation across lengthy inputs.
AI.method: Propose Maven with editable evidence memory that defines answer-conditioned evidence-state value. Rewards action-level state transitions: add actions by marginal gain/hindsight contribution, link actions by evidence synergy, drop actions by improved answer support after removing misleading evidence. Uses GRPO for assigning rewards to action spans.
AI.result: Outperforms outcome-only RL and evidence-identification baselines across Llama and Qwen models on LongBench v2, LongReason, and RULER benchmarks. Produces more sufficient evidence sets and lower distractor retention.
AI.conclusion: Long-context RL benefits more from optimizing stateful evidence navigation than one-shot evidence extraction. Action-level feedback on evidence state transitions is crucial for effective long-context reasoning.

title: Conditional Co-Ablation: Recovering Self-Repair Backups in Transformer Circuits
summary: Mechanistic interpretability often relies on component-level interventions to discover how a model produces a behavior. This guides attribution, capability knockout, and model pruning downstream to operate by scoring each unit by the effect of ablation in isolation. Such first-order scoring is natural when component importance is additive, but becomes misleading when a transformer self-repairs: after a primary component is removed, a dormant backup can take over, muting the primary's measured effect while the backup itself appears irrelevant on the intact model. We recast this failure as a recovery task, conditional circuit completion, and introduce Conditional Co-Ablation (CoAx), a label-free, output-grounded score that asks how much each remaining unit's ablation effect grows once a primary set has been removed. This conditional growth exposes the second-order interaction that single-unit scores discard. On the GPT-2-small IOI circuit, CoAx raises backup-head recovery from 0.33 to 0.91 ROC-AUC, outperforming all baselines, including self-repair-aware gradient scores (best 0.82); counterfactual patching verifies that the recovered heads causally carry the repair. The same label-free procedure transfers to induction across eight models. Beyond discovery, the recovered backups correct self-repair-masked attribution, identify the components required for capability knockout, and yield repair-aware structured pruning scaling from 124M to 7B. Component importance is therefore not merely an isolated-unit property: in robust circuits, the components that matter can become visible only under the interventions that make them necessary.

AI.tldr: CoAx is a new interpretability method that reveals backup components in transformer circuits by measuring how unit ablation effects change when other components are already removed, addressing the self-repair problem where dormant backups mask component importance.
AI.motivation: Current interpretability methods that score components by ablation in isolation fail when transformers self-repair. When a primary component is removed, dormant backups can take over, making the primary appear less important and the backup appear irrelevant, leading to misleading measurements.
AI.method: The paper introduces Conditional Co-Ablation (CoAx), a label-free, output-grounded scoring method that measures how much each remaining unit's ablation effect grows after a primary set has been removed. This conditional growth captures second-order interactions that single-unit scores miss.
AI.result: On GPT-2-small IOI circuit, CoAx improved backup-head recovery from 0.33 to 0.91 ROC-AUC, outperforming all baselines including self-repair-aware gradient scores (best 0.82). The method successfully transferred to induction across eight models and enabled repair-aware structured pruning from 124M to 7B parameters.
AI.conclusion: Component importance in transformer circuits is not an isolated-unit property - in robust circuits, the components that matter become visible only under interventions that make them necessary. CoAx successfully addresses the self-repair problem in mechanistic interpretability.

title: SUNTA: Hierarchical Video Prediction with Surprise-based Chunking
summary: Hierarchical state-space models (HSSMs) offer a promising approach to long-horizon prediction by segmenting sequences into temporal chunks. However, their performance hinges on how chunk boundaries are determined. While prior HSSMs typically rely on fixed-length chunking or similarity-based boundary detection, these methods often misalign with the intrinsic temporal structure of the data. We argue that chunking should instead be driven by prediction errors, which more directly indicate when longer-range context becomes necessary.
