Nevertheless, integrating surprise-based chunking into HSSMs introduces critical challenges, including hierarchical collapse during end-to-end training and the absence of surprise signals during open-loop prediction. To address these issues, we propose Surprise-based Nested Temporal Abstraction (SUNTA), a method that employs a decoupled training strategy to preserve surprise signals and uses internal inconsistency as a top-down surprise metric to determine chunk boundaries within imagined rollouts. Experiments on video prediction tasks in 2D and 3D environments demonstrate that SUNTA outperforms baselines, uniquely maintaining accurate predictions over 250 timesteps, whereas all baselines degrade within the first 10 timesteps.

AI.tldr: SUNTA introduces surprise-based chunking for hierarchical state-space models, using internal inconsistency as a surprise metric to determine temporal boundaries during open-loop prediction, achieving long-horizon prediction accuracy where baselines fail.
AI.motivation: Current hierarchical state-space models use fixed-length chunking or similarity-based boundary detection, which often misalign with the intrinsic temporal structure of data. The authors argue that prediction errors (surprise) better indicate when longer-range context is needed, but integrating surprise-based chunking faces challenges of hierarchical collapse during training and lack of surprise signals during open-loop prediction.
AI.method: The proposed SUNTA method employs a decoupled training strategy to preserve surprise signals and uses internal inconsistency as a top-down surprise metric to determine chunk boundaries within imagined rollouts. This approach addresses the issues of hierarchical collapse during end-to-end training and the absence of surprise signals during open-loop prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on video prediction tasks in 2D and 3D environments show that SUNTA outperforms baselines, uniquely maintaining accurate predictions over 250 timesteps, while all baselines degrade within the first 10 timesteps.
AI.conclusion: Surprise-based chunking driven by internal inconsistency is an effective approach for hierarchical state-space models, enabling accurate long-horizon predictions by better aligning chunk boundaries with the intrinsic temporal structure of data.

title: Hybrid quantum-classical neural network for sentiment analysis
summary: Quantum machine learning has recently emerged as a promising paradigm that leverages the expressive power of quantum circuits to address complex learning tasks. In this work, we investigate the applicability of hybrid quantum-classical neural networks to sentiment analysis, a central problem in natural language processing. We focus on a dataset of tweets related to COVID-19, where the textual content is vectorized using TF-IDF and fed into both classical feedforward networks and hybrid architectures incorporating parameterized quantum circuits. Our results show that hybrid models can achieve accuracy comparable to the classical baseline, while exhibiting distinct learning dynamics, especially in terms of validation loss and accuracy, that suggest a richer representational capacity. Moreover, when applying transfer learning to an SMS spam classification task, the hybrid models consistently outperform the classical counterpart, achieving an accuracy increase of 15 percentage points (from 66% to 81%) on the spam class, demonstrating enhanced generalization. These findings highlight the feasibility of employing QML for natural language processing and point toward the potential advantages of hybrid models as quantum hardware continues to advance.

AI.tldr: Hybrid quantum-classical neural networks achieve comparable accuracy to classical models for COVID-19 tweet sentiment analysis, and demonstrate superior transfer learning performance (+15% accuracy) for SMS spam classification.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether quantum machine learning, particularly hybrid quantum-classical neural networks, can be effectively applied to natural language processing tasks like sentiment analysis, leveraging the expressive power of quantum circuits for complex learning problems.
AI.method: Researchers used COVID-19 tweet dataset with TF-IDF vectorization, comparing classical feedforward neural networks against hybrid architectures incorporating parameterized quantum circuits. They evaluated both sentiment analysis and transfer learning to SMS spam classification.
AI.result: Hybrid models achieved comparable accuracy to classical baselines for sentiment analysis, with distinct learning dynamics suggesting richer representational capacity. In transfer learning to SMS spam classification, hybrid models outperformed classical counterparts by 15 percentage points (66% to 81% accuracy on spam class).
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of quantum machine learning for NLP tasks and suggests hybrid quantum-classical models offer enhanced generalization capabilities, pointing toward potential advantages as quantum hardware advances.

title: ContextNest: Verifiable Context Governance for Autonomous AI Agent
summary:
Autonomous AI agents increasingly depend on external knowledge stores, yet most retrieval pipelines provide relevance without durable guarantees of provenance, version identity, integrity, traceability, or point-in-time reconstruction. We formalize this as context governance and present ContextNext, an open specification and reference implementation for governed AI-consumable knowledge vaults. ContextNext does not replace Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG); it supplies the governance layer beneath retrieval, determining which artifacts are approved, current, attributable, and integrity-verified before retrieval systems operate over them.
  The specification combines typed Markdown documents with metadata, deterministic set-algebraic selectors, contextnest:// URI references, SHA-256 hash-chained version histories, graph-level checkpoints, source nodes for live data through the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and audit traces of agent context consumption. These mechanisms let organizations reconstruct which knowledge versions informed an agent output and whether those versions were AI-eligible when consumed.
  We report first empirical results from two controlled experiments. In a stale-version attack isolating the governance-versus-retrieval failure mode, governed selection strictly Pareto-dominates BM25 sparse retrieval, with higher answer-quality pass rate (97% versus 93-90%) at about one-third the input-token cost. In a retrieval-determinism experiment over a 1,060-document corpus, deterministic selectors and BM25 return stable document sets across repeated identical queries (Jaccard 1.0), while a dense+HNSW baseline is non-deterministic on 80% of queries (mean Jaccard 0.611, worst case 0.210). These results suggest that context governance addresses failure modes retrieval quality alone is not designed to resolve. We release a core engine, CLI, and MCP server under open licenses.

AI.tldr: ContextNext is an open specification and reference implementation for governed AI knowledge vaults that adds provenance, version control, integrity verification, and audit capabilities to AI agent context, addressing governance failures that standard retrieval systems don't solve.
AI.motivation: Current AI agent retrieval pipelines focus on relevance but lack durable guarantees for provenance, version identity, integrity, traceability, and point-in-time reconstruction. This creates governance gaps where organizations can't verify what knowledge versions informed AI outputs or whether those versions were properly authorized.
AI.method: ContextNext combines typed Markdown documents with metadata, deterministic set-algebraic selectors, contextnest:// URI references, SHA-256 hash-chained version histories, graph-level checkpoints, MCP source nodes for live data, and audit trails. It acts as a governance layer beneath retrieval systems to control which artifacts are approved, current, attributable, and integrity-verified.
AI.result: In experiments: 1) In stale-version attack scenarios, governed selection Pareto-dominates BM25 sparse retrieval with higher answer-quality pass rate (97% vs 93-90%) at ~1/3 the input-token cost. 2) In retrieval-determinism tests over 1,060 documents, deterministic selectors and BM25 returned stable document sets (Jaccard 1.0), while dense+HNSW baseline was non-deterministic on 80% of queries (mean Jaccard 0.611).
AI.conclusion: Context governance addresses critical failure modes that retrieval quality alone cannot resolve, providing organizations with audit trails, version control, and integrity verification for AI-consumable knowledge, enabling reconstruction of which knowledge versions informed specific agent outputs.

title: A More Accurate Algorithm Comparison through A/B Testing using Offline Evaluation Methods
summary: A/B testing is the gold standard for selecting the better algorithm in online services. While offline evaluation has attracted attention as a safer alternative due to the high experimental costs and the potential risk of degrading user experience and revenue in A/B testing, it is widely recognized that the estimation accuracy of offline evaluation is substantially lower. As a result, final selection decisions are typically made through A/B testing. Contrary to this conventional view, we reveal a counterintuitive phenomenon in which A/B testing can produce a higher algorithm selection error rate than offline evaluation. This occurs because the sample mean estimator used in A/B testing does not induce positive correlation, which is crucial for reducing critical selection errors, namely underestimating the truly superior algorithm and overestimating the truly inferior one. In contrast, offline evaluation methods unintentionally generate this beneficial correlation by relying on shared offline data when estimating and comparing the performance of multiple algorithms. Building on this insight, we propose an estimator that intentionally induces positive correlation to improve algorithm selection in A/B testing. The key idea is to introduce a hypothetical middle algorithm and to estimate the performance difference between algorithms A, M, and B in a stepwise manner using shared data at each step. This approach enables the application of offline evaluation techniques in each step, thereby inducing positive correlation and reducing critical selection errors. Furthermore, we derive the optimal middle algorithm regarding the resulting variance and analyze its advantages over existing methods through bias-variance analysis. Experiments on real-world data demonstrate that our estimator achieves the same selection error rate as existing approaches while using only one half of the A/B testing data.

AI.tldr: A/B testing can produce higher algorithm selection errors than offline evaluation due to lack of positive correlation in sample mean estimators. The paper proposes a novel estimator that induces positive correlation by introducing a middle algorithm and using shared data in stepwise comparisons, reducing critical selection errors.
AI.motivation: While A/B testing is the gold standard for algorithm selection, it has high experimental costs and risks to user experience/revenue. Offline evaluation is safer but has lower accuracy, leading to final decisions still relying on A/B testing. The paper challenges conventional wisdom by showing A/B testing can actually have higher error rates than offline evaluation for algorithm selection.
AI.method: The authors propose an estimator that intentionally induces positive correlation to reduce critical selection errors in A/B testing. The key innovation is introducing a hypothetical middle algorithm and estimating performance differences between algorithms A, M, and B in a stepwise manner using shared data at each step. This allows applying offline evaluation techniques in each step to induce positive correlation. They also derive the optimal middle algorithm regarding variance and analyze advantages through bias-variance analysis.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world data demonstrate that the proposed estimator achieves the same selection error rate as existing approaches while using only half of the A/B testing data, showing significant efficiency improvements.
AI.conclusion: The paper reveals a counterintuitive phenomenon where A/B testing can produce higher selection errors than offline evaluation due to lack of positive correlation. By designing estimators that intentionally induce positive correlation through shared data and stepwise comparisons, algorithm selection in A/B testing can be significantly improved, reducing both data requirements and critical selection errors.

title: Enhancing Fitness Intelligence through Domain-Specific LLM Post-Training
comment: 8 pages, 6 tables, 2 figures. Accepted by the 12th International Conference on Big Data Computing and Communications (BigCom 2026)
summary: Scientific Fitness Coaching (SFC) is typically delivered by human professionals, making it costly and inaccessible to many. While recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) show considerable promise for more inclusive fitness coaching, directly deploying prevailing general-purpose LLMs in SFC reveals critical limitations. These models often lack sufficient domain-specific knowledge integration, leading to weak performance on complex SFC scenarios. In this paper, we introduce FitOne, a series of fitness LLMs (with 8B and 32B parameters) designed to improve reliability and domain specialization for SFC applications. Built upon the Qwen3 foundation models, FitOne is developed through a three-stage post-training pipeline consisting of continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, using large-scale, high-quality datasets derived from rigorous knowledge engineering. We conduct comprehensive evaluations of FitOne on professional fitness certification exams, including ACSM-EP and NSCA-CSCS, as well as general capabilities such as knowledge reasoning and instruction following. Experimental results show that, while retaining strong general capabilities, FitOne-8B/32B achieves average improvements of up to 10.09%/9.29% and 12.73%/7.01% on the ACSM-EP and NSCA-CSCS exams, respectively, compared with the Qwen3 base models. Furthermore, in-depth ablation studies confirm the necessity of each training stage, highlighting the pipeline's effectiveness in balancing domain expertise enhancement with general ability retention. We believe this research advances LLM systems toward more reliable fitness intelligence and will inspire future research on developing domain-specific LLMs.

AI.tldr: FitOne is a series of fitness-focused Large Language Models (8B/32B parameters) that outperforms general-purpose LLMs on professional fitness certification exams through specialized training, while maintaining strong general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Scientific Fitness Coaching (SFC) is costly and inaccessible due to human professionals. While LLMs show promise for inclusive fitness coaching, current general-purpose models lack sufficient domain knowledge and perform poorly on complex SFC scenarios.
AI.method: Built upon Qwen3 foundation models, FitOne uses a three-stage post-training pipeline: 1) continual pre-training, 2) supervised fine-tuning, and 3) reinforcement learning, using large-scale, high-quality datasets from rigorous knowledge engineering.
AI.result: FitOne-8B/32B achieves average improvements of up to 10.09%/9.29% on ACSM-EP and 12.73%/7.01% on NSCA-CSCS exams compared to base Qwen3 models, while retaining strong general capabilities. Ablation studies confirm the necessity of each training stage.
AI.conclusion: The research advances LLM systems toward reliable fitness intelligence and provides a framework for developing domain-specific LLMs, demonstrating effective balance between domain expertise enhancement and general ability retention.

title: Probabilistic Low-Voltage Peak Load Forecasting with Time Series Foundation Models Evaluated on Application-Oriented Metrics
comment: A poster abstract of this publication will be available at the 15th DACH+ Conference on Energy Informatics (2026 in Linz, Austria)
summary: Low-voltage load forecasting is an important component in current and future energy systems with a high degree of electrification and decentralized generation. However, current forecasting methods require significant manual effort, often lack uncertainty estimation and proper peak prediction, and they are often not adequately evaluated in terms of grid requirements. In the present study, we provide an extensive evaluation of short-term net load forecasts of 200 real-world low-voltage feeders with a focus on the rapidly evolving time series foundation models. Our study compares Chronos-Bolt, Chronos-2 and TabPFN-TS to six baseline models and demonstrates superior performance, in particular for Chronos-2. An ablation study, in which weather covariates are omitted, shows that time series foundation models adapt to increased uncertainty, despite the importance of weather information. A novel application-oriented metric links the model's forecasting capabilities in peak prediction to the trade-off in grid asset planning and operation between cost reduction and minimizing the risk of failure.

AI.tldr: Time series foundation models (especially Chronos-2) outperform traditional methods for low-voltage load forecasting, handling uncertainty better and improving peak prediction critical for grid planning.
AI.motivation: Low-voltage load forecasting is crucial for modern energy systems with high electrification and decentralized generation, but current methods have limitations: they require significant manual effort, lack proper uncertainty estimation, have poor peak prediction capabilities, and aren't adequately evaluated against grid requirements.
AI.method: Extensive evaluation of short-term net load forecasts on 200 real-world low-voltage feeders, comparing three time series foundation models (Chronos-Bolt, Chronos-2, and TabPFN-TS) against six baseline models. Includes an ablation study omitting weather covariates and develops a novel application-oriented metric linking peak prediction to grid asset planning trade-offs.
AI.result: Time series foundation models demonstrate superior performance, with Chronos-2 performing best. The ablation study shows these models adapt to increased uncertainty even when weather information is omitted. The novel metric effectively connects forecasting capabilities to practical grid planning considerations (cost reduction vs. risk of failure).
AI.conclusion: Time series foundation models represent a significant advancement for low-voltage load forecasting, offering improved accuracy, better uncertainty handling, and superior peak prediction capabilities that directly address grid planning needs. Chronos-2 emerges as particularly effective for this application.

title: Coding-agents can replicate scientific machine learning papers
summary: Scientific machine learning papers typically make computational claims, e.g., that the relative mean square error is less than 5% or that the 95% predictive credible interval covers the test data. A coding agent can be prompted to replicate those claims from paper materials alone, but the prompt does not by itself reliably preserve progress or check whether generated evidence supports the paper's claims. We introduce Paper-replication, a workflow that makes each selected paper claim a target with recorded evidence, and implement it as a coding-agent skill. The workflow makes the agent record those targets, reconstruct the paper's method, run computational experiments, link generated outputs to provenance and comparisons with the paper's claims, record where matched evidence appears in the replication report, and pass validation checks before completion. We evaluate Paper-replication on twelve independent runs across four scientific machine learning papers. All twelve workspaces pass the completion gate, and all 158 recorded targets are matched with report coverage. Even in this completed workspace state, repeated runs differ in how papers are divided into targets, in numerical fidelity to the source papers, in elapsed replication time, in the number of intermediate executions replaced before final evidence is accepted, and in the rules used to accept evidence. Paper-replication makes completion depend on workspace evidence and validation checks rather than on the agent's final message.

AI.tldr: Paper-replication is a coding-agent workflow that systematically replicates computational claims from scientific machine learning papers, making each claim a tracked target with recorded evidence and validation checks.
AI.motivation: Scientific ML papers make computational claims (e.g., error rates, credible intervals) that need reliable replication. Current approaches using coding agents alone don't preserve progress or validate whether generated evidence actually supports the paper's claims.
AI.method: A workflow that makes each paper claim a target with recorded evidence, implemented as a coding-agent skill. The agent records targets, reconstructs methods, runs computational experiments, links outputs to provenance and comparisons, records evidence locations in replication reports, and passes validation checks.
AI.result: Evaluated on 12 runs across 4 scientific ML papers: all 12 workspaces passed completion gates, all 158 recorded targets matched with report coverage. Variations observed in target division, numerical fidelity, replication time, execution replacements, and evidence acceptance rules.
AI.conclusion: Paper-replication makes completion depend on workspace evidence and validation checks rather than agent messages, providing a systematic approach to claim replication with recorded evidence and automated validation.

title: Do Newer Lightweight CNNs Perform Better Under Resource Constraints? A Controlled Multigenerational Study of Architecture, Initialization, Training Budget, and Efficiency
summary: Newer lightweight convolutional neural networks are often presented as improving predictive performance and deployment efficiency, but such claims require controlled evaluation. This study compares nine lightweight CNN model packages across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet under a shared downstream protocol. We report top-1 accuracy, macro F1, top-5 accuracy, parameter count, FP32 storage, GMACs, batch-size-1 latency on an NVIDIA L4 and AMD Ryzen 5 5500U CPU, peak PyTorch CUDA allocated tensor memory, and point estimate Pareto frontiers. EfficientNetV2-S achieves the highest observed top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 at 97.57% and 86.98%, while RepViT-M1.0 leads Tiny ImageNet at 79.87%. EfficientNet-B0 remains within 0.22, 0.85, and 1.79 percentage points of the best result on the three datasets while using approximately 79% fewer parameters and 86% fewer GMACs than EfficientNetV2-S. It also appears on every evaluated accuracy and resource Pareto frontier, making it the most consistently competitive intermediate-budget option. MobileNetV3-Small has the lowest GMAC count, is the fastest model under both CPU thread settings, and records higher observed accuracy than MobileNetV4-Conv-S on all three datasets. Under random initialization, it leads MobileNetV4-Conv-S by 2.55, 1.76, and 0.99 points, with paired test-set intervals excluding zero for the fixed trained models. EfficientNet-B0 remains 3.29, 10.10, and 17.54 points below its pretrained counterpart after 100 epochs of scratch training, despite requiring about five times the recorded training time. SqueezeNet1.1 has the fewest parameters and lowest peak CUDA allocation, but substantially weaker accuracy. Latency rankings differ sharply between the L4 and CPU environments, showing that GMACs alone do not predict measured inference performance. Overall, newer designs provide selective rather than universal gains

AI.tldr: Comprehensive evaluation of nine lightweight CNN models across three datasets shows newer models provide selective rather than universal gains, with EfficientNet-B0 emerging as the most consistently competitive intermediate-budget option.
AI.motivation: While newer lightweight CNNs claim improved performance and efficiency, these claims require controlled evaluation under shared protocols to provide meaningful comparisons and guide model selection.
AI.method: Systematically compared nine lightweight CNN model packages (EfficientNetV2-S, RepViT-M1.0, EfficientNet-B0, MobileNetV3-Small, MobileNetV4-Conv-S, SqueezeNet1.1, etc.) across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet using standardized training and evaluation protocols. Metrics included accuracy (top-1, top-5, F1), efficiency (parameters, GMACs, storage), latency (NVIDIA L4 GPU, AMD CPU), memory usage, and Pareto frontier analysis.
AI.result: EfficientNetV2-S achieved highest top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-10 (97.57%) and CIFAR-100 (86.98%), while RepViT-M1.0 led on Tiny ImageNet (79.87%). EfficientNet-B0 remained competitive within 0.22-1.79 percentage points of best results while using 79% fewer parameters and 86% fewer GMACs than EfficientNetV2-S, appearing on all accuracy-resource Pareto frontiers. MobileNetV3-Small had lowest GMACs, fastest inference, and outperformed MobileNetV4-Conv-S on all datasets. EfficientNet-B0 showed significant accuracy drops (3.29-17.54 points) from pretrained versions despite longer training. Latency rankings differed substantially between GPU and CPU, showing GMACs alone don't predict inference performance.
AI.conclusion: Newer lightweight CNN designs provide selective rather than universal gains across accuracy, efficiency, and deployment scenarios. EfficientNet-B0 emerges as the most consistently competitive option for intermediate budgets. Model selection should consider specific deployment constraints as GMACs don't reliably predict actual inference latency, and pretraining remains crucial for achieving reported accuracy levels.

title: A$^{2}$utoLPBench: An Auto-Generated, Agent-Friendly LP Benchmark via Inverse-KKT Construction
summary: Most LP-from-text benchmarks are static datasets of word problems written and labeled by hand. Once such a dataset is released, its size is fixed, its difficulty is fixed, and every problem can leak into the training data of future LLMs. We present \textbf{A$^{2}$utoLPBench}, a benchmark for testing LLM-driven agents on linear programming problems written in plain text. We first pick a feasible point and dual, then write down a problem for which that point is optimal and the objective value is known. The answer is known by construction, with no solver call and no human annotator. The evaluation environment bundles a reference solver-critic baseline and a Docker image whose usage instructions are written for an LLM-driven agent to read. With these in place, any agent can run the benchmark and get a calibrated score with one command. Because the benchmark is a generator rather than a fixed dataset, it has properties no fixed dataset can match: an unlimited supply of fresh problems, a difficulty knob set by $(n,m)$, ground-truth answers correct by construction, low LLM-side cost per problem relative to human authoring, repeatable scores across independent batches, and resistance to training-data leakage when fresh post-cutoff seed ranges are used.

AI.tldr: A²utoLPBench: An auto-generated benchmark for testing LLM agents on linear programming problems with unlimited fresh problems, difficulty control, and built-in ground truth.
AI.motivation: Current LP-from-text benchmarks are static datasets with fixed size and difficulty, prone to training data leakage and limited scalability. Need for a benchmark that can generate unlimited fresh problems with known answers.
AI.method: First pick a feasible point and dual, then construct a linear programming problem where that point is optimal with known objective value. The benchmark includes a reference solver-critic baseline and Docker environment designed for LLM agents.
AI.result: Creates a benchmark generator with properties no fixed dataset can match: unlimited fresh problems, difficulty control via (n,m) parameters, ground-truth answers correct by construction, low cost per problem, repeatable scores, and resistance to training-data leakage.
AI.conclusion: A²utoLPBench provides a scalable, leakage-resistant benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on linear programming problems, enabling calibrated scoring with one command while overcoming limitations of static datasets.

title: Liquid Latent State Dynamics for Interpretable Turbofan Degradation Modeling
summary: Multivariate time-series models for prognostics are often evaluated by point prediction accuracy, yet their internal states rarely expose a coherent degradation process. We study liquid neural networks as latent dynamics models for aircraft engine health monitoring on the C-MAPSS benchmark. The proposed model encodes a history window into a latent state, evolves that state with a liquid transition model, and decodes future sensor observations. To separate health evolution from operating-condition variation, the latent state is factorized into degradation and condition components. Remaining useful life, monotonic risk, and latent-consistency losses supervise the degradation component, while condition prediction and decorrelation losses discourage operating-condition leakage. Across FD001--FD004, the full disentangled model improves overall sensor forecasting RMSE from 0.2438 for a GRU baseline to 0.2266, with the largest gains on the multi-condition subsets FD002 and FD004. The learned degradation state also forms a clearer temporal degradation axis, reaching an average state-speed Spearman correlation of 0.5960. Direct remaining-useful-life regression remains stronger for the GRU baseline, indicating that the proposed representation is currently more effective as an interpretable world model for degradation dynamics than as a calibrated lifetime regressor. These results suggest that liquid latent dynamics can bridge predictive maintenance forecasting and inspectable health-state modeling.

AI.tldr: Liquid neural networks with factorized latent states improve multivariate time-series forecasting for aircraft engine health monitoring, creating interpretable degradation dynamics while separating operating-condition variations.
AI.motivation: Current multivariate time-series models for prognostics focus on point prediction accuracy but lack coherent degradation process representation in their internal states, limiting interpretability and separation of health degradation from operating-condition variations.
AI.method: Proposes liquid neural networks as latent dynamics models with factorized latent states: encodes history window into latent state, evolves it with liquid transition model, decodes future observations. Latent state is factorized into degradation and condition components. Uses RUL, monotonic risk, and latent-consistency losses for degradation supervision, and condition prediction/decorrelation losses to prevent operating-condition leakage.
AI.result: Improves sensor forecasting RMSE from 0.2438 (GRU baseline) to 0.2266 across FD001-FD004, with largest gains on multi-condition subsets FD002/FD004. Learned degradation state forms clearer temporal degradation axis (average Spearman correlation 0.5960). Direct RUL regression remains stronger for GRU baseline, indicating representation is more effective as interpretable world model than calibrated lifetime regressor.
AI.conclusion: Liquid latent dynamics can bridge predictive maintenance forecasting and inspectable health-state modeling by creating interpretable degradation representations while separating operating-condition variations, though further improvements needed for direct RUL regression performance.

title: A rubric-based controlled comparison of frontier language models on expert-authored clinical reasoning tasks
summary: Multiple-choice medical benchmarks are increasingly saturated, and recent rubric-based evaluations such as HealthBench have shown that open-ended clinical performance is far from solved - its "Hard" subset top score remains 32%. We present a small, deliberately difficult evaluation dataset of five clinician-authored clinical scenarios spanning four specialties (anaesthesia, internal/family medicine, emergency medicine, and obstetrics), each accompanied by an atomic, weighted, MECE rubric (25-62 criteria per task; 184 criteria total) authored from a clinician-drafted golden answer. We evaluate three frontier models: GPT 5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, and Gemini 3.1 Pro. Mean rubric pass rates were 0.47 (Claude), 0.39 (GPT), and 0.37 (Gemini). The central finding is an inversion of clinical priority: the highest-weighted (weight-5, critical) criteria passed at only 32.4-41.7%, while low-stakes weight-1 criteria passed at 80-90%. 56 of 108 critical (weight-5) criteria (52%) were satisfied by no model. Three LLM autoraters reproduced expert met/not-met labels on 92.8-94.7% of 552 graded criteria. We position this as a methods-and-preliminary-findings contribution: the five tasks demonstrate a scalable, defensible pipeline ready to develop into a large-scale benchmark.

AI.tldr: Researchers created a challenging clinical evaluation dataset with 5 difficult scenarios across 4 medical specialties using detailed, weighted rubrics, revealing that frontier LLMs perform poorly on critical criteria while excelling at low-stakes items.
AI.motivation: Medical benchmarks have become saturated with multiple-choice questions, but open-ended clinical reasoning remains unsolved. Current benchmarks like HealthBench show poor performance (32% on "Hard" subset), indicating a need for more rigorous evaluation of clinical reasoning in language models.
AI.method: Created a small but difficult evaluation dataset with 5 clinician-authored clinical scenarios across 4 medical specialties, each with detailed MECE (mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive) rubrics containing 25-62 weighted criteria per task. Evaluated three frontier models (GPT 5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3.1 Pro) against clinician-authored golden answers using atomic, weighted rubrics with 184 total criteria.
AI.result: Mean rubric pass rates: Claude 47%, GPT 39%, Gemini 37%. Key finding: critical weight-5 criteria had only 32.4-41.7% pass rates, while low-stakes weight-1 criteria achieved 80-90% pass rates. 52% of critical criteria (56 of 108) were not satisfied by any model. LLM autoraters reproduced expert judgments with 92.8-94.7% accuracy on 552 graded criteria.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates a significant gap in LLMs' clinical reasoning abilities, particularly for critical decision-making. It presents a scalable, defensible evaluation pipeline ready for expansion into a large-scale benchmark, highlighting the need for improved evaluation of clinical priorities in medical AI systems.

title: Fast and Accurate Anomaly Detection in Time Series
summary: Anomaly detection is a critical and evolving field in Machine Learning, with applications targeting different domains such as cybersecurity, finance, healthcare, manufacturing and IoT (Internet of Things) systems. Traditionally, anomaly detection algorithms have been designed using both supervised and unsupervised learning paradigms. The fundamental challenge in real-world anomaly detection scenarios is related to the inherent class imbalance (anomalies are typically rare) and, for supervised methods, to the scarcity of labelled anomalous data. Indeed, labelling is both expensive and time-consuming. Conversely unsupervised methods do not require labelling, but may suffer from high false positive rates when deployed in safety-critical applications. In this work we introduce a novel unsupervised algorithm for anomaly detection in time series based on the Haar discrete wavelet and a suitably designed $t$-test. We establish the theoretical foundation of the proposed $t$-test and, through extensive experimentation across 343 datasets, demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised and self-supervised benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel unsupervised algorithm for anomaly detection in time series that uses Haar discrete wavelet transforms and a specially designed t-test, achieving state-of-the-art performance across extensive experiments.
AI.motivation: Real-world anomaly detection faces challenges of class imbalance (anomalies are rare) and scarcity of labeled anomalous data for supervised methods. Unsupervised methods avoid labeling costs but often suffer from high false positive rates in safety-critical applications.
AI.method: The authors propose an unsupervised algorithm for time series anomaly detection based on the Haar discrete wavelet transform combined with a specially designed t-test. The paper establishes the theoretical foundation for this t-test approach.
AI.result: Through extensive experimentation across 343 datasets, the algorithm demonstrates superior performance compared to state-of-the-art unsupervised and self-supervised benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed wavelet-based t-test algorithm offers an effective solution for time series anomaly detection that addresses the limitations of both supervised and traditional unsupervised methods, particularly in scenarios with data imbalance and labeling challenges.

title: UA-ChatDev: Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Agent Collaboration for Reliable Software Development
summary: Software development is a complex task that demands cooperation among agents with diverse roles. Large language models (LLMs) have enabled autonomous multi-agent software development frameworks that leverage role-based collaboration to automate requirements analysis, coding, testing, and refinement. However, existing approaches typically assume that intermediate agent outputs are equally reliable, leaving them vulnerable to hallucination propagation, where incorrect decisions generated in early development phases are transferred to downstream agents and negatively impact final software quality. To address this challenge, we propose UA-ChatDev, an uncertainty-aware multi-agent software development framework that integrates uncertainty quantification into agent interactions. It introduces a lightweight uncertainty estimation mechanism based on token-level log probabilities to assess the confidence of agent responses and employs phase-aware threshold calibration to selectively trigger retrieval-based verification when uncertainty exceeds acceptable levels. Extensive experiments on the SRDD benchmark demonstrate that UA-ChatDev consistently outperforms existing single-agent and multi-agent software development frameworks across completeness, executability, consistency, and overall quality metrics. Further ablation studies and communication analyses verify that uncertainty-aware interactions enhance code execution reliability.

AI.tldr: UA-ChatDev is an uncertainty-aware multi-agent software development framework that integrates uncertainty quantification into LLM-based agent interactions to mitigate hallucination propagation and improve software quality.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent software development frameworks assume equal reliability of intermediate agent outputs, making them vulnerable to hallucination propagation where early errors negatively impact final software quality.
AI.method: Proposes UA-ChatDev with lightweight uncertainty estimation based on token-level log probabilities to assess agent response confidence, plus phase-aware threshold calibration to trigger retrieval-based verification when uncertainty exceeds acceptable levels.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on SRDD benchmark show UA-ChatDev consistently outperforms existing single-agent and multi-agent frameworks across completeness, executability, consistency, and overall quality metrics. Ablation studies confirm uncertainty-aware interactions enhance code execution reliability.
AI.conclusion: Integrating uncertainty quantification into multi-agent software development frameworks effectively addresses hallucination propagation and improves software development quality through more reliable agent interactions.

title: A Memory Efficient Unified Algorithm for Online Learning of Linear Dynamical Systems
summary: Motivated by the challenge of stabilizing a general unknown linear dynamical system (LDS) from observations, we study the natural prerequisite of online prediction. Our goal is to achieve sublinear regret with a memory footprint that adapts to the intrinsic complexity of the dynamics rather than the full hidden -- state dimension. We focus on the practically central regime of systems with low instability complexity -- eigenvalues outside the real stable interval that do not decay rapidly, together with non-semisimple modes-potentially embedded in an otherwise stable real spectrum of much higher dimension; we write $k$ for this count. This regime is the primary setting in which stabilization is plausible: we show that many systems with high instability complexity cannot be stabilized without exponentially large controls. Thus, prediction is meaningful for stabilization precisely when the instability complexity is small. Within this regime, we introduce a unified online algorithm that handles every LDS (including non-diagonalizable systems with complex or exploding modes) with a learnable parameter count of $\widetilde{O}(k)$. Finally, we prove a lower bound showing that $k$ is a valid complexity measure: any filter-based predictor needs at least $k$ filters. Experiments corroborate our theory: on a high-dimensional system, our predictor sharply outperforms prior methods at an equal parameter budget.

AI.tldr: An online learning algorithm for predicting linear dynamical systems with low instability complexity, achieving sublinear regret with Õ(k) parameters where k is the count of problematic eigenvalues.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the challenge of stabilizing unknown linear dynamical systems from observations, focusing on the prerequisite problem of online prediction. The authors identify that prediction is meaningful for stabilization precisely when the system has low instability complexity (small k).
AI.method: The authors introduce a unified online algorithm that handles all linear dynamical systems (including non-diagonalizable systems with complex or exploding modes) with a learnable parameter count of Õ(k). They focus on systems with low instability complexity - eigenvalues outside the stable real interval that don't decay rapidly, together with non-semisimple modes.
AI.result: 1) Prove that many systems with high instability complexity cannot be stabilized without exponentially large controls. 2) Present an online algorithm achieving sublinear regret with parameter count adapting to intrinsic complexity k rather than full state dimension. 3) Prove a lower bound showing k is a valid complexity measure - any filter-based predictor needs at least k filters. 4) Experimental results show their predictor outperforms prior methods on high-dimensional systems at equal parameter budget.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes that instability complexity k is the fundamental measure for prediction in linear dynamical systems, provides efficient algorithms for low-k systems, and demonstrates practical superiority over existing methods, making prediction (and thus stabilization) feasible for systems with small instability complexity.

title: Criticality-Based Guard Rail Validation for AI Agent Decisions in Autonomous Telecom Networks
summary: The evolution toward fully autonomous telecommunications networks (Autonomous Network Levels 4-5) requires AI/ML agents to make real-time network decisions without human intervention. However, no standardized runtime mechanism exists to intercept and validate individual inference outputs before they trigger live network state changes, creating risks of erroneous autonomous decisions. This paper proposes the Guard Rail Validation (GRV) framework, a standardizable runtime architecture for intercepting and validating AI-driven decisions before execution. The framework evaluates decisions across multiple weighted dimensions -- including action scope, action type, service criticality, agent autonomy level, reversibility, and temporal behavioural patterns -- to determine a criticality level. Based on this level, graduated validation mechanisms are applied: execute-with-logging, bounds checking, independent agent validation, or multi-agent consensus. The framework additionally provides cross-agent conflict detection with criticality-weighted priority resolution and runtime conformance logging for regulatory compliance (e.g., EU AI Act Article 14). We present the architecture, algorithmic procedures, O-RAN deployment model, and evaluate threat coverage against known AI/ML attacks in telecommunications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Guard Rail Validation (GRV) framework, a standardized runtime architecture for intercepting and validating AI-driven decisions in autonomous telecom networks before they trigger network state changes.
AI.motivation: The evolution toward fully autonomous telecom networks (Levels 4-5) requires AI/ML agents to make real-time decisions without human intervention, but there's no standardized runtime mechanism to intercept and validate individual inference outputs before they trigger live network state changes, creating risks of erroneous autonomous decisions.
AI.method: Proposes GRV framework that evaluates AI-driven decisions across multiple weighted dimensions (action scope, action type, service criticality, agent autonomy level, reversibility, temporal patterns) to determine criticality levels. Based on criticality, applies graduated validation mechanisms: execute-with-logging, bounds checking, independent agent validation, or multi-agent consensus.
AI.result: The framework provides cross-agent conflict detection with criticality-weighted priority resolution and runtime conformance logging for regulatory compliance. Includes architecture, algorithmic procedures, O-RAN deployment model, and evaluation of threat coverage against known AI/ML attacks in telecommunications.
AI.conclusion: GRV framework addresses the critical gap in autonomous network evolution by providing a standardized runtime validation mechanism that can prevent erroneous AI decisions while supporting regulatory compliance and safe deployment of autonomous telecom networks.

title: Beyond the Performance Illusion: Structure-Aware Stratified Partitioning and Curriculum Distributionally Robust Optimization for Spatially Correlated Domains
summary: Performance evaluation in AI systems commonly assumes that random dataset splits produce independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) subsets. We show that this assumption often breaks down in spatiotemporally correlated domains such as aerial surveillance, precision agriculture, and medical imaging, leading to two systematic failures: data leakage, where correlated samples span training and validation splits and inflate performance estimates, and hidden stratification, where errors on minority subpopulations are obscured by aggregate metrics. To address these issues, we propose a unified evaluation and training framework for spatially correlated data. We introduce Structure-Aware Stratified Partitioning (SASP), which constructs validation splits that reduce spatiotemporal leakage while preserving meaningful class balance, and Curriculum Distributionally Robust Optimization (CDRO), a curriculum-based relaxation of distributionally robust training that stabilizes optimization under these stricter splits. Across multiple benchmarks, this combination yields consistently improved generalization, more reliable confidence calibration, and exposes failure modes that remain hidden under conventional random-split evaluation.

AI.tldr: A unified framework for evaluating and training AI systems on spatially correlated data that addresses data leakage and hidden stratification issues caused by conventional random splits.
AI.motivation: Conventional performance evaluation assumes i.i.d. subsets from random dataset splits, but this breaks down in spatiotemporally correlated domains (aerial surveillance, precision agriculture, medical imaging), leading to two systematic failures: data leakage (correlated samples span training/validation splits inflating performance estimates) and hidden stratification (minority subpopulation errors obscured by aggregate metrics).
AI.method: Two main components: 1) Structure-Aware Stratified Partitioning (SASP) that constructs validation splits reducing spatiotemporal leakage while preserving class balance, and 2) Curriculum Distributionally Robust Optimization (CDRO), a curriculum-based relaxation of distributionally robust training that stabilizes optimization under these stricter splits.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, the combination yields consistently improved generalization, more reliable confidence calibration, and exposes failure modes that remain hidden under conventional random-split evaluation.
AI.conclusion: A unified evaluation and training framework is needed for spatially correlated data to address systematic failures in conventional approaches, with the proposed methods providing practical solutions for more reliable AI system assessment.

title: Purified OPSD: On-Policy Self-Distillation Without Losing How to Think
summary: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) has emerged as a promising paradigm for improving LLM reasoning, where a privileged teacher with access to reference solutions provides token-level supervision on the student's own generated trajectories. However, we find that OPSD consistently fails on long chain-of-thought (long-CoT) reasoning models, yielding at best marginal gains while destabilizing the reflective reasoning capability these models depend on. Through a novel decomposition of the teacher's supervision signal, we identify the root cause: the teacher's supervision is dominated by a reference-induced component that drives rote memorization of reference-specific shortcuts, while the question-conditioned, inference-transferable component is ignored or actively opposed. Based on this diagnosis, we propose a two-step solution. First, we construct a reference-only teacher (the same model conditioned on the reference without the question) to isolate the non-transferable component of the supervision signal; the residual after subtracting this component captures the question-conditioned, inference-transferable correction. Second, we use pointwise mutual information (PMI) as the mechanism to transform this residual into a well-formed PMI target distribution that the student can directly distill from, filtering out the reference-induced shortcut. Experiments on four long-CoT models across two datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over both the base model and standard OPSD, while preserving the models' natural epistemic behavior throughout training.

AI.tldr: A novel two-step method improves on-policy self-distillation for long chain-of-thought reasoning by isolating question-conditioned supervision signals from reference-induced shortcuts using reference-only teachers and PMI transformation.
AI.motivation: Standard on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) fails for long chain-of-thought reasoning models, providing only marginal gains while destabilizing their reflective reasoning capabilities. The root cause is that teacher supervision gets dominated by reference-specific shortcuts rather than transferable reasoning patterns.
AI.method: 1) Construct a reference-only teacher (same model conditioned only on reference solutions without the question) to isolate non-transferable supervision signals. 2) Use the residual (after subtracting reference-only supervision) as a question-conditioned, inference-transferable correction, transformed via pointwise mutual information into a well-formed PMI target distribution for distillation.
AI.result: Experiments on four long-CoT models across two datasets show consistent improvements over both base models and standard OPSD, while preserving the models' natural epistemic behavior throughout training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decomposition and PMI-based transformation successfully addresses the limitations of OPSD for long chain-of-thought reasoning by filtering out reference-induced shortcuts and focusing on transferable, question-conditioned supervision signals.

title: SA-HGNN: Sample-Adaptive Hyperbolic Graph Neural Network for EEG-Based Depression Recognition
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been widely used to capture spatial functional connectivity patterns to improve electroencephalography (EEG)-based depression recognition performance. However, the functional connectivity of brain networks in patients with depression exhibits an inherent hierarchical structure, making it difficult to capture accurate connection patterns. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel model named Sample-Adaptive Hyperbolic Graph Neural Network (SA-HGNN), which aims to accurately extract the authentic hierarchical structure of depression-affected brain networks. Specifically, the proposed model comprises three core modules. First, a Sample-Adaptive Graph Construction module dynamically constructs personalized brain network topologies to capture more complex spatial relationships within the brain network. Second, hyperbolic graph convolution is employed to overcome the representation bottlenecks of Euclidean space, leveraging hyperbolic geometry to precisely capture latent hierarchical relationships within the brain network. Finally, an Attention Pooling module adaptively filters out highly redundant noise channels in EEG signals, effectively mitigating the interference of inherent noise on the authentic hierarchical topology. Extensive experiments on public EEG datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our method across resting-state and task-related paradigms, validating its robustness to noise and efficacy in capturing abnormal functional connectivity patterns in brain networks of patients with depression.

AI.tldr: A novel SA-HGNN model that uses sample-adaptive hyperbolic graph neural networks to capture hierarchical brain connectivity patterns for improved EEG-based depression recognition.
AI.motivation: Existing GNNs struggle to capture the inherent hierarchical structure of brain functional connectivity in depression patients, and traditional Euclidean approaches face representation bottlenecks for capturing these complex hierarchical patterns accurately.
AI.method: Proposes SA-HGNN with three core modules: 1) Sample-Adaptive Graph Construction for personalized brain network topologies, 2) Hyperbolic graph convolution to overcome Euclidean space limitations and capture hierarchical relationships, 3) Attention Pooling to filter out redundant noise channels in EEG signals.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public EEG datasets show superior performance across resting-state and task-related paradigms, demonstrating robustness to noise and effectiveness in capturing abnormal functional connectivity patterns in depression patients' brain networks.
AI.conclusion: The SA-HGNN model successfully addresses the challenges of capturing hierarchical brain connectivity in depression recognition, providing an effective solution for EEG-based depression diagnosis through personalized topology construction and hyperbolic geometry representation.

title: Copewell: A Multi-Agent Swarm Architecture for Equitable Mental Wellness Support
summary: Mental health disorders affect nearly one billion people globally, yet 75% of individuals in low- and middle-income countries receive no treatment due to workforce shortages, cost barriers, and stigma. Current AI-powered wellness solutions predominantly rely on single-mode conversational interfaces that suffer high abandonment rates and fail to provide measurable, immediate relief calibrated to users' dynamic emotional states. This paper presents Copewell, a novel multi-agent swarm system designed to expand access to mental wellness support through human-centered AI principles. Our architecture introduces three technical innovations: (1) a multi-source assessment framework integrating self-reported, physiological, and contextual data to mitigate algorithmic bias; (2) valence-arousal emotion mapping using Russell's Circumplex Model of Affect to route users to specialized AI agents; and (3) dual-mode intervention delivery combining conversational support with evidence-based sensory wellness protocols. We examine the sociotechnical design considerations underlying Copewell's development, including a privacy-first architecture, embedded ethical oversight through a dedicated Ethics Supervisor agent, and participatory design informed by mental health practitioners. Early practitioner engagement and beta deployment inform design decisions and identify directions for future empirical evaluation. This work contributes to responsible AI discourse by demonstrating how technical architecture can operationalize equity and safety principles from inception.

AI.tldr: Copewell is a multi-agent AI system that addresses mental health treatment gaps through human-centered design, combining multi-source assessment, emotion-based routing to specialized agents, and dual-mode interventions to provide scalable, accessible support.
AI.motivation: Mental health disorders affect 1 billion people globally, with 75% in low- and middle-income countries receiving no treatment due to workforce shortages, cost barriers, and stigma. Current AI wellness solutions have high abandonment rates, fail to provide measurable relief, and don't adapt to dynamic emotional states.
AI.method: The paper introduces Copewell with three technical innovations: 1) multi-source assessment framework integrating self-reported, physiological, and contextual data to reduce bias; 2) valence-arousal emotion mapping using Russell's Circumplex Model to route users to specialized AI agents; 3) dual-mode intervention delivery combining conversational support with evidence-based sensory protocols.
AI.result: The work presents early practitioner engagement and beta deployment that informed design decisions, including a privacy-first architecture, embedded ethical oversight via Ethics Supervisor agent, and participatory design with mental health practitioners. Directions for future empirical evaluation are identified.
AI.conclusion: Copewell demonstrates how technical architecture can operationalize equity and safety principles from inception, contributing to responsible AI discourse by showing how human-centered AI can expand access to mental wellness support while addressing sociotechnical considerations.

title: kNNGuard: Turning LLM Hidden Activations into a Training-Free Configurable Guardrail
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in domains requiring guardrails to detect unsafe, off-topic, or adversarial prompts. Existing guardrails predominately rely on fine-tuning to build classifiers, which often suffer from low generalization and high inference latency. We present kNNGuard, a training-free guardrail that utilizes the activation space of an off-the-shelf LLM. Given a small bank of 50 safe and unsafe prompts, kNNGuard extracts hidden activations and performs multi-layer kNN fusing activation-space and embedding-space scores for classification. Across six domains spanning topical and security prompts, kNNGuard achieves competitive or superior F1 compared to fine-tuned state-of-the-art guardrails while running 2.7x faster than the best comparable guardrail, and 10x faster than a fine-tuned safety classifier without gradient updates or fine-tuning. Domain adaptation requires only updating the labeled bank, which can be constructed in under 10 seconds and several orders of magnitude faster than established guardrails. We also analyze the impact of system prompts, layer selection, and integration into production LLM pipelines as a configurable, low-latency guardrail.

AI.tldr: kNNGuard is a training-free LLM guardrail that uses k-nearest neighbors on activation space from a small bank of safe/unsafe examples, achieving competitive performance while being 2.7-10x faster than fine-tuned methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM guardrails rely on fine-tuning which suffers from low generalization, high inference latency, and slow domain adaptation. There's a need for efficient, fast, training-free guardrails that can generalize well across domains.
AI.method: kNNGuard extracts hidden activations from an off-the-shelf LLM using only 50 labeled safe/unsafe prompts as reference. It performs multi-layer kNN classification by fusing activation-space and embedding-space scores, requiring no gradient updates or fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across six domains (topical and security), kNNGuard achieves competitive or superior F1 scores compared to fine-tuned state-of-the-art guardrails while running 2.7x faster than best comparable guardrail and 10x faster than fine-tuned safety classifiers. Domain adaptation takes under 10 seconds.
AI.conclusion: kNNGuard provides an effective, low-latency, configurable guardrail solution for LLMs that eliminates training overhead while maintaining competitive performance, making it suitable for production deployment with fast domain adaptation.

title: AgenticSTS: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Memory for a long-horizon LLM agent is a contract about what each future decision is allowed to see. The simplest contract appends past observations, tool calls, and reflections to every prompt, which makes prior context easy to access but also turns it into a jumbled mixture in which the effect of any single memory component is hard to isolate. We introduce and instrument an alternative bounded contract: every decision is made from a fresh user message assembled by typed retrieval, with no raw cross-decision transcript appended. The prompt thus stays bounded across runs of any length, and any single layer can be ablated in isolation. We instantiate the contract in Slay the Spire 2, a closed-rule stochastic deck-building game whose runs require hundreds of tactical and strategic decisions. A public online benchmark of frontier LLMs on the same game reports zero wins at the lowest difficulty across five configurations, and the developer-reported human win rate at the same difficulty is 16%; the task is hard but not saturated. Within our harness, a fixed-A0 ablation shows the largest observed difference when triggered strategic skills are enabled: the no-store baseline wins 3/10 games and adding the skill layer 6/10. At this sample size the comparison is directional rather than statistically decisive (Fisher exact p\approx0.37); a cross-backbone probe and public accumulating-context baselines are reported as operational comparisons rather than controlled tests of the contract variable itself. We release a reproducible testbed: 298 completed trajectories with condition tags, frozen memory/skill snapshots, prompt records, and analysis scripts -- an agent design and a validated, reusable methodology for studying how explicit memory layers shape long-horizon LLM-agent decisions.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce a bounded memory contract for LLM agents that uses typed retrieval instead of appending all past context, tested on Slay the Spire 2 where it shows directional improvements over baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM agent memory approaches append all past observations, tool calls, and reflections to every prompt, creating a jumbled mixture where individual memory components' effects are hard to isolate. This makes it difficult to study how specific memory layers influence agent decisions.
AI.method: Introduced a bounded memory contract where each decision is made from a fresh user message assembled by typed retrieval, with no raw cross-decision transcript appended. This keeps prompts bounded across runs and allows ablation of individual layers. The method was instantiated and tested in Slay the Spire 2, a complex deck-building game requiring hundreds of decisions.
AI.result: On the Slay the Spire 2 benchmark: frontier LLMs have zero wins at lowest difficulty, human win rate is 16%. In their harness, no-store baseline wins 3/10 games, adding skill layer wins 6/10 (p≈0.37, directional). Released reproducible testbed with 298 trajectories, memory snapshots, and analysis scripts.
AI.conclusion: The bounded memory contract with typed retrieval provides a more structured approach to LLM agent memory that enables better isolation and study of individual memory components. The method offers a validated, reusable methodology for analyzing how explicit memory layers shape long-horizon agent decisions.

title: Fourier Neural Operators for Rayleigh-Bénard Convection
summary: We propose an improved Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) for modeling two-dimensional Rayleigh-Bénard convection by predicting time increments instead of full solutions, achieving higher accuracy than a standard FNO baseline. The resulting model is compact (314k parameters, 1.26 MB) and fast (7 ms inference), while maintaining similar accuracy as demonstrated in previous benchmarks. We show that although FNOs generalize to finer meshes, accuracy remains limited by the resolution of the training data.

AI.tldr: Improved Fourier Neural Operator for 2D Rayleigh-Bénard convection uses time increment prediction instead of full solutions, achieving better accuracy than standard FNO with compact size and fast inference.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve modeling accuracy for Rayleigh-Bénard convection problems using neural operators, addressing limitations of standard Fourier Neural Operators in predicting full solutions.
AI.method: The method proposes an improved Fourier Neural Operator that predicts time increments rather than full solutions for 2D Rayleigh-Bénard convection. This approach results in a more compact model (314k parameters, 1.26 MB) with fast inference (7 ms).
AI.result: The improved FNO achieves higher accuracy than standard FNO baseline and maintains similar performance to previous benchmarks while being computationally efficient. However, it's shown that FNO generalization to finer meshes is limited by training data resolution.
AI.conclusion: Time increment prediction improves FNO performance for convection modeling, creating an efficient and accurate solution, but model accuracy remains constrained by the resolution of training data when applied to finer meshes.

title: A Hippocampus for Linear Attention: An Exact Memory for What the Recurrent State Forgets
summary: Linear-attention and state-space language models compress the prefix into a fixed-size recurrent state, yielding O(1) memory at the cost of a lossy exact memory: when many key--value associations compete, earlier facts are overwritten and needle recall degrades. Inspired by Complementary Learning Systems, we give linear attention a hippocampal complement. HOLA (Hippocampal Linear Attention) keeps the usual delta-rule state as a compressive memory and adds a bounded exact KV cache, forming a semiparametric test-time memory: the state models linearly compressible structure, while the cache stores associations that should not be forced through that state. The cache writes without a learned eviction module, keeping tokens with large beta * ||e||, the prediction residual actually committed to the state; a decoupled RMSNorm-gamma cache read then turns these exact KV pairs into sharp retrieval rather than soft averaging. At 340M parameters trained on 15B SlimPajama tokens, HOLA lowers Wikitext perplexity from 27.32 to 22.92 (-16.1%), below a full-attention Transformer++ (26.88), and improves LAMBADA perplexity from 30.95 to 30.26. It also achieves the best linear in-context retrieval and remains much more robust than GDN or a matched HOLA+recency cache on RULER needle-in-a-haystack recall out to 32k tokens (16x its training length).

AI.tldr: HOLA adds a hippocampal exact-memory cache to linear attention, improving language modeling performance while maintaining O(1) memory efficiency.
AI.motivation: Linear-attention and state-space models compress context into fixed-size states, causing lossy memory where earlier facts are overwritten when many associations compete, degrading needle recall performance.
AI.method: HOLA combines a compressive delta-rule state with a bounded exact KV cache. It writes to cache based on large prediction residuals (beta * ||e||) without learned eviction, and uses decoupled RMSNorm-gamma for sharp retrieval instead of soft averaging.
AI.result: At 340M parameters trained on 15B tokens, HOLA reduces Wikitext perplexity from 27.32 to 22.92 (-16.1%), outperforms full-attention Transformer++ (26.88), improves LAMBADA from 30.95 to 30.26, and shows superior linear in-context retrieval with robust needle-in-a-haystack recall up to 32k tokens.
AI.conclusion: HOLA's hippocampal complement to linear attention successfully addresses the memory degradation problem by separating linearly compressible structure modeling from exact association storage, achieving better performance than both linear models and full-attention Transformers while maintaining memory efficiency.

title: Ask the Right Comparison:Bias-Aware Bayesian Active Top-$k$ Ranking with LLM Judges
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as cheap, scalable judges that compare candidate outputs pairwise -- to rank responses, select models, or triage papers. Yet LLM judges are both noisy and systematically biased: they favor verbose or well-formatted answers and exhibit position effects, so simply aggregating their votes recovers a ranking of presentation, not of true quality. We study the practical goal of identifying the \topk{} items under a fixed comparison budget, and make two contributions. First, we cast judging as Bayesian inference over latent quality with explicit, judge-specific bias covariates (verbosity, position), regularized by a shrinkage prior so that the data decide which biases a given judge actually exhibits. Second, we introduce a \topk-aware active acquisition rule that chooses the next comparison to maximally reduce uncertainty about \topk{} \emph{membership}, rather than about the full ranking. On a controlled benchmark with known ground-truth quality, judged by sixteen real LLMs spanning open and proprietary families (Llama, Qwen, Phi-4, GPT-4o-mini/5.1/5.5, Gemini, DeepSeek, and Claude Haiku/Sonnet/Opus), naive aggregation plateaus at a wrong \topk{} on biased judges regardless of budget, while our bias-aware model recovers it; \topk-aware acquisition reaches this ceiling with far fewer comparisons than round-robin or a global-uncertainty (D-optimal) rule. Bias is real but heterogeneous and capability-dependent: cheap and mid-tier judges carry a strong verbosity bias that our model corrects (lifting recall from $\sim$$0.5$--$0.6$ to $0.84$--$1.0$), whereas the frontier judges we tested show little bias and already rank accurately, so bias-aware modeling changes little there.

AI.tldr: LLM judges are noisy and biased, favoring verbose/well-formatted answers. The paper proposes a Bayesian bias-aware model and top-k active acquisition to identify top items efficiently.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as scalable judges for pairwise comparisons (ranking responses, model selection, paper triage), but they exhibit systematic biases (verbosity preference, position effects) and noise. Simply aggregating their votes yields rankings based on presentation rather than true quality.
AI.method: 1) Bayesian inference over latent quality with explicit judge-specific bias covariates (verbosity, position), regularized by shrinkage priors that let data determine which biases each judge exhibits. 2) Top-k aware active acquisition rule that selects comparisons to maximally reduce uncertainty about top-k membership rather than full ranking.
AI.result: On a benchmark with known ground truth (16 real LLMs including Llama, GPTs, Gemini, Claude), naive aggregation plateaued at wrong top-k regardless of budget, while the bias-aware model recovered correct top-k. Top-k aware acquisition reached performance ceiling with far fewer comparisons than round-robin or D-optimal rules. Bias patterns were heterogeneous: cheap/mid-tier judges had strong verbosity bias (corrected by model, lifting recall from ~0.5-0.6 to 0.84-1.0), while frontier judges showed little bias.
AI.conclusion: LLM judge bias is real but heterogeneous. Bayesian bias-aware modeling with top-k active acquisition enables accurate identification of top items with fewer comparisons, especially important for biased judges. For frontier judges with minimal bias, the approach offers less benefit.

title: Grounded autonomous research: a fault-tolerant LLM pipeline from corpus to manuscript in frontier computational physics
comment: 39 pages, 5 figures. Accepted at the ICML 2026 AI for Science Workshop (https://openreview.net/forum?id=R5YXaPgUAx). Includes the pipeline-generated companion physics manuscript as an appendix. Data and scaffolding archive: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21126996
summary: Autonomous-research agents have demonstrated end-to-end LLM automation in machine-learning sandboxes where execution provides calibration. Frontier physical science differs categorically: physical reasoning underlies every methodology choice, toolchains are often underdocumented, and calibration must come from external literature anchors - which unscaffolded agents cite but do not confront, hallucinating plausible, unverifiable results from internal priors. We present a pipeline that runs end-to-end from a corpus of 11,083 recent condensed-matter physics arXiv papers to a publication-grade manuscript with three substantive physics findings (here on altermagnetic piezomagnetism): the agent autonomously conceives a research direction by mapping the corpus, calibrates methodology by reproducing published references, conducts novel first-principles computations, and writes the manuscript - grounded in literature throughout, across 47 fresh-context sessions in six phases sharing only on-disk state, with 2,162 literature-consultation events. Fault tolerance emerges from redundancy: fresh-context isolation, distributed grounding, and adversarial review catch what any single session misses; pre- and post-pilot stages are fully autonomous, and pilot requires bounded human intervention only at reproduction failures - operational knowledge curation, not scientific direction. Two paired failure modes - a pre-architecture baseline and a no-pilot ablation - isolate structurally enforced numerical confrontation at calibration checkpoints as the operative grounding mechanism. The primitives, characterized failure modes, and quantified intervention pattern lay a foundation for autonomous research in high-stakes scientific domains beyond computational physics.

AI.tldr: An autonomous research agent pipeline that goes from analyzing 11,083 physics papers to producing a publication-ready manuscript with novel physics findings, using literature-grounded methodology and fault tolerance mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous research agents work well in machine-learning sandboxes but fail in frontier physical science where physical reasoning, underdocumented toolchains, and external literature calibration are essential. Agents often hallucinate plausible but unverifiable results.
AI.method: A pipeline that: 1) autonomously conceives research directions by mapping a large physics corpus, 2) calibrates methodology by reproducing published references, 3) conducts novel first-principles computations, 4) writes manuscripts grounded in literature. Uses fault tolerance through fresh-context isolation, distributed grounding, adversarial review, and operates across 47 sessions with 2,162 literature consultations.
AI.result: Successfully produced a publication-grade manuscript with three substantive physics findings on altermagnetic piezomagnetism. The system demonstrated end-to-end autonomy from paper corpus to final manuscript with bounded human intervention only at reproduction failures (operational knowledge curation, not scientific direction).
AI.conclusion: The primitives, characterized failure modes, and quantified intervention patterns establish a foundation for autonomous research in high-stakes scientific domains beyond computational physics, with structurally enforced numerical confrontation at calibration checkpoints identified as the key grounding mechanism.

title: Predictive Conformal Slip Monitoring: An Empirical Evaluation of Rolling Split Conformal Prediction for Pre-Incident Traction Loss Detection
comment: 10 pages, 4 tables. codes and data available at:https://github.com/nearpot/predictive-conformal-slip-monitoring
summary: Conventional traction control architectures intervene only after the adhesion limit of a tire has already been breached. This paper investigates whether Rolling Split Conformal Prediction , monitoring the volatility of non-conformity residuals from a per-driver Random Forest model of expected slip behavior , can serve as a statistically grounded pre-incident warning signal, ahead of gross traction loss. Unlike an earlier internal draft of this work, the evaluation reported here corrects a confound in the slip proxy (vehicle speed is included as an explicit model feature, not left implicit in the target's denominator), uses every racing lap for each driver rather than only the fastest lap, and is scored against real, timestamped incident labels extracted from FIA Race Control Messages and track-limits lap deletions rather than narrated post-hoc. The result is negative: across 19 drivers and 55,563 test-phase telemetry samples, the rolling-volatility detector achieves a mean precision of essentially 0.0 and mean recall of 0.0 against 14 ground-truth incidents, while flagging on average 15.3% of all samples as anomalous , too high a false-alarm rate for any early-warning use. A static 95th-percentile threshold baseline performs no better in any way that would justify the added complexity of the conformal-volatility formulation. Residual autocorrelation diagnostics show the split-conformal exchangeability assumption is violated for every driver (Ljung-Box p < 0.001, n = 19/19), which is one plausible driver of the high false-alarm rate. We report this as a methodologically rigorous negative finding, diagnose its likely causes, and outline what a genuinely predictive version of this approach would require.

AI.tldr: Rolling Split Conformal Prediction failed as an early-warning system for traction loss in F1 telemetry, achieving near-zero precision and recall with high false alarm rates due to violated statistical assumptions.
AI.motivation: Conventional traction control intervenes after adhesion loss occurs, so researchers wanted to develop a statistically-grounded pre-incident warning system using conformal prediction to detect anomalies before gross traction loss.
AI.method: Used Rolling Split Conformal Prediction with per-driver Random Forest models to monitor volatility of non-conformity residuals from expected slip behavior. Evaluated on F1 telemetry data across 19 drivers with 55,563 test samples, using FIA Race Control Messages and track-limit lap deletions as ground truth incidents.
